- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <1u65q3l1v0rcriqpa82bvev2p8j9hiqcdq@4ax.com> <fnuse4$6np$1@reader2.panix.com> <p4d6q3hg7qlidtj2sc556ttnkqtcduq5ki@4ax.com> <fnve1k$sfc$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```
On Fri, 1 Feb 2008 15:32:04 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>In article <p4d6q3hg7qlidtj2sc556ttnkqtcduq5ki@4ax.com>,
>Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>reasonable to conclude that you are working at such a place now?

Web access is almost universal nowadays. But they need SOME way to lower contractors'
social status.  Denial of VPN access is a popular choice. Chicago  roads were terrible
this morning due to a snowstorm. Contractors had to drive in it while employees worked
from home. The parking lot was more than half empty.  There is no valid security reason
when SecureID is used. VPN ports don't cost anything in royalties. 

Other places restrict contractors to one entry door. The reason is because administrators
are too lazy to set them up in the security system, but it also serves as a social marker.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-02-02T13:16:23+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<60hr2oF1rb07oU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <1u65q3l1v0rcriqpa82bvev2p8j9hiqcdq@4ax.com> <fnuse4$6np$1@reader2.panix.com> <p4d6q3hg7qlidtj2sc556ttnkqtcduq5ki@4ax.com> <fnve1k$sfc$1@reader2.panix.com> <5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 1 Feb 2008 15:32:04 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> a social marker.

Contractors do have a choice, Robert.

If the corporate culture is such that contractors are despised, while being 
considered necessary, then moves should be made to address that culture. 
Maybe a round table with some Senior Managers, pointing out the fact that 
such an attitude, apart from being immoral, is simply costly and 
inefficient, and showing how contractors could bring much more to the table 
if they were treated properly. (Where is the management logic in 
demotivating an expensive resource?)

I have worked in places where contractors were treated exactly like 
permanent staff (except for pay) and they were usually happier shops (for 
everybody, not just the contractors...) Happy contractors are more likely to 
share expertise with the locals and even go the extra mile occasionally 
(driving home to check a manual is not "going the extra mile" in this sense 
:-)) If a company recognises that they need contractor expertise, then they 
should recognise that to maximise that expertise, it is wise to keep 
everybody happy.

There will always be the disgruntled permies who resent the freedom of 
contracting and the fact that someone doing "the same job" (whatever that 
means...) is being paid more, but won't drop the cuddle blanket of paid 
holidays, sick leave, insurance,  (and perceived - it isn't real, and hasn't 
been for decades- job security...) and go contracting themselves, and the 
best way I've found to deal with them is to talk frankly  (and without 
antagonism or hostility) and remind them of why they are NOT contracting. 
Once everybody knows where they stand it is easier to get on with the job, 
without gnawing resentment.

Corporate culture is a major (although largely unrecognised) factor in 
determining the success of IT projects. It is the reponsibility of managers 
to ensure this culture is positive and that applies to permanent staff as 
well as anybody else. If they can't treat their own staff properly, they 
certainly won't treat contractors properly.

If it is a hopeless hierachic structure governed by mean spirited control 
freaks who have no insight into motivating and treating people properly, and 
all attempts to address this have failed, then, contractors should vote with 
their feet. (So should permanent staff, but I recognise that it is much 
harder for them. If it wasn't, they probably wouldn't be permanent in the 
first place...)

Many years ago, I worked on such a site and I have never forgotten that 
experience. I was pretty inexperienced at contracting and believed I HAD to 
stay for the duration (six months); nowadays I would be out in four weeks 
:-).

(Aside to young contractors - read your contract and cross out the paragraph 
that says you cannot terminate, but they can give you one month. Make it the 
same for both sides. If the agency protest that it is a "standard contract" 
and "everybody signs it" tell them you won't. Do this AFTER you have been 
interviewed and accepted by the client company.)

I hope the U.K. company I am referring to have moved on, and have better 
managers now, but I have no intention of finding out...:-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-02-03T01:30:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fo35fq$ppi$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <fnve1k$sfc$1@reader2.panix.com> <5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com> <60hr2oF1rb07oU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <60hr2oF1rb07oU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>
>"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
>news:5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com...
>> On Fri, 1 Feb 2008 15:32:04 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

[snip]

>> Other places restrict contractors to one entry door. The reason is because 
>> administrators
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>if they were treated properly.

Mr Dashwood, corporate culture is, I've found, rather similar to just 
about any other kind of culture, bacterial included: an organism increases 
its chances for survival by doing what is reinforced as rewarding 
behavior.

For example... in a culture with an attitude of 'the nail that sticks out 
gets hammered down' ('being hammered down' is not, of course, considered a 
reward) then sticking out is a behavior which is not rewarded.  In a 
culture where there is the attitude of 'the nail that sticks out might be 
a useful thing on which to hang a coat or a picture' then sticking out is 
a behavior which might be rewarded.

So... if one's boss, and one's boss's boss, and one's boss's boss's boss 
consider that consultants are creatures to be despised, misused and blamed 
for failures - consultants don't need telephones, remember? - then it 
might just possibly be that if one is capable of reaching that *next* 
layer of management - a layer which might be in another building, another 
city, another country - one might discover, surprise of surprises, that 
each level of management is doing what it sees as behavior which will 
increase its chances of survival.

Consider where the rot of a fish begins, and all that.

>(Where is the management logic in 
>demotivating an expensive resource?)

Logic is a game played by a particular set of rules, Mr Dashwood; if a 
rule is 'making an outsider feel worse makes insiders feel better' then 
demotivating an outsider has the benefit of motivating insiders.  See how 
easy?

[snip]

>If a company recognises that they need contractor expertise, then they 
>should recognise that to maximise that expertise, it is wise to keep 
>everybody happy.

From another angle... if the need for consultants is seen as an inability 
on the part of the staff then the continued presence of the consultants is 
a constant reminder of this failure... what's easier to get rid of, the 
symptom or the disease?

[snip]

>Corporate culture is a major (although largely unrecognised) factor in 
>determining the success of IT projects.

Comparative anthropology is a hobby of mine, Mr Dashwood... and I'm never 
surprised that many managers seem unaware of the works of Boas, let alone 
later contributors to the field.

(a smattering of Durkheim might help, also)

DD
```

```
On Sun, 3 Feb 2008 01:30:34 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>So... if one's boss, and one's boss's boss, and one's boss's boss's boss 
>consider that consultants are creatures to be despised, misused and blamed 
>for failures - consultants don't need telephones, remember? 

Denial of telephones was early '90s. Denial of internet access was late '90s. What can we
deny them today?

Cubicles. 

I worked at Wal-Mart for six months as a systems programmer without a cubicle. My
workspace  was a 'TV tray' in a corrisor attached to the outside of a cubicle. It was
barely large enough to hold a CRT; we held keyboards on our laps. Later we got flat
screens, which left enough room (barely) for a keyboard on the little tray. There was no
room for manuals, notes or even a telephone.
```

In article <1agaq39hsq45qnfpu44pcrcpr6obskvttj@4ax.com>,
Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
>On Sun, 3 Feb 2008 01:30:34 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Cubicles. 

Plus ca change, plus c'est la selbe dreck... or something like that; I've 
worked in places where a couple of consultants would share a picnic-table 
or I'd be assigned to the printer-cube... the latter wasn't too bad, got 
to meet a lot of folks and the effect of the fumes could, at times, take 
me back to my Kollidj Daze.

>
>I worked at Wal-Mart for six months as a systems programmer without a
>cubicle.

At the end of interviews the prospective client will, frequently, toss off 
the mandatory 'So, is there anything you'd like to ask us?'... and my 
reply is 'Sure... I'd be interested in seeing where you'd have me 
sitting.'

I've refused jobs based on the reply... maybe not 'voting with my feet' 
but certainly with *some* anatomical consideration.

DD
```

wrote:

>

>this...

They don't tell you about working conditions during the interview because they don't know.
They hadn't thought that far ahead. Their attitude is 'We'll figure out something after
you arrive.'

Why would anyone work for Wal-Mart? Because they are the 800 pound gorilla. One expects to
find world-class technology -- the world's largest private database, the world's fastest
private network. You don't learn it's all bullshit, their technology is ten years old,
until you've moved to Bentonville. Then it's too late to change your mind.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-02-02T22:04:47-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hqcaq35soung5tqur6huf651jhriq2131j@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <1u65q3l1v0rcriqpa82bvev2p8j9hiqcdq@4ax.com> <fnuse4$6np$1@reader2.panix.com> <p4d6q3hg7qlidtj2sc556ttnkqtcduq5ki@4ax.com> <fnve1k$sfc$1@reader2.panix.com> <5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com> <60hr2oF1rb07oU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Sat, 2 Feb 2008 13:16:23 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>Many years ago, I worked on such a site and I have never forgotten that 
>experience. I was pretty inexperienced at contracting and believed I HAD to 
>stay for the duration (six months); nowadays I would be out in four weeks 
>:-).

As a practical matter, nothing bad would happen if you quit with same day notice.

>(Aside to young contractors - read your contract and cross out the paragraph 
>that says you cannot terminate, but they can give you one month. Make it the 
>same for both sides.

The standard contract in the US says you must give two weeks' notice, the company need
give no notice. It's called Employmet At Will. That means at the will of the employer. 

I worked for an Israel-based company that gave me one day's notice. I called the
contracting company, my employer, asking why I wasn't given more notice. They said "We
knew two weeks ago that today would be your last day. Amdocs asked us not to tell you, so
we didn't." In this case, I wasn't fired, they didn't 'walk me out'. It was just normal
business practice to give contractors no notice. 

Contracts are now written for either three or six months. The duration means absolutely
nothig. The company will terminate you whenever they decide to. It could be one month or
five years. During the interview, I try to find out whether there is a real project with a
defined delivery date. That's usually, but not always, a good indication of the duration.
In one case I signed onto an eight month IBM project that really was an eight month
effort. Two months in, some IBM big shot decided to get rid of all contractors to save
money. The project was still due in eight months, overworked employees had to do it alone.
Meanwhile I'd signed a six month apartment lease. IBM's response was tough luck, that's
your business. 

Typical employment contracts are 'unilateral', meaning all the benefit goes to one party.
Unilateral contracts are technically void under common law. I'd love to see a test case
establishing that for computer contractors.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<5braq3h5c0kvfp2819iqlqem29f7eqttel@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <1u65q3l1v0rcriqpa82bvev2p8j9hiqcdq@4ax.com> <fnuse4$6np$1@reader2.panix.com> <p4d6q3hg7qlidtj2sc556ttnkqtcduq5ki@4ax.com> <fnve1k$sfc$1@reader2.panix.com> <5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com> <60hr2oF1rb07oU1@mid.individual.net> <hqcaq35soung5tqur6huf651jhriq2131j@4ax.com>`

```
On Sat, 02 Feb 2008 22:04:47 -0600 Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:

:>The standard contract in the US says you must give two weeks' notice, the company need
:>give no notice. It's called Employmet At Will. That means at the will of the employer. 

What is this "standard contract"?

you negotiate such terms.

:>I worked for an Israel-based company that gave me one day's notice. I called the
:>contracting company, my employer, asking why I wasn't given more notice. They said "We
:>knew two weeks ago that today would be your last day. Amdocs asked us not to tell you, so
:>we didn't." In this case, I wasn't fired, they didn't 'walk me out'. It was just normal
:>business practice to give contractors no notice. 

Don't blame Amdocs for this (though there is much to blame them for) - they
fulfilled their obligation with your pimp. Improve your contract with your
borker.

:>Contracts are now written for either three or six months. The duration means absolutely
:>nothig. The company will terminate you whenever they decide to. It could be one month or
:>five years. During the interview, I try to find out whether there is a real project with a
:>defined delivery date. That's usually, but not always, a good indication of the duration.
:>In one case I signed onto an eight month IBM project that really was an eight month
:>effort. Two months in, some IBM big shot decided to get rid of all contractors to save
:>money. The project was still due in eight months, overworked employees had to do it alone.
:>Meanwhile I'd signed a six month apartment lease. IBM's response was tough luck, that's
:>your business. 

:>Typical employment contracts are 'unilateral', meaning all the benefit goes to one party.
:>Unilateral contracts are technically void under common law. I'd love to see a test case
:>establishing that for computer contractors. 

Consider negotiating (look it up in a dictionary and try to understand the
concept).

My contracts all have a notice period, on both sides.

On the other hand, it depends on what YOU bring to the table. If you are a
dime a dozen, you will tend to have limited negotiating power. If you bring
special needed skills, you will have much more power.
```

wrote:

>> dime a dozen, you will tend to have limited negotiating power. If you 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

I don't get to negotiate with the principal (employer). I'm talking to a pimp, who has
limited clout. Worse, I'm talking to a recruiter who is low in the pecking order within
the pimp organization. 

Employers don't want to negotiate. They want to pick up a phone, order five commodity
Cobol programmers, and find them on their doorstep tomorrow morning.

:>>> On the other hand, it depends on what YOU bring to the table. If you are a
:>>> dime a dozen, you will tend to have limited negotiating power. If you 
:>>> bring
:>>> special needed skills, you will have much more power.

:>>Absolutely. I agree with this 100%. Negotiation is a skill that all 
:>>contractors would do well to acquire (and it CAN be learned :-)).

:>I don't get to negotiate with the principal (employer). I'm talking to a pimp, who has

Then it seems, sadly, that you fit in the "dime a dozen" category.

:>Employers don't want to negotiate. They want to pick up a phone, order five commodity
:>Cobol programmers, and find them on their doorstep tomorrow morning. 

They can do that for commodities when the supply exceeds the demand.

Sort of like me and rental cars - the major all provide about the same
service, so I shop on price and have no problem at all using different brands.

You have to break yourself out of being a commodity. Get special skills that
give you a competitive advantage.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<60mtlbF1rkv49U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <1u65q3l1v0rcriqpa82bvev2p8j9hiqcdq@4ax.com> <fnuse4$6np$1@reader2.panix.com> <p4d6q3hg7qlidtj2sc556ttnkqtcduq5ki@4ax.com> <fnve1k$sfc$1@reader2.panix.com> <5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com> <60hr2oF1rb07oU1@mid.individual.net> <hqcaq35soung5tqur6huf651jhriq2131j@4ax.com> <5braq3h5c0kvfp2819iqlqem29f7eqttel@4ax.com> <60lsmcF1rqcjoU1@mid.individual.net> <1bjbq3pdicep1vu74hpul4qpr8fdkj2cqf@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:1bjbq3pdicep1vu74hpul4qpr8fdkj2cqf@4ax.com...
> On Mon, 4 Feb 2008 02:08:25 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cobol programmers, and find them on their doorstep tomorrow morning.

Having sat on the hiring side of that particular fence, I agree that it 
would be nice if that could happen. In reality, it doesn't. If you don't 
care about quality, you will just take whoever the pimps give you, but I 
don't know anyone who does that.

I certainly don't. And I've had more than one Agency struck off the Approved 
Vendors list for providing rubbish that was totally unsuited to the 
requirement (a waste of EVERYBODY's time), as well as demonstrating that 
they did not understand the requirement.

There have always been agencies who operate on the principle that of you 
throw enough shit at a wall, some of it will stick, and there have always 
been managers who neither know nor care whether they have shit, as long as 
they meet the head count requirement (makes you wonder why they are 
managers; usually they don't last too long...)

Any company that treats people as a commodity deserves whatever it gets.

Fortunately, in my experience I have only rarely encountered such places.

Certainly, the majority of the companies I have worked for are careful about 
who works on site, whether contract or permanent.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-02-04T10:32:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fo6pje$o9r$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <5braq3h5c0kvfp2819iqlqem29f7eqttel@4ax.com> <60lsmcF1rqcjoU1@mid.individual.net> <1bjbq3pdicep1vu74hpul4qpr8fdkj2cqf@4ax.com>`

```
In article <1bjbq3pdicep1vu74hpul4qpr8fdkj2cqf@4ax.com>,
Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
>On Mon, 4 Feb 2008 02:08:25 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
><dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>the pimp organization. 

Ahhhh, not even a pimp... but a pimple?  When I've found myself in 
situations of wanting to leave and being told 'sorry, but the contract 
says we can throw you out yesterday but if you leave and we think we can 
still make money you have to pay us'; my response was a quiet, measured 
'please... this is just not working out and I'm very unhappy; if you want 
I can come in, tomorrow, and speak to Mr/s Smith and say *exactly* what I 
*really think about the project, the management, the quality of the work 
environment and other stuff... calmly, quietly and in an understated 
manner... and I guarantee that you'll get a call within a half-hor 
demanding you get me off-site and then you'll *never* place a person here 
ever again.  Shall we be decent about the whole matter?'

>
>Employers don't want to negotiate. They want to pick up a phone, order
>five commodity
>Cobol programmers, and find them on their doorstep tomorrow morning. 

This is close to what I've found; my experience, for the greatest part, is 
that if I am not on-site within 48 hours of my papers being submitted then 
I don't have that particular job.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-02-04T23:15:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<apqfq3h55fjjstc17j0r4v5taobu0oqmr5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <5braq3h5c0kvfp2819iqlqem29f7eqttel@4ax.com> <60lsmcF1rqcjoU1@mid.individual.net> <1bjbq3pdicep1vu74hpul4qpr8fdkj2cqf@4ax.com> <fo6pje$o9r$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```
On Mon, 4 Feb 2008 10:32:14 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

> When I've found myself in 
>situations of wanting to leave and being told 'sorry, but the contract 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>ever again.  Shall we be decent about the whole matter?'

If they wanted to get rid of you, there'd be no courteous talk like that. They'd take your
badge, walk you out and refuse to say ANYthing or answer any questions. The legal policy
manual tells them to. Let them reap what they sow. 

I've left a place that way only once, after a week. It had ALL the red flags. Small
company, small town, employees all lifers, really bad code, exceptionally screwed up
software environment, weak management, little experience with contractors.

>>Employers don't want to negotiate. They want to pick up a phone, order
>>five commodity
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I don't have that particular job.

Thanks for confirming. The agency SAYS you don't have the job to pressure you. It's their
idea, not the client's. It usually takes more than 48 hours for the client to get a userid
and computer. Talking directly to the client usually buys more time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-02-05T10:40:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fo9efn$sa2$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <1bjbq3pdicep1vu74hpul4qpr8fdkj2cqf@4ax.com> <fo6pje$o9r$1@reader2.panix.com> <apqfq3h55fjjstc17j0r4v5taobu0oqmr5@4ax.com>`

```
In article <apqfq3h55fjjstc17j0r4v5taobu0oqmr5@4ax.com>,
Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
>On Mon, 4 Feb 2008 10:32:14 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>badge, walk you out and refuse to say ANYthing or answer any questions.

I've had folks terminate my contract prematurely, Mr Wagner, and there's 
never been any such actions on their part... they've called the pimp, the 
pimp called me and said 'it isn't working out' and I've shown up to clean 
out my desk, such is Life.

(Once, a week after termination, the Project Lead called me up to ask 
about something I'd been working on... not because it was of any benefit 
to the project, mind you, but just because... well, he was 'just asking', 
you know?  I said I'd be more than happy to discuss matters related to the 
job once a deposit had been paid against any future unexpected 
termination, he said he could not do that, I asked if he really expected 
me to work for free... and after a silence he repeated that he was 'just 
asking'.)

>The legal policy
>manual tells them to. Let them reap what they sow. 

That they chose to behave in a fashion, Mr Wagner, does not require me to 
do so... that's a Brooklyn Bridge defense.

[snip]

>>>Employers don't want to negotiate. They want to pick up a phone, order
>>>five commodity
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>pressure you.

Agencies do what they do, Mr Wagner, and I do what I do... and when I get 
a call from a headhunter saying 'They love you, they want you, they need 
you to call their own and they want you to start in three weeks' my 
response is 'That's nice... if I'm still available then I'll consider 
going on-site; if somone else wants to pay me for my time then guess whose 
desires get priority?'

*That* is when I've seen some rather... desperate shuffling.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-02-06T02:17:00+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<60r5tnF1rg0ieU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <5braq3h5c0kvfp2819iqlqem29f7eqttel@4ax.com> <60lsmcF1rqcjoU1@mid.individual.net> <1bjbq3pdicep1vu74hpul4qpr8fdkj2cqf@4ax.com> <fo6pje$o9r$1@reader2.panix.com> <apqfq3h55fjjstc17j0r4v5taobu0oqmr5@4ax.com>`

"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:apqfq3h55fjjstc17j0r4v5taobu0oqmr5@4ax.com...
> On Mon, 4 Feb 2008 10:32:14 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> manual tells them to. Let them reap what they sow.

I have never seen a company do this, although I can believe it could happen. 
However, I believe it would be used in extremis and only after all 
reasonable discussion had broken down. If you REALLY pissed them off, they'd 
do it gladly, otherwise they would have no reason to.

As for leaving prematurely, I believe Doc's argument above is a good one, 
and bears out what I said about no-one being able to MAKE you work in a 
place.

(However, I wouldn't take this approach lightly; if I had signed a contract, 
I would make every endeavour to honour it. If there were special 
circumstances (like family reasons requiring an unexpected relocation, for 
example...) where it simply isn't practical to work out the notice time, I'd 
consult with the company, explain the circumstances and ask to be released 
from the contract. If they said "No." I'd honour the contract and do the 30 
days - for me, if I give my word, it means something.)

> I've left a place that way only once, after a week. It had ALL the red 
> flags. Small
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It is unfortunate for all concerned. Nevertheless, I would not consider the 
factors you identified to be "red flags", and have worked successfully in a 
number of companies that fit most, if not all of these criteria.

>>>Employers don't want to negotiate. They want to pick up a phone, order
>>>five commodity
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> idea, not the client's.

If you have signed a contract, I can't see how you can NOT have the job.

> It usually takes more than 48 hours for the client to get a userid
> and computer. Talking directly to the client usually buys more time.

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <5braq3h5c0kvfp2819iqlqem29f7eqttel@4ax.com> <60lsmcF1rqcjoU1@mid.individual.net> <1bjbq3pdicep1vu74hpul4qpr8fdkj2cqf@4ax.com> <fo6pje$o9r$1@reader2.panix.com> <apqfq3h55fjjstc17j0r4v5taobu0oqmr5@4ax.com> <60r5tnF1rg0ieU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Wed, 6 Feb 2008 02:17:00 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
>news:apqfq3h55fjjstc17j0r4v5taobu0oqmr5@4ax.com...
>> On Mon, 4 Feb 2008 10:32:14 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>> If they wanted to get rid of you, there'd be no courteous talk like that. 
>> They'd take your
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I have never seen a company do this, although I can believe it could happen. 

I've seen it dozens of times. I've seen it happen to hundreds of people in one whack.

>However, I believe it would be used in extremis and only after all 
>reasonable discussion had broken down. If you REALLY pissed them off, they'd 
>do it gladly, otherwise they would have no reason to.

I've seen them fire people for trivial reasons, for instance an accent was hard to
understand. Groups of hundreds can be terminated by a stroke of the pen in headquarters. 

>As for leaving prematurely, I believe Doc's argument above is a good one, 
>and bears out what I said about no-one being able to MAKE you work in a 
>place.

Legally, a personal service contract cannot be enforced by specific performance. In plain
English, they can't make you work. That would be slavery. What they can do is charge you
for 'damages', which by coincidence happen to equal your final paychedk.

>(However, I wouldn't take this approach lightly; if I had signed a contract, 
>I would make every endeavour to honour it. If there were special 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>days - for me, if I give my word, it means something.)

I would do the same if I saw them honoring their committments. If I saw them writing six
month contracts ("it's a standard contract") and terminating people whenever they ran out
of work due to poor planning, I'd feel no obligation. The latter is now THE NORM in the
US.  When in Rome ...

>>>>Employers don't want to negotiate. They want to pick up a phone, order
>>>>five commodity
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>If you have signed a contract, I can't see how you can NOT have the job.

Employment At Will.
```

```
"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote:
>
> I've seen them fire people for trivial reasons, for instance an accent was hard to
> understand. Groups of hundreds can be terminated by a stroke of the pen in headquarters.

A friend once got a job as a beginning programmer at a regional grocery
chain. His first week they sent him off to school. When he got back, the
whole DP shop had been fired, except for him. When he asked why he
had not been fired, they said "Your salary isn't high enough." Apparently
someone in management decided to save money by eliminating all the
people making more than minimum salary. My friend says that headhunters
call that the "grocery bagger mentality" because grocery management treats
everyone view all employees as grocery baggers; cheap and expendable.
```

On Sat, 02 Feb 2008 00:17:27 GMT, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
>news:5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>you know are not able to work from home (when employees are)?

I talked to someone from a sister team and learned all THEIR contractors have VPN access.
Our consensus was it all depends on the manager's attitude toward contractors. It's a Soft
thing, not company policy.

Everyone in the company has Web access. All 3 contractors on my team do not have VPN
access. One of them made a major effort to get it.

I was amazed at how rapidly roads were cleaned. They were terrible at 9am, clear at noon.
I had no delay going home for lunch.

down to the office.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-02T08:06:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uN_oj.64600$vt2.4847@bignews8.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <fnve1k$sfc$1@reader2.panix.com> <5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com> <qEOoj.26935$m_6.14703@fe01.news.easynews.com> <fo1qvl$hhl$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:
> William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> down to the office.

I've been a full time consultant since 1980, and have worked both ways.
Some managers are apparently reassured by seeing a warm body at a
workstation. Perhaps they're not confident enough in their managerial
skills to trust in productivity and quality control assessments. It's
been since the early '90s that I was required to be onsite, other than
for occasional meetings. These days, even the onsite meetings are rare,
usually just for planning meetings, demos and software installation.
Communication is normally done by email or phone, with an occasional
conference call.

I've always had a relatively few, long term clients who knew me well. My
clients tend toward small business and local government, not Fortune 500
corporations. I only remember doing a few small one-shot projects for
large companies like BellSouth and Vulcan Materials. I don't know how
representative that is of typical consultants.

Considering the emphasis on "green" these days, you would think companies
that require bodily presence would consider that it's a lot "greener" to
move electrons than human bodies.
```

clients.  And the reason for the onsite visit was either to personally
attend start-up meetings and/or to have security put on my laptop.
Other than that, I always been supplied with log ons, secure ids,
whatever they use and access the mainframe and/or network via my own
broadband internet in my home.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-02-02T20:31:19-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9959q31d9im18p9sn6tcvvemt992oob1cq@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <p4d6q3hg7qlidtj2sc556ttnkqtcduq5ki@4ax.com> <fnve1k$sfc$1@reader2.panix.com> <5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com> <fo1qla$hul$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```
On Sat, 2 Feb 2008 13:19:38 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>In article <5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com>,
>Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>consultants/contractors/hired guns did not have on-the-job web access.'

That's why I said nowadays. Things change (am I allowed to say that in a Cobol forum?) I'd
be surprised if that shop didn't have Web access today.

Merrill Lynch in 1999 required manager approvall for Web access. Only a few long-term
employees had it. Then Top Management gave it to everyone in the company. Middle managers
hated the change, thought it would destroy productivity. Of course it didn't.

The terms consultant and contractor are not synonymous. A consultant is truly independent,
finds his own gigs (does not work for an agency), typically works part-time and has
multiple clients. A contractor is a temporary employee hired through an agency on the
Approved Vendor List, is an employee of that agency, works full-time for one client.

The term contractor is a misnomer because it implies guaranteed employment for the
duration of the contract. In fact, it is Employment At Will. The company can terminate the
'contract' any time, for any reason or no reason, with no notice. It happens so frequently
that we have a phrase 'walked out', meaning someone appears at your desk to ask for your
badge and escort you out the door. At one place, when a contractor refused to leave that
way, two strongarm security guards literally dragged him across the floor, kicking and
screaming.

Foreigners working on an H1 visa are especially vulnerable because loss of employment
means deportation. That's why they do the crappiest jobs without complaint and never voice
an opinion  .. in public. Privately, they say it's like slavery.
```

In article <9959q31d9im18p9sn6tcvvemt992oob1cq@4ax.com>,
Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
>On Sat, 2 Feb 2008 13:19:38 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>be surprised if that shop didn't have Web access today.

Would you be surprised it a shop did not have web access in 1987?  To say 
'I have worked' allows for yesterday and twenty years ago... or at least 
that's how using the phrase has worked previously.

>
>Merrill Lynch in 1999 required manager approvall for Web access. Only a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>hated the change, thought it would destroy productivity. Of course it didn't.

On the other hand... look at the havoc wrought by... regulating child 
labor!  Eight-hour days!  Coffee breaks!  Doomed, I tell you, we are 
doomed!

>
>The terms consultant and contractor are not synonymous. A consultant is
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>one client.

I find the terms are used different ways in different places, Mr Wagner... 
let's see... from 
<http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.basic.misc/msg/68b9ea81c18e98dd?dmode=source>

--begin quoted text:

Shucks, I'd blush were I able to remember how... but in some places it is
only a matter of terminology; 'consultants' were folks from the Big-8...
errrr, -6... errr, -3 firms who were leading the projects and everyone
else was a 'contractor'... which meant that a 25-yr-old freshly-minted
college grad who had just 'graduated' from Consulting-Company University -
an internal organisation that taught such kids to say 'Now that's a *good*
question' with an air of gravitas so as to give them time to scurry off to
the senior lead of the group and whimper 'Whaddo I do *now*, boss?' - were
called 'consultants' while a greybeard coder who said 'You know, back when
we had just moved up to twin, slaved 360s we solved that by...' was a
'contractor'.

(It was on such a site that I heard one of the locals say 'What does that
contractor think he is... a consultant?')

Me?  I'se jes' a COBOL-codin' fool and I call myself a
consultant/contractor/hired gun; I don't care what others call me as long
as their checks clear the bank.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-02-04T01:56:44+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<60ls0iF1qht3eU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <p4d6q3hg7qlidtj2sc556ttnkqtcduq5ki@4ax.com> <fnve1k$sfc$1@reader2.panix.com> <5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com> <fo1qla$hul$1@reader2.panix.com> <9959q31d9im18p9sn6tcvvemt992oob1cq@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:9959q31d9im18p9sn6tcvvemt992oob1cq@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 2 Feb 2008 13:19:38 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
> multiple clients.

Not necessarily. I have worked on that basis, but I've also worked 
independently through an agency, without being employed by the agency. It 
was termed "consultancy" although it was certainly governed by a contract.


> A contractor is a temporary employee hired through an agency on the
> Approved Vendor List, is an employee of that agency, works full-time for 
> one client.

Again, not necessarily. Not all contractors are from Agencies on the 
Approved Vendor list; I have employed people with special skills who were 
not on such a list. In fact, if the "Approved list " is a limiting factor in 
getting the right people then it gets ignored... I would have no problem if 
a "non-approved" Agency offered me someone I thought was excellent, in 
justifying my hiring decision to senior management. (And have done so...). 
Also, contractors who are full time employees of an Agency are very often 
not worth much. Their employers use their clients as training grounds for 
quite junior people and charge full rates for them. I have re-negotiated 
rates for such people where I was forced to use them on my project.

While your definitions may be OK for a specific discussion, there are 
exceptions and we shouldn't get too bogged down in the difference between 
contractors and consultants.
>
> The term contractor is a misnomer because it implies guaranteed employment 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'contract' any time, for any reason or no reason, with no notice.

Only if you sign a contract that states they can do that. I won't sign such 
a contract and haven't for 30 years. As I mentioned earlier, people starting 
to go free-lance should read the Agency contract and stike out what isn't 
fair. Agencies simply try it on. If you have passed the interview, the 
Agency is already counting the money you are ging to make for them. Do you 
really think they are going to go back to the drawing board (and have to 
explain to the Client that you won't be starting on Monday (after having 
agreed with the Client that you wuld accept the job), because you found the 
Agency's "standard contract" too restrictive and unfair), and take a hit on 
re-advertising, when they could be generating revenue by simply changing a 
few clauses in a contract that no-one is ever going to read anyway...?

I have two favourite clauses that I simply change in front of them:

1. Terms of notice. (1 month either way, except in cases of real bad 
behaviour where the Client can request immediate termination).

2. Intellectual property. They will tell you you must sign away the rights 
to anything you do for the Client, as it then becomes the Client's property. 
Don't do it. Strike it out or amend it so that whatever you do is theirs, 
but under a non-exclusive licence, whereby you retain IP rights to it, and 
you never relinquish the right to repeat solutions you have devised.

I have NEVER had an Agency refuse to change the contract (at the end of the 
day, they really don't care as long as you are generating cash for them), 
although on a couple of  occasions they had to consult with their Boss or 
the owner of the Agency.

The usual protest is: "It's a standard contract; EVERYBODY signs it!"

Smile sweetly and say: "Sorry. It is patently unfair and I cannot in all 
conscience sign it. If you are unable to amend it then I guess you are stuck 
with a contract that  may not be enforceable in Law anyway, as Courts do not 
look favourably on Employers or Intermediaries who try to abrogate the 
rights of employees, and which I at least, simply cannot sign."  Don't 
budge. If they say: "We cannot entertain making exceptions to it." Then get 
up and say:"Well, it's kind of a pity because it seemed like a good job and 
I know I could have made a contribution. However, it's obvious that we can't 
do business...:Bye."

I would bet money they'll be on the phone to you before you get 
home...around about the same time the Agent who is about to lose his 
commission realizes that the deal has not been closed, or the guy who owns 
the Agency hears that cash is walking out the door...

It is a VERY competitive business (I've seen all aspects of it) and if you 
have someone a Client liked in interview, you have to be crazy to let them 
walk.

> It happens so frequently

Only because people don't read contracts or have been beaten into a form of 
submission where they believe this is the only job they can get...

> that we have a phrase 'walked out', meaning someone appears at your desk 
> to ask for your
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> screaming.

I have never been in that situation. I can assure you that I would leave 
freely and head straight to my lawyer. They'd have a civil suit for breach 
of contract slapped on them the next day. (But only an incredibly stupid 
employer would even try it on, knowing that the contract stipulates 1 month 
on both sides...)
>
> Foreigners working on an H1 visa are especially vulnerable because loss of 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> an opinion  .. in public. Privately, they say it's like slavery.

Then maybe they have never experienced actual slavery. My understanding is 
that it is not something you can volunteer for...

It may be that contracting in the USA is "different" but I can't see how 
unfairness in a contract would be accepted by any thinking person, anywhere. 
A contract is simply an Agreement; it isn't Holy Writ. If you don't like the 
terms of the Agreement, change them. That's what negotiation is all about. 
If you're not prepared to read a contract and only sign it if you agree with 
it, then you deserve to be frog marched off the site. Not because of the 
contract terms, but because you were stupid...:-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-02-04T00:35:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5i6dq351iqmdd55eh6s0ekr4e7h3cg9du1@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <p4d6q3hg7qlidtj2sc556ttnkqtcduq5ki@4ax.com> <fnve1k$sfc$1@reader2.panix.com> <5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com> <fo1qla$hul$1@reader2.panix.com> <9959q31d9im18p9sn6tcvvemt992oob1cq@4ax.com> <60ls0iF1qht3eU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Mon, 4 Feb 2008 01:56:44 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>
>
>"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
>news:9959q31d9im18p9sn6tcvvemt992oob1cq@4ax.com...
actors and consultants.
>>
>> The term contractor is a misnomer because it implies guaranteed employment 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Only if you sign a contract that states they can do that. 

Employment At Will is the legal default in the US (except one state). The only excepetion
would be if the contract said otherwise. All regular non-contract employees work under
Employment At Will.

>I won't sign such a contract and haven't for 30 years. 

It's almost impossible to get a guarantee in he US.  I've only heard of one exception,
which specified 30 days notice. Labor unions have that in their contracts, but only 12% of
US workers belong to unions and none of them are computer people.

>As I mentioned earlier, people starting 
>to go free-lance should read the Agency contract and stike out what isn't 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>behaviour where the Client can request immediate termination).

Remember, the agency has a separate contract with the client, which in the US always says
(or defaults to) Employment At Will.  If the agency gives you a 1 month guarantee and the
employer terminates you with less notice, the agency has to pay the difference. In the
exception I mentioned above, the agency paid 1 month's salary plus legal expenses for a
total of $20K.

>Smile sweetly and say: "Sorry. It is patently unfair and I cannot in all 
>conscience sign it. If you are unable to amend it then I guess you are stuck 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>walk.

I interviewed with the client, they loved me, contract was signed. While I was packing, an
ad for the job reappeared on DICE. It was exactly the same with the addition of one line
reading "Cannot pay more than $X", where X was $20 per hour less than I had agreed to.

I called the agency asking why they were trying to find someone cheaper. They said my rate
was too high, they were barely breaking even, wouldn't I take less, etc. etc. They said
the re-posting had been an accident and took it down. A few weeks after I started, the
agency's first billing arrived at the client's mailroom. Someone in the mailroom saw my
name and by mistake sent the bill to me. I saw that the bill rate was 240% of what I was
being paid. 

>> that we have a phrase 'walked out', meaning someone appears at your desk 
>> to ask for your
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>on both sides...)

"They" would have to be the agency, not the employer. You didn't have a contract with the
employer. 

>> Foreigners working on an H1 visa are especially vulnerable because loss of 
>> employment
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>that it is not something you can volunteer for...

They did at the turn of the last century, when they were called 'coolies'.

>It may be that contracting in the USA is "different" but I can't see how 
>unfairness in a contract would be accepted by any thinking person, anywhere. 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>contract terms, but because you were stupid...:-)

Here's trick to watch out for. It's happened to me THREE TIMES. 

Agencies are nervous between the day of the offer and your first day at work. They know
you have other irons in the fire and are afraid you'll receive and take a better offer. To
minimize their exposure, they make the offer on Friday and they say the client demands you
start Monday .. at 8am sharp. It's a lie they all tell. They haven't talked to the client
yet.

You bust your butt packing your stuff in one day, make hotel reservations and start
driving. En route you receive a call on your cell phone. They are checking to see that
you're moving to the client site. A few hours later you receive another call. They talked
to the client (for the first time) and learned he will not be ready for you Monday at 8am,
he wants you to start Thursday. There's nothing you can do except continue as planned and
sit in a hotel room for three days. 

To avoid this trick, call the client directly and negotiate a reasonable starting date.
When the pimp calls to demand 8am Monday, tell him the client asked you to start Thursday.
```

On Mon, 04 Feb 2008 00:35:08 -0600 Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:

:>Employment At Will is the legal default in the US (except one state). The only excepetion

Apples and oranges.

:>>I won't sign such a contract and haven't for 30 years. 

:>It's almost impossible to get a guarantee in he US.  I've only heard of one exception,
:>which specified 30 days notice. Labor unions have that in their contracts, but only 12% of
:>US workers belong to unions and none of them are computer people.

You can equally negotiate terms of employment.

You want X days notice? Negotiate it.

:>>As I mentioned earlier, people starting 
:>>to go free-lance should read the Agency contract and stike out what isn't 
:>>fair. Agencies simply try it on. If you have passed the interview, the 
:>>Agency is already counting the money you are ging to make for them. Do you

:>>explain to the Client that you won't be starting on Monday (after having

:>>few clauses in a contract that no-one is ever going to read anyway...?

:>>I have two favourite clauses that I simply change in front of them:

:>>1. Terms of notice. (1 month either way, except in cases of real bad 
:>>behaviour where the Client can request immediate termination).

:>Remember, the agency has a separate contract with the client, which in the US always says
:>(or defaults to) Employment At Will.  If the agency gives you a 1 month guarantee and the
:>employer terminates you with less notice, the agency has to pay the difference. In the
:>exception I mentioned above, the agency paid 1 month's salary plus legal expenses for a
:>total of $20K.

So what was the problem?

:>>Smile sweetly and say: "Sorry. It is patently unfair and I cannot in all 
:>>conscience sign it. If you are unable to amend it then I guess you are stuck 
:>>with a contract that  may not be enforceable in Law anyway, as Courts do not 
:>>look favourably on Employers or Intermediaries who try to abrogate the 
:>>rights of employees, and which I at least, simply cannot sign."  Don't 
:>>budge. If they say: "We cannot entertain making exceptions to it." Then get 
:>>up and say:"Well, it's kind of a pity because it seemed like a good job and 
:>>I know I could have made a contribution. However, it's obvious that we can't 
:>>do business...:Bye."

:>>I would bet money they'll be on the phone to you before you get 
:>>home...around about the same time the Agent who is about to lose his 
:>>commission realizes that the deal has not been closed, or the guy who owns 
:>>the Agency hears that cash is walking out the door...

:>>It is a VERY competitive business (I've seen all aspects of it) and if you 
:>>have someone a Client liked in interview, you have to be crazy to let them 
:>>walk.

:>I interviewed with the client, they loved me, contract was signed. While I was packing, an
:>ad for the job reappeared on DICE. It was exactly the same with the addition of one line
:>reading "Cannot pay more than $X", where X was $20 per hour less than I had agreed to.

:>I called the agency asking why they were trying to find someone cheaper. They said my rate
:>was too high, they were barely breaking even, wouldn't I take less, etc. etc. They said
:>the re-posting had been an accident and took it down. A few weeks after I started, the
:>agency's first billing arrived at the client's mailroom. Someone in the mailroom saw my
:>name and by mistake sent the bill to me. I saw that the bill rate was 240% of what I was
:>being paid. 

There is no real connection between what your norker is billing and what you
are billing.

Your deal is with the borker, and the borker has a deal with the client.

Try to cut out the middleman.

:>>> that we have a phrase 'walked out', meaning someone appears at your desk 
:>>> to ask for your
:>>> badge and escort you out the door. At one place, when a contractor refused 
:>>> to leave that
:>>> way, two strongarm security guards literally dragged him across the floor, 
:>>> kicking and
:>>> screaming.

:>>I have never been in that situation. I can assure you that I would leave 
:>>freely and head straight to my lawyer. They'd have a civil suit for breach 
:>>of contract slapped on them the next day. (But only an incredibly stupid 
:>>employer would even try it on, knowing that the contract stipulates 1 month 
:>>on both sides...)

:>"They" would have to be the agency, not the employer. You didn't have a contract with the
:>employer. 

But you do have a contract with the borker. If the client wishes to terminate,
it does not remove the borkers obligation to you (unless you agreed to such a
clause).

:>>> Foreigners working on an H1 visa are especially vulnerable because loss of 
:>>> employment
:>>> means deportation. That's why they do the crappiest jobs without complaint 
:>>> and never voice
:>>> an opinion  .. in public. Privately, they say it's like slavery.

:>>Then maybe they have never experienced actual slavery. My understanding is 
:>>that it is not something you can volunteer for...

:>They did at the turn of the last century, when they were called 'coolies'.

:>>It may be that contracting in the USA is "different" but I can't see how 
:>>unfairness in a contract would be accepted by any thinking person, anywhere. 
:>>A contract is simply an Agreement; it isn't Holy Writ. If you don't like the 
:>>terms of the Agreement, change them. That's what negotiation is all about. 
:>>If you're not prepared to read a contract and only sign it if you agree with 
:>>it, then you deserve to be frog marched off the site. Not because of the 
:>>contract terms, but because you were stupid...:-)

:>Here's trick to watch out for. It's happened to me THREE TIMES. 

:>Agencies are nervous between the day of the offer and your first day at work. They know
:>you have other irons in the fire and are afraid you'll receive and take a better offer. To
:>minimize their exposure, they make the offer on Friday and they say the client demands you
:>start Monday .. at 8am sharp. It's a lie they all tell. They haven't talked to the client
:>yet.

:>You bust your butt packing your stuff in one day, make hotel reservations and start
:>driving. En route you receive a call on your cell phone. They are checking to see that
:>you're moving to the client site. A few hours later you receive another call. They talked
:>to the client (for the first time) and learned he will not be ready for you Monday at 8am,
:>he wants you to start Thursday. There's nothing you can do except continue as planned and
:>sit in a hotel room for three days. 

:>To avoid this trick, call the client directly and negotiate a reasonable starting date.
:>When the pimp calls to demand 8am Monday, tell him the client asked you to start Thursday.

I don't understand.

Is the borker paying you for Monday to Thursday under such circumstances? If
so, what is the big deal? If not, you have a problem with your contract.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1l5eq395btg8is51pet4s220o8fn0d6qml@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <p4d6q3hg7qlidtj2sc556ttnkqtcduq5ki@4ax.com> <fnve1k$sfc$1@reader2.panix.com> <5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com> <fo1qla$hul$1@reader2.panix.com> <9959q31d9im18p9sn6tcvvemt992oob1cq@4ax.com> <60ls0iF1qht3eU1@mid.individual.net> <5i6dq351iqmdd55eh6s0ekr4e7h3cg9du1@4ax.com> <5bkdq39fodojsl4fkf65qdbgr3i77tpds0@4ax.com>`

```
On Mon, 04 Feb 2008 10:55:51 +0200, Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

>On Mon, 04 Feb 2008 00:35:08 -0600 Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:

>:>Agencies are nervous between the day of the offer and your first day at work. They know
>:>you have other irons in the fire and are afraid you'll receive and take a better offer. To
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>so, what is the big deal? If not, you have a problem with your contract.

No. He's paying you for billable hours. There is no pay for holidays, sick days, bad
weather days, etc. 

That's if you are "hourly." The alternative is "salaried", a mode being phased out.
Salaried people are paid like employees, but make $20K/yr less. In the old days, salaried
people were paid during unemployment between engagements, called "bench time." Since 2000
they are simply fired when the project ends.
```

On Mon, 04 Feb 2008 07:54:27 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:

>On Mon, 04 Feb 2008 10:55:51 +0200, Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:
>
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>they are simply fired when the project ends. 

Maybe in your world, but not mine.  I'm salaried, have health benefits
and a 401K.  I'm allowed 60 days bench time before I'm even considered
for a lay off.   In 11 years the longest bench time I've had is 35
days.  You're just working for the wrong people.

Steve
