###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-02-05T01:43:55+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<60ofkcF1se76cU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <p4d6q3hg7qlidtj2sc556ttnkqtcduq5ki@4ax.com> <fnve1k$sfc$1@reader2.panix.com> <5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com> <fo1qla$hul$1@reader2.panix.com> <9959q31d9im18p9sn6tcvvemt992oob1cq@4ax.com> <60ls0iF1qht3eU1@mid.individual.net> <5i6dq351iqmdd55eh6s0ekr4e7h3cg9du1@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:5i6dq351iqmdd55eh6s0ekr4e7h3cg9du1@4ax.com...
> On Mon, 4 Feb 2008 01:56:44 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> would be if the contract said otherwise.

Then make it say otherwise. That was my point.


>All regular non-contract employees work under
> Employment At Will.

As we are discussing contract work, that hardly seems relevant...

>
>>I won't sign such a contract and haven't for 30 years.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> US workers belong to unions and none of them are computer people.

I don't belong to a union and I have it in all of mine... :-)  And that's 
irrespective of whether I go through an Agency or go direct.

I can honestly say I never had to invoke it.

I try to keep the agreements I make and find that the people I make them 
with usually do too. It is just good business.

>
>>As I mentioned earlier, people starting
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> (or defaults to) Employment At Will.

And how is that my problem as a contractor? I'm signing a contract with ONE 
party. What is in it is negotiated with that party and is agreeable to them 
and to me or it doen't get signed. If an Agency is not capable of 
negotiating a fair deal with their client, then maybe they should be driving 
trucks rather than selling expertise...

> If the agency gives you a 1 month guarantee and the
> employer terminates you with less notice, the agency has to pay the 
> difference.

And quite right too. They should have made a better agreement with the 
client. Whatever... my agreement is with them and they cough up or I see 
them in Court.

>In the
> exception I mentioned above, the agency paid 1 month's salary plus legal 
> expenses for a
> total of $20K.

A puttance compared to the money they generate from contractors.

>
>>Smile sweetly and say: "Sorry. It is patently unfair and I cannot in all
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> being paid.

If you deal with cowboy agencies you really deserve what you get. It seems 
to me you are not even trying to negotiate a better deal, Robert.

>
>>> that we have a phrase 'walked out', meaning someone appears at your desk
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> employer.

Irrelevant. I would sue whoever I DID have the contract with, and I'd win 
because it was clearly breached.
>
>>> Foreigners working on an H1 visa are especially vulnerable because loss 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> They did at the turn of the last century, when they were called 'coolies'.

And that is relevant today.... how?

>
>>It may be that contracting in the USA is "different" but I can't see how
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Here's trick to watch out for. It's happened to me THREE TIMES.

Hmmmm... no comment :-)

>
> Agencies are nervous between the day of the offer and your first day at 
> work. They know
> you have other irons in the fire and are afraid you'll receive and take a 
> better offer.

How can you if you have signed a contract? Only if it has no notice period. 
So, in this case, it is to your advantage to have no notice period. Which 
makes the whole document largely  pointless really doesn't it?

>To
> minimize their exposure, they make the offer on Friday and they say the 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> yet.

So why shouldn't you phone the person who interviewed you and confirm when 
they are expecting you?

>
> You bust your butt packing your stuff in one day, make hotel reservations 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> sit in a hotel room for three days.

And bill the agency for your time, as it was they who required you to be 
there. (I wouldn't go until I had confirmed with the Client, anyway.)
>
> To avoid this trick, call the client directly and negotiate a reasonable 
> starting date.

Gosh, exactly what I suggested above... :-)

> When the pimp calls to demand 8am Monday, tell him the client asked you to 
> start Thursday.

It seems to me that you really don't have the business side of contracting 
well covered, Robert.

It's the 21st century, nobody can press gang you into working for somebody 
and neither can they railroad the terms of employment, UNLESS YOU LET THEM. 
Learn to negotiate; it is an essential life skill. Good negotiation doesn't 
mean ripping people off or making patently unfair deals; it simply means: "I 
will do this, if you will do that."   In terms of termination, a company 
obviously has a right to terminate your employment immediately if you start 
swinging an axe at the mainframe, or selling drugs in the car park, but not 
simply because a project was cancelled or they found somebody cheaper, or 
changed their mind arbitrarily. In these cases they must pay out a fair 
notice period and you must also work out a fair notice period (which should 
be the same period) if you find that there are pressing reasons why you 
cannot continue to work there. I'm not suggesting you should quit on 
caprice, but you MUST be able to get out, if they are able to fire you. It 
is simply fair.

I believe you when you outline what Agencies do in the USA, and what the 
"default" terms of employment are, but, as Binyamin pointed out, it comes 
down to power. If they want your skill and expertise, you are an idiot if 
you sell it short.

Fortunately, in Europe and even here in NZ they have a much more enlightened 
approach.

I remember one place in England where everybody had to "clock in" and "clock 
out", including the computer people. I had signed a three month contract 
(not knowing that clocking on/off was a requirement) so I sucked it up for 
twelve weeks.

(I like to work on sites where I haven't worked before, and only 
occasionally go back to previous employers, usually because they have 
something of particular interest to me, or they contact me and have a 
specific task they want me to do. In cases where the company don't know me 
and I don't know them, I suggest a 3 month contract at a sightly lower rate 
so we can both decide if we can do business. This seems fair to me and a 
number of companies have agreed to it. Only on one occasion did I not 
extend, and I have never had the company not want to extend...)

When they came to extend for one year I wrote into the contract that I would 
not be required to do the clocking in. All Hell broke loose. I had the 
Agency and the Client both phoning me demanding that I withdraw this clause, 
as it was standard practice etc. I refused. In fact, I told them to decide 
within three days or I'd be off somewhere else. A Senior manager (who didn't 
have to clock in :-)) phoned me the next day and said it was no problem and 
they were happy to have me there for 12 months. (I ended up staying 18...and 
never clocked in or out again :-))

All that any of us have for our survival is the couple of pounds of grey 
slush inside our heads. Invest in it, then sell it for as much as you can 
get. Selling it short or wasting your time on unfair and restrictive 
contracts with bullying agencies or companies, is simply not good survival 
skill.

It is about perception.

If you believe you have no choice, then you don't...:-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-02-04T22:50:49-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g7mfq31ta2md529qkcmh2flk0msqvk581t@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <p4d6q3hg7qlidtj2sc556ttnkqtcduq5ki@4ax.com> <fnve1k$sfc$1@reader2.panix.com> <5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com> <fo1qla$hul$1@reader2.panix.com> <9959q31d9im18p9sn6tcvvemt992oob1cq@4ax.com> <60ls0iF1qht3eU1@mid.individual.net> <5i6dq351iqmdd55eh6s0ekr4e7h3cg9du1@4ax.com> <60ofkcF1se76cU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Tue, 5 Feb 2008 01:43:55 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
>news:5i6dq351iqmdd55eh6s0ekr4e7h3cg9du1@4ax.com...

>>All regular non-contract employees work under
>> Employment At Will.
>
>As we are discussing contract work, that hardly seems relevant...

American employers think it applies to all workers.


>> They said my rate
>> was too high, they were barely breaking even, wouldn't I take less, etc. 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>to me you are not even trying to negotiate a better deal, Robert.

I negotiated a higher rate than they wanted to pay. The POINT of the story is that
agencies are greedy. They think 58% the bill rate is not a big enough cut, they want 75%.

In the old days, before Y2K, agencies kept 30% of the bill rate.

The agency isn't on your side; the agency is your competitor. The less you make, the more
they make.

>>>> Foreigners working on an H1 visa are especially vulnerable because loss 
>>>> of
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>And that is relevant today.... how?

Agencies regard contractors as coolies. Over 80% of US computer contractors are not
Americans, they're from other countries, mostly India. I know that because occasionally I
receive group emails listing their names. They too are our competition. If we try to drive
too hard a bargain, the agency has a hundred of them who'll accept the terms without
arguing. 

>> Agencies are nervous between the day of the offer and your first day at 
>> work. They know
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>How can you if you have signed a contract? Only if it has no notice period. 

It happens all the time. Legally the agency could sue, but they don't bother.

>So, in this case, it is to your advantage to have no notice period. Which 
>makes the whole document largely  pointless really doesn't it?

The worker give two weeks notice; the company, none. That's the Standard Contract. 

>> When the pimp calls to demand 8am Monday, tell him the client asked you to 
>> start Thursday.
>
>It seems to me that you really don't have the business side of contracting 
>well covered, Robert.

As a former options trader, I understand the basic tenet of business is supply and demand.
At least with options, you can always find SOMEone who'll buy. Contracting is like an
illiquid market, at times there are no buyers at any price. 

>It's the 21st century, nobody can press gang you into working for somebody 
>and neither can they railroad the terms of employment, UNLESS YOU LET THEM. 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>swinging an axe at the mainframe, or selling drugs in the car park

Or showing them up by writing much better code. Not by saying a single critical word; just
by writing code that's too good. They're afraid the boss will find out and their job will
be in jeopardy.

>, but not 
>simply because a project was cancelled or they found somebody cheaper, or 
>changed their mind arbitrarily. 

It happens routinely .. not only to contractors but also to employees. You read about it
in the paper all the time. Company X lays off 30,000, stock goes up 10%.

>In these cases they must pay out a fair 
>notice period and you must also work out a fair notice period (which should 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>is simply fair.

US labor law isn't based on the law of equity, it is based on implied contract. The
underlying assumption is that both parties have equal power and bargaining acumen. You're
saying the same thing. In reality, there is a huge IMbalance of power. The result is
unilateral contracts.

>I believe you when you outline what Agencies do in the USA, and what the 
>"default" terms of employment are, but, as Binyamin pointed out, it comes 
>down to power. If they want your skill and expertise, you are an idiot if 
>you sell it short.

Most employers don't know how to assess skill during an interview. They don't ask the
right questions. They know after a month or two of experience. 

I remember one interview that went like this:
Q. Do you have any experience with X?
A. I wrote it.
Q. You wrote what??
A. I designed and wrote the tool.
Q. That's impossible. It's owned by the Y company.
A. Right. I used to work for their predecessor, the Z company.
Q. Umm .. we're looking for three years experience USING the tool. 
     Do you have that?
A. I've not had a job using it, except testing during development.
Q. Did that take three years?
A. No, it took about six months.
Q. I'm afraid you don't meet our qualifications. Good luck.

>Fortunately, in Europe and even here in NZ they have a much more enlightened 
>approach.

That's easy; it couldn't be any less enlightened. :)

>When they came to extend for one year I wrote into the contract that I would 
>not be required to do the clocking in. All Hell broke loose. I had the 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>never clocked in or out again :-))

I knew a guy wrote into the contract that he would have root permission. Needless to say,
neither the pimp nor manager who signed the contract had a clue what that meant.  When he
asked for it, the team lead and sysadmin laughed. Root permission .. yeah right! This was
for applications, not systems. He showed them the contract. Phone calls were made;
meetings were held. The next day he got it, and a lot of dirty looks.

>If you believe you have no choice, then you don't...:-)

Good pep talk, coach.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-02-06T01:58:07+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<60r4qdF1rpe1rU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <p4d6q3hg7qlidtj2sc556ttnkqtcduq5ki@4ax.com> <fnve1k$sfc$1@reader2.panix.com> <5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com> <fo1qla$hul$1@reader2.panix.com> <9959q31d9im18p9sn6tcvvemt992oob1cq@4ax.com> <60ls0iF1qht3eU1@mid.individual.net> <5i6dq351iqmdd55eh6s0ekr4e7h3cg9du1@4ax.com> <60ofkcF1se76cU1@mid.individual.net> <g7mfq31ta2md529qkcmh2flk0msqvk581t@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:g7mfq31ta2md529qkcmh2flk0msqvk581t@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 5 Feb 2008 01:43:55 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Then contractors should gently point out that it doesn't...
>
>>> They said my rate
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> I negotiated a higher rate than they wanted to pay.

But you didn't, because they were not satisfied and tried to get round it. A 
negotiation is complete when the parties concerned AGREE and sign the 
agreement.


>The POINT of the story is that
> agencies are greedy.

Greed is by means limited to agencies... :-)

>They think 58% the bill rate is not a big enough cut, they want 75%.

Er... some agencies WANT 95%. Fortunately, we have a choice of the agencies 
we use.

If, as a contractor, I am totally unselective and forward to my CV to every 
agency that advertises, it is almost certain I will be on file with some 
cowboy agencies... On the other hand, If I develop a working relationship 
with a handful of reputable and ethical agencies, where I know the 
principals and they know me, I have a much better chance of getting a fair 
deal.

In the days when I worked as a COBOL contractor I worked through only 2 
agencies in the U.K. and knew the people in both of them.  About a week 
before I planned returning to the U.K., I would contact them and let them 
know I was looking for work. The principal of one of them would meet me at 
Heathrow, accommodate me at his house for a few days and have me placed 
within a week, on one occasion (the second UK contract I ever did)  he even 
loaned me a car to get to work until I could afford to buy one. Over the 
years he made serious money out of me, but he deserved to. (I also did quite 
nicely thank you :-)) The point is, that if you get to know your agent, 
build a working relationship as you should in any business, you don't 
encounter many of the problems being described here.

I look back on these early years with fondness, not regret. I NEVER got 
ripped off by an agency or a company, because I negotiated fairly, did what 
I said I would do, and didn't back stab anybody.
>
> In the old days, before Y2K, agencies kept 30% of the bill rate.

Yes, I remembe the days of 50% and it was later cut to 33%. IN the UK now 
many companies REQUIRE agencies to take no more than 15%.

When I am recruiting for a project I ensure that this is the case, and sit 
down with the successful candidate and the agency rep, to ensure that the 
rates on the contract with the candidate reflect this. I don't want people 
who are being exploited, workingon my project .


>
> The agency isn't on your side; the agency is your competitor. The less you 
> make, the more
> they make.

A very cynical and short term view. The best games are the ones where 
everyone wins.
>
>>>>> Foreigners working on an H1 visa are especially vulnerable because 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> arguing.

And they will get what they pay for. If you can't offer anything more than 
this cheap labour pool then you have placed yourself in the wrong market.

>
>>> Agencies are nervous between the day of the offer and your first day at
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Contract.

Only if you sign it. My point is... Don't!!!
>
>>> When the pimp calls to demand 8am Monday, tell him the client asked you 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> illiquid market, at times there are no buyers at any price.

Then it's time to do something else...

>
>>It's the 21st century, nobody can press gang you into working for somebody
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> be in jeopardy.

Robert, this is just silly. Maybe you violated their standards, maybe you 
pissed everyone off, but NO-ONE is going to be fired for writing code that 
"shows people up"...

>
>>, but not
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> saying the same thing.

Yes, because I believe it.

> In reality, there is a huge IMbalance of power. The result is
> unilateral contracts.

Again, it comes down to perception. The worst that can happen is you don't 
get the job. If the job was never available you wouldn't have got it either. 
It is not the end of the world. Develop skills that are in demand and sell 
them.

>
>>I believe you when you outline what Agencies do in the USA, and what the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> right questions.

Interviewing is an acquired skill which I agree is not always well done by 
companies (and applicants). It isn't just about technical skills. If you go 
to an interview and feel someone hasn't "asked the right questions" then 
there are positive aspects of your candidacy which have not emerged.

Why would you allow that to happen?

Can you not offer the information you would have, in response to the "right" 
question, anyway? Anyone leaving an interview should feel reasonably certain 
that they communicated the most positive information they could (without 
lying :-)), and presented themselves in as positive a way as possible, 
irrespective of the questions asked. If you believe you can contribute to 
the site, then tell them that and discuss how... (Maybe you had similar 
experience on another site and it was solved).

Answer the questions (whether they are "right" or "wrong") and take the 
opportunity when answering a "wrong" question to present the information you 
would have, if it had been the "right" question, at the same time...

>They know after a month or two of experience.

If you believe that (and I'm neither agreeing nor disagreeing with you), why 
not suggest a "Trial contract" for three months with review by both sides at 
the end of it? I have done this and it has worked very well.

The point is that it isn't a rigid process determined only by questions and 
contracts. It is a negotiation.


>
> I remember one interview that went like this:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Q. I'm afraid you don't meet our qualifications. Good luck.

How about this...

> Q. Do you have any experience with X?
> A. Yes, I worked on the team that designed and built it. It was originally 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> really useful to you folks :-)

By now they have forgotten all about the "three years experience" rule and 
will eagerly discuss the current problems and what has led them to seek 
contract resource.

Don't wave your superiority in their face; instead, inspire confidence that 
you can deal with whatever's bugging them, and are a useful guy to have 
around. Be genuinely interested in the problems they are having. You are 
presenting yourself as a solution.

If you can get them talking about their actual problems and can persuade 
them you have solutions (without actually detailing the solutions... they 
have to pay for it :-)) there is no way you can fail the interview.

>
>>Fortunately, in Europe and even here in NZ they have a much more 
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> meetings were held. The next day he got it, and a lot of dirty looks.

Good for him! And good for them that they honoured their contractual 
obligation, even though it was not "the usual".

You see... terms ARE negotiable. :-)
>
>>If you believe you have no choice, then you don't...:-)
>
> Good pep talk, coach.

:-) You can stay on the reserve bench and think over what I said...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-02-05T19:48:11-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d1uhq31f72snoajtm3jsm40kjq7tu95128@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <p4d6q3hg7qlidtj2sc556ttnkqtcduq5ki@4ax.com> <fnve1k$sfc$1@reader2.panix.com> <5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com> <fo1qla$hul$1@reader2.panix.com> <9959q31d9im18p9sn6tcvvemt992oob1cq@4ax.com> <60ls0iF1qht3eU1@mid.individual.net> <5i6dq351iqmdd55eh6s0ekr4e7h3cg9du1@4ax.com> <60ofkcF1se76cU1@mid.individual.net> <g7mfq31ta2md529qkcmh2flk0msqvk581t@4ax.com> <60r4qdF1rpe1rU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Wed, 6 Feb 2008 01:58:07 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>
>
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>agreement.

They signed. The project lasted a year, at the original rate.

>>The POINT of the story is that
>> agencies are greedy.
>
>Greed is by [no] means limited to agencies... :-)

This is one of Doc's Brooklyn Bridge defenses.  :)

>>They think 58% the bill rate is not a big enough cut, they want 75%.
>
>Er... some agencies WANT 95%. Fortunately, we have a choice of the agencies 
>we use.

Sounds like you've been talking to Tata, Kelly or Robert Half. 

>If, as a contractor, I am totally unselective and forward to my CV to every 
>agency that advertises, it is almost certain I will be on file with some 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>deal.

I've never worked for the same agency on consecutive projects. Not because of any
displeasure (I've worked for some twice, non-consecutively), rather because of low demand
for Cobol contractors. 

>> In the old days, before Y2K, agencies kept 30% of the bill rate.
>
>Yes, I remembe the days of 50% and it was later cut to 33%. IN the UK now 
>many companies REQUIRE agencies to take no more than 15%.

Average in the US is about 50%, certainly more than 33%. Books about the agency business
say they should target 50%, will go broke at less than 35%.

>When I am recruiting for a project I ensure that this is the case, and sit 
>down with the successful candidate and the agency rep, to ensure that the 
>rates on the contract with the candidate reflect this. I don't want people 
>who are being exploited, working on my project .

US agencies will not reveal their bill rate to the candidate. They have a contract clause
forbidding the worker to reveal his rate to the client and other contractors. 

>> The agency isn't on your side; the agency is your competitor. The less you 
>> make, the more
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>everyone wins.

I'll say that next time they try to bargain the rate down. 

I have to laugh when the pimp tries to blame a low rate on the client. "The Widget Company
cannot pay more than $28." 

When they ask "What's your lowest rate?", I've been known to answer "I'd be happy with 70%
of the bill rate, whatever it comes to." After a pause, they growl "We need a number."


>>>>>> Foreigners working on an H1 visa are especially vulnerable because 
>>>>>> loss
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>this cheap labour pool then you have placed yourself in the wrong market.

They're not stupid. Many of them are better than Americans in general ability. They're not
very strong on Cobol, though. They all know Java and C, regard Cobol as a step down.

>>>It's the 21st century, nobody can press gang you into working for somebody
>>>and neither can they railroad the terms of employment, UNLESS YOU LET 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>"shows people up"...

It's happened to me twice and I've seen it happen to others. It would have happened more
times if I hadn't played dumb. It only happens in Old Style Cobol shops, where former
mainframers feel threatened by New Technology. No, the written (and unwritten) standards
were not violated. I went through them item by item to make sure. The code was simply too
well written.

Places where this is most likely are small shops having lousy code and weak management
above the team lead. It's unlikely to happen in very large companies. 

Managers want the best talent. Low-level supervisors are the ones who fear competition. 
They fear the manager might replace them with the contractor. Suppose the shop has a batch
job that runs four hours every day. The WORST thing a contractor could do is fix it to run
in one hour. The manager would find out for sure, and that scares the locals.

>If you believe that (and I'm neither agreeing nor disagreeing with you), why 
>not suggest a "Trial contract" for three months with review by both sides at 
>the end of it? I have done this and it has worked very well.

It's a six month project. The development phase is scheduled to finish in two months. They
can't try someone out for three months. 

>> Q. Really?? We need someone with reasonable experience using it and who is 
>> completely familiar with it. We're asking for three years with it...
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Q. Yes, it causes (whatever). Have you come across that?

Mr. Big doesn't have a clue what problems they had. His introductory sentence is "Before
we start I must tell you I'm Not Technical." That means don't ask me any technical
questions, all I know is what it says on the requirements sheet. I'm assessing soft
skills, like whether you play golf and How About Dem Bears. 

You get the impression he's looking for a Drinking Buddy.

Most contractors are hired by phone interview, not an in person interview. Typically,
there will be three people at the other end -- Mr. Big, Team Lead and Mysterious Person.
Team Lead will ask most questions, reading from a script. Mysterious Person is the one to
watch out for. He's been there twenty years and is now counting the days 'till retirement.
His role is protecting turf and tradition, not getting things done. I've not figured out
how to handle him. When I was managing, I simply fired him. Maybe he senses that.
```

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:d1uhq31f72snoajtm3jsm40kjq7tu95128@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 6 Feb 2008 01:58:07 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[65 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sounds like you've been talking to Tata, Kelly or Robert Half.

Nope. These days, I rarely talk to agencies at all. When I do, I adopt the 
same attitude I have always had: "Can we do business? Let's negotiate. I 
will do this if you will do that...".

>
>>If, as a contractor, I am totally unselective and forward to my CV to 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> for Cobol contractors.

Even more reason to build a business relationship...
>
>>> In the old days, before Y2K, agencies kept 30% of the bill rate.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> contractors.

Cross it out! Why are you so willing to simply roll over and say: "Shaft 
me!"? :-) It is a NEGOTIATION. You just don't seem to have grasped this 
concept...
>
>>> The agency isn't on your side; the agency is your competitor. The less 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'll say that next time they try to bargain the rate down.

"Bargaining" is not negotiating. What added benefits would they be prepared 
to give, in order to justify you lowering your rate. ("I will do this, if 
you will do that.")

>
> I have to laugh when the pimp tries to blame a low rate on the client. 
> "The Widget Company
> cannot pay more than $28."

"Then, sadly, the Widget company will have to make do with lesser 
expertise... Now, if I was to accept $28, what benefits can your agency 
bring to my table to make the package worth considering?".

>
> When they ask "What's your lowest rate?", I've been known to answer "I'd 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

"We are in negotiation and you want me to throw in my hand at round one :-) 
I won't tell you my lowest rate because i really don't know. It would depend 
on what other factors were included in the package. Do you want to talk 
about accommodation, travel, per diem, (all things that are tax deductible 
for you or the client), or things like parking previleges on the client 
site... what can you bring to the table to support me accepting a lower than 
market rate? I'm happy to discuss it. Let's find out what my lowest rate is, 
based on the overall package."
>
>>>>>>> Foreigners working on an H1 visa are especially vulnerable because
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> step down.

I never suggested they are stupid. I merely suggested that you are competing 
in the wrong market.
>
>>>>It's the 21st century, nobody can press gang you into working for 
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> well written.

Sorry, I don't accept that.
>
> Places where this is most likely are small shops having lousy code and 
> weak management
> above the team lead. It's unlikely to happen in very large companies.

I have never seen it, but I believe you see it that way.
>
> Managers want the best talent. Low-level supervisors are the ones who fear 
> competition.
> They fear the manager might replace them with the contractor.

 Then the contractor should reassure them that he is a contractor by choice 
and is NOT looking for a permannent job, never mind replacing people who are 
already in permanent jobs. Of course, if this is not the case, then you 
CAN'T say that. If that is the situation, we can't pursue this 
conversation... I'm not into dishonesty...:-)

>Suppose the shop has a batch
> job that runs four hours every day. The WORST thing a contractor could do 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> can't try someone out for three months.

OK, 2 weeks... Negotiate.
>
>>> Q. Really?? We need someone with reasonable experience using it and who 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> skills, like whether you play golf and How About Dem Bears.

Do you think those are worthless questions?

They deserve answers just as much as questions on your knowledge of Unix 
commands...

You should not treat this interview any more frivolously than you would a 
technical one.

Think holistically. What use to the company is someone who has incredible 
expertise, but will never work with the existing team or fit into the 
corporate culture?
>
> You get the impression he's looking for a Drinking Buddy.

Not just him; everybody. Human beings are social animals.
>
> Most contractors are hired by phone interview, not an in person interview. 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> senses that.

I have obtained jobs through phone interview, sometimes in another country. 
Never been a problem.

I'm not saying what you say is wrong, Robert, I know you have no reason to 
lie about it. But I do think your perception needs adjustment.

Everyone ISN'T out to get you when you start a contract, and if you are open 
and honest, and supportive of what is being done, you will make friends 
quickly. If you are hostile, hyper-critical and only too willing to show how 
superior you are, you will alienate everybody and they'll all be glad to see 
the back of you. You have to keep a reasonably low profile for a few weeks 
until you have made allies, and had a chance to look at how they do things. 
Then, you can gradually introduce suggestions for improving things, without 
being pedantic or bullying. If you haven't got credibility, no-one will 
listen to you. You can't get credibility if you alienate everybody right off 
the bat.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-02-06T10:42:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<foc2ud$d8$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <60r4qdF1rpe1rU1@mid.individual.net> <d1uhq31f72snoajtm3jsm40kjq7tu95128@4ax.com> <60slguF1rso9tU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <60slguF1rso9tU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>Think holistically. What use to the company is someone who has incredible 
>expertise, but will never work with the existing team or fit into the 
>corporate culture?

The use might be, Mr Dashwood, as someone who brings a set of skills that 
are technical, rather than social; a large-enough organisation might have 
a broom-closet or a 'skunk works' where producing Code That Works is more 
valued than knowing the ball-scores.

In the US there is the term 'team player' that gets bandied about; it 
seems to be used to indicate 'someone who's just like everyone else, an 
interchangeable part'... but if everyone else's skills were up to the task 
at hand then they might not be looking for a consultant.

I'm not much for sports metaphors in the business world... as I see sports 
as ritualised combat I tend to substitute military metaphors.  A brigade 
has enough room in it for parade soldiers - folks who can shine their 
boots and march precisely - and for snipers, the guys who go out into the 
field, settle into position for a week and wait for the universe around 
them to coalesce into the right series of shapes so a single shot 
traverses 1,200 meters and deals with a target.

To expect a sniper to be a marching-soldier will, quite often, result in 
disappointment all around.

DD
```

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:foc2ud$d8$1@reader2.panix.com...
> In article <60slguF1rso9tU1@mid.individual.net>,

> disappointment all around.

A very lucid analogy, Doc. I take your point.

And I know that "team player" is translated by some managers as "brown 
noser" or "yes" man.

Nevertheless, speaking only for myself, and having worked alongside some 
incredible social misfits (interesting... sometimes amusing, but always 
stressful...:-)), I have to say that I would prefer people on my team who 
can at least relate to others to the extent of passing social machinery and 
contributing to workshops... :-) I recognise that this may mean missing out 
on certain skill levels, but the welfare of the team as a whole is more 
important than any individual on it.

Pete.
```

_(thread depth: 22)_

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:focdug$alp$1@reader2.panix.com...
> In article <60tjc9F1slqueU1@mid.individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> consultants/contractors/hired guns.

You may take it as read that things get done on my team. That is largely 
because I'm careful who gets on it (irrespective of their employment 
status).

In cases where my choices are limited by other factors, I manage the 
potential trouble makers  and misfits more actively than the others :-)

Once people demonstrate they don't need management (a combination of many 
intangibles, but largely attitude and results), I don't actively manage 
them; so long as I'm not sure about that, then I do actively manage them...

I have found this approach to work pretty well and it means I don't waste my 
time babysitting and micromanaging people. I can then provide help and 
support to everybody.

Pete.
```

```
wrote:
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-02-06T13:36:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<focd5o$dh8$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <60r4qdF1rpe1rU1@mid.individual.net> <d1uhq31f72snoajtm3jsm40kjq7tu95128@4ax.com> <60slguF1rso9tU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <60slguF1rso9tU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>
>"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message  news:d1uhq31f72snoajtm3jsm40kjq7tu95128@4ax.com...

[snip]

>> I have to laugh when the pimp tries to blame a low rate on the client. 
>> "The Widget Company
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>bring to my table to make the package worth considering?".

This seems remarkably similar, Mr Dashwood, to my own Standard Reply of 
'Then I hope they are very happy with the person they find to fill this 
slot... because it isn't going to be me.'

>
>>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>based on the overall package."

I believe, Mr Dashwood, that Mr Wagner is speaking about body-shops which 
have a rate to offer and little else.  Such shops are interested, in my 
experience, in establishing a relationship which results in their invoices 
to a customer being paid... nothing less and, usually, very, *very* little 
more.  What you are suggesting sounds more like... let me grub around the 
archives for a moment, ahhhh... from 
<http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/9afa669dbced6a2d?dmode=source>

--begin quoted text:

'Let me explain... a few years back I was on a gig, making a hair under 
US$65/hr, and the project leader started making noises about taking me on 
full-time.  I asked 'What do you have to offer?' and he responded 
'US$67,500/yr to start.' I said 'If that's all that you have then I can't 
understand what reasons there are for me to consider, seriously, an offer 
to cut my salary in half.'

The pimp nodded and said 'I understand...'... and I smiled, broadly, and 
said 'No, you *don't* understand, just as the manager didn't.  Does the 
company have a good insurance package to offer?  Fine, then explain it to 
me... does the company have a good tuition rebate program?  Good, tell me 
about it... does the company have excellent vacation schedules, maybe a 
travel-agent tie-in for cheap places to go?  Great, let me hear about 
them... but all he had to give was the figure for an annual salary and if 
that, indeed, is all he had... then he was *right* not to go on.  A job's 
a variety of factors and this guy could not, even to his own mind, compete 
with my then-current situation on price... and he had nothing to offer 
beyond that.'

Later on, as I was walking with Killer around ManMade lake, I mused on 
this... and I thought about how little I had seen of bargaing that 
includes playing salary off against other forms of remuneration.

'The salary is a little low... how about this, in addition to the salary I 
get full tuition rebate on *any* graduate-level course I take and get a B 
or better in?

'The salary's a little low... how about raising the company matching-rate 
for my pension contributions from 25% to 40%?'

'The salary's a little low... how about accelerating the schedule and 
amounts by which I accumulate vacation?'

It has been my experience that managers would respond to suggestions such 
as these with a smile and 'That's a good idea but Personnel wouldn't let 
me.'

(side note - if someone with sufficient Corporate Authority makes a call 
to Personnel *anything* can be done... but let's leave that aside for the 
moment)

--end quoted text

[snip to 'showing people up by writing better code']

>> It's happened to me twice and I've seen it happen to others. It would have 
>> happened more
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Sorry, I don't accept that.

Mr Dashwood, I knew a fellow who took a job writing COBOL for New York 
State in the late 1980s... he'd been, among other things, a FORTRAN coder 
since the late 1960s.

While sitting around waiting for something to happen he looked at some 
programs... and then, using COBOL (to implement some LISP techniques he'd 
learned in the Oldene Dayse) he wrote a utility to parse the 88-levels out 
of source files and format them into an easy-reference guide.

As he tells it - and I was not there - his boss went into his manager's 
office, screaming about 'What is this guy... some ringer you brought in to 
make us all look bad?'

(see http://dictionary.reference.com/browse/ringer)

>>
>> Places where this is most likely are small shops having lousy code and 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I have never seen it, but I believe you see it that way.

It may be more than a matter of interpretation, Mr Dashwood... if it is a 
fact that some people feel threatened by 'The Different' and that 'The 
Better' can be, at times, a manifestation of 'The Different'... well, the 
rest is left as an exercise for the reader.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

```
On Wed, 6 Feb 2008 13:36:56 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>In article <60slguF1rso9tU1@mid.individual.net>,
>Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>>> When they ask "What's your lowest rate?", I've been known to answer "I'd 
>>> be happy with 70%
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>more.  What you are suggesting sounds more like... 

I forgot to mention the phrase "all inclusive", as in "What's your lowest all inclusive
rate?" Translation: we don't want to negotiate benefits, give us a number, buy your own
benefits. I picture a spreadsheet with the cursor in the rate column next to my name.

On the rare occasions when I mentioned a benefit, they became irritated. "No no, I need an
ALL INCLUSIVE rate."
```

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:focd5o$dh8$1@reader2.panix.com...
> In article <60slguF1rso9tU1@mid.individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> slot... because it isn't going to be me.'

We agree?! "Then one of us must be wrong..." :-)
>
>>
…[55 more quoted lines elided]…
> or better in?

That is an excellent one...
>
> 'The salary's a little low... how about raising the company matching-rate
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> amounts by which I accumulate vacation?'

All good...

>
> It has been my experience that managers would respond to suggestions such
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> moment)

Absolutely. I have seen this in action.

>
> --end quoted text
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>

OK.

Some years ago I was in New York and while I was there I received three 
offers of employment. (I was't looking for work and these offers came about 
incidentally to the actual purpose of my visit)

One of them was from a major consultancy, the principal of which I found to 
be a racist bigot (I thanked him for the lunch and left).

The second was from someone who spent most of the time telling me about the 
wonders of life in New York city, what a privelege it was to have a chance 
to work there, and how I could be assured that by working in New York my 
career would at last be fulfilled...Sadly, he seemed to have no concept of a 
world beyond New York City. (I thanked him for the lunch and left)

The last one was the most entertaining...

"We can start a programming school. The City will pay us to train the wops 
and the spics and the blacks. Geeez, Pete, we can charge 'em a hunnerd 
bucks, JUST TER DO THE APTITOOD TEST!!!"  (I thanked him for the luch and 
left)

The reason I relate these stories is that it didn't give me a favourable 
impression of IT management in New York city.

If there are insecure managers or programmers who believe that they are 
threatened by competency, New York is where I would expect to find them.

As you (through your friend), and Robert (at first hand), have now both 
related experiences where competency was seen as a threat, I have to accept 
what you say and stand corrected.

All I can say is that it is a completely new and foreign experience for me. 
I have never felt threatened by anyone else's competency, rather, I have 
seen it as an opportunity to learn something.  I don't know anyone (and I 
have come in contact with a large number of people involved in IT) who has 
ever related such an experience (apart from the two of you).

Perhaps the world outside of New York city (or the USA) is indeed.... 
different.

Pete.
```

```
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:focd5o$dh8$1@reader2.panix.com...
>> In article <60slguF1rso9tU1@mid.individual.net>,
>> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>>>I won't tell you my lowest rate because i really don't know. It would 
>>>depend
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>> --begin quoted text:

[snip]

>> 'The salary is a little low... how about this, in addition to the salary I
>> get full tuition rebate on *any* graduate-level course I take and get a B
>> or better in?
>
>That is an excellent one...

It resulted from my having seen a Catch-22 employed when it came to 
tuition reimbursement.  A company's policy would say that tuition would be 
paid for any job-related course... and then the Corner-Office Idiot would 
deny requests as being unrelated to one's job ('Advanced Database 
Techniques?  We only use the most basic ones here, no need for you to know 
anything else.') or superfluous ('All our code works fine under the new 
compiler, there's no need for a course in Release x.xx Features.')

[snip]

>> It has been my experience that managers would respond to suggestions such
>> as these with a smile and 'That's a good idea but Personnel wouldn't let
…[64 more quoted lines elided]…
>impression of IT management in New York city.

Point of information, Mr Dashwood... the fellow I mentioned was coding for 
the State, not the City; he was located in Albany, about one hundred fifty 
miles north.

>
>If there are insecure managers or programmers who believe that they are 
>threatened by competency, New York is where I would expect to find them.

The sentiment below was formed, I believe, long before she saw the Isle of 
Manhattan... so it might hold elsewhere.  From

<http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/9b3c2b947a2a9169?dmode=source>

--begin quoted text:

Date: 1997/12/29

[snip]

Hmmmm... decades back, when I got my First Job, my sainted Mother, may
she sleep with the angels, gave me the following sage counsel:

'Then it comes to work remember two things:  you can be wrong about
something... and be fired for it; you can be right about something...
and be fired for it.'

--end quoted text 

>
>As you (through your friend), and Robert (at first hand), have now both 
>related experiences where competency was seen as a threat, I have to accept 
>what you say and stand corrected.

No, Mr Dashwood... you might say 'I have read accounts by people who have 
reported that demonstrating competence on the job has resulted in what 
seems to have been unpleasant outcomes; perhaps Oscar Wilde was on to 
something when he said that 'no good deed goes unpunished'.'... or a 
formulation less verbose.

>
>All I can say is that it is a completely new and foreign experience for me. 
>I have never felt threatened by anyone else's competency, rather, I have 
>seen it as an opportunity to learn something.

What a wonderful world it is that has new things to learn in it!

DD
```

```
In article <d1uhq31f72snoajtm3jsm40kjq7tu95128@4ax.com>,
Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
>On Wed, 6 Feb 2008 01:58:07 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
>wrote:

[snip]

>>Greed is by [no] means limited to agencies... :-)
>
>This is one of Doc's Brooklyn Bridge defenses.  :)

It is not 'mine', Mr Wagner... I believe I usually cite the source whence 
I recall learning it, my Sainted Paternal Grandmother (may she sleep with 
the angels).

[snip]

>>If, as a contractor, I am totally unselective and forward to my CV to every 
>>agency that advertises, it is almost certain I will be on file with some 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>because of low demand for Cobol contractors. 

My experience with this aspect is similar; I make it clear, from the 
start, that I prefer working to unemployment and that the paycheques need 
to keep flowing to my banque.  If Agency A placed me on a job then Agency 
A gets its percentage as long as it keeps me on-site; if Agency A has 
nothing for me to do and doesn't want to pay me for doing nothing then 
perhaps Agency B might assist me in allowing me to maintain my contract 
with my landlord.

Nothing personal here, just business... I like/need to work.

DD
```

```
Or maybe it is just my perception.   It may be that those experiences

were treated as employees were the ones where you were an employee.

supports this guess.
```

```
In article <uwTpj.99288$KG4.66167@fe09.news.easynews.com>,
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

[snip]

>Do you think that there MIGHT be a relationship between the types of
>groups that 
>you are hired into and the attitude that you (occasionally) demonstrate within 
>this group?

Hmmmmm... to search the archives - if such a thing is polite - yields 
<http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/1ed938c3c5c59da0?dmode=source>

--begin quoted text:

'Fun' is in the mind of the beholder... and it is difficult to escape the 
village in the pack that one carries on one's back.  To search the 
archives - if such a thing is polite - yields 
<http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/2573afac4b2540d3?dmode=source>

--begin quoted text:

Here's a story for you...

Are you sitting comfortably? Good. Then I'll begin....

A traveller approaches the Gates of a great city.

He sees an old man, dressed in rags, sitting in the dust, warming his 
ancient bones in the hot sun.

The traveller addresses the old man...

"What are the people in this City like?"

"What were the people in the City you came from like?"

The traveller shudders at the memory.

"They were awful. Mean, money-grubbing, unkind, impolite, unfriendly, 
unhelpful, a real bunch of nasty no-hopers. That's why I left..."

The old man nods gently and says:

"I'm afraid they're just the same here..."

The traveller decides to move on and try his luck at the next city.

The old man dozes...

Presently, the sound of a tired horse approaching makes him raise his 
head. Another traveller stands before him and asks the same question:

"What are the people in this City like?"

The old man responds...

"What were the people in the City you came from like?"

"Ah, they were marvellous! Good, decent, generous folk. Always a smile and 
a helping hand where needed, truly the salt of the Earth. In fact I'm 
wondering if the Wander Lust that brought me here was such a good thing... 
I had many good and genuine friends back there..."

The old man stands up and opens the gate.

"You will find the same decent people here. Welcome to our City..."

They go into the City together.

--end quoted text

--end quoted text

DD
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hgekq3d6p44hk6djnmjua7pkeqf9kf5e3e@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <1u65q3l1v0rcriqpa82bvev2p8j9hiqcdq@4ax.com> <fnuse4$6np$1@reader2.panix.com> <p4d6q3hg7qlidtj2sc556ttnkqtcduq5ki@4ax.com> <fnve1k$sfc$1@reader2.panix.com> <5so6q3l17qiq3gf69mi6bfk976dh1p0hc5@4ax.com> <araeq3hboc5le6jejepfopq4co8hqlbpr1@4ax.com> <cpvfq3t1ej7poa0en2ipdu4urogh937vbh@4ax.com> <bsvgq3tkpg6f6odndukkm4k3c5jlakrut5@4ax.com> <fr8hq351lj160cqtqodq9h3m7t2j9nskos@4ax.com> <fejjq3hmlphn209ap93i4rmhjfc0ujopnn@4ax.com>`

```
On Wed, 06 Feb 2008 08:17:18 -0700, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On Tue, 05 Feb 2008 12:03:15 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>supports this guess.

>They treated all contractors that way, not just me.

>>>I just told you that wasn't my experience.    So you're saying I'm
>>>either a liar or a fool.    You seem to know everything - which one am
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>You said "all contractors".

"They treated all contractors that way " refers to all THEIR contractors, not all
contractors on the planet.

The pronoun "they" is referential to the (unquoted) earlier subject "places where I
worked", therefore the elided pronoun "their" must corefer to the same subject. The
general rules are:

"If a pronoun in the antecedent VP is semantically bound, the corresponding pronoun in the
elided VP must be bound in parallel. If the first pronoun is referential, the second
pronoun must corefer with it."

 "No LF representation may contain both semantically bound and free occurrences of the
same pronoun. "

For example:

Felix hates his neighbor, and Max does too.

1. Felix hates Howard's neighbor, and Max hates Howard's neighbor.
2. Felix hates his own neighbor, and Max hates Felix's neighbor.
3. Felix hates his own neighbor, and Max hates his own neighbor.

Answer 2 is the same as answer 1. Both are correct, depending on the referent of "his".
Answer 3 is sloppy reading, because the second "his" does not refer to the same subject.

http://books.google.com/books?id=oGQQop8ii0sC&pg=PA116&dq=elided+pronoun&sig=xueBKPfHlOvvCQ9g9md96_v-hSM#PPA116,M1

>I responded that this is not what I have seen.
>
>You respond to my statement "all contractors" again.    This isn't a
>comment on my perception?   

 If my experience contradicts your experience, your complaint is against reality. Don't
kill the messenger because he delivered a message you don't like.

>I ask whether you're saying I'm a liar or a fool.

No. It's your disjunction. The adjectives are not exclusive. A person can be both a liar
and a fool, only one, or neither. 

I don't offer black and white choices. 

>You modify your statement to 14 Fortune 100 companies, but add
>something about my "perception" giving me a clue about which you think
>I am.

You introduced the word perception in "Or maybe it is just my perception."

>I suppose it must be comfortable to always be Right.

Thanks for the flattery.
```

- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <29n2q3p0caoh922pmsenajrl1niln3j4m3@4ax.com> <fns89c$pde$1@reader2.panix.com> <1u65q3l1v0rcriqpa82bvev2p8j9hiqcdq@4ax.com> <fnuse4$6np$1@reader2.panix.com> <p4d6q3hg7qlidtj2sc556ttnkqtcduq5ki@4ax.com> <60hp9jF1qt2isU1@mid.individual.net> <5sj7q3tm90cp7t9i1e95oie89ccn65iacc@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:5sj7q3tm90cp7t9i1e95oie89ccn65iacc@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 2 Feb 2008 12:45:55 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> Obtuse: dense: slow to learn or understand; lacking intellectual acuity.
It has other overtones besides those. It can also mean obstructive or 
deliberately indirect. NOT sharp,.pointed or acute

The Free Dictionary gives one meaning as: "Characterized by a lack of 
intelligence or sensitivity: an obtuse remark" (or action...)

Meriam-Webster gives: "lacking sharpness or quickness of sensibility or 
intellect" and gives "insensitive" and "stupid" as synonyms.

I think that covers it pretty well, at least in the context in which I used 
it :-).

>Who are you
> calling obtuse?

Someone who thinks it is OK to drive home to consult a manual when they 
could easily take it to work or ensure that the workplace obtain one...

Not a sensitive or supportive action, in my opinion, and so I wouldn't 
tolerate it. (If they charged the drive time against my project, I would not 
approve the time sheet, either...)

Pete.
```

```
On Sun, 3 Feb 2008 02:16:25 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> wrote:

>>>> I worked at place with such a policy in 2001. When I needed to look
>>>> something up in a
>>>> manual, I drove home to do it. Time per lookup was 30-45 minutes.

>>>If I thought anyone on my team was beng that obtuse, they wouldn't be on 
>>>it
>>>for very long. :-)

>>Who are you
>> calling obtuse?
>
>Someone who thinks it is OK to drive home to consult a manual when they 
>could easily take it to work or ensure that the workplace obtain one...

The manuals were available ONLY on the Web. 

To make them available in workplace lacking Web access, you wouldn't print them on paper,
you'd put them on a server accessible through the intranet. Doing so requires management
approvals. Another solution would be to put them on CDs, but that would limit access to
one person. At that place, I HAD all the Oracle manuals on a CD, but did not have Micro
Focus bacause I seldom need them.

>Not a sensitive or supportive action, in my opinion, and so I wouldn't 
>tolerate it. (If they charged the drive time against my project, I would not 
>approve the time sheet, either...)

Support is reciprocal. Denying access to the tools people need to do their job is not
supportive.
```

```
In addition to Robert's manual reference, you might want to see:
     "Using a multiple-row FETCH statement with host variable arrays" at
   http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/dsnapk10/2.2.2.4.1

 and

       "Declaring host variable arrays in COBOL" at
http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/dsnapk10/2.4.3.6

If it matters, the latter does NOT (seem to me) to allow for OCCURS DEPENDING 
ON.

and

the FETCH syntax information at:
  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/dsnsqk10/5.65
```

```
<0LGoj.23675$m_6.612@fe01.news.easynews.com>, William M.
Klein<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

Linux.  We have DB2 for LUW 9.5 running on zSeries Linux.

We are using DB2/VSE as the client, but that's not really relevant for this
discussion.  The only issue is that DB2 for LUW does not support this kind
of 'multi-row fetch'.  (Even if LUW did support it I doubt VSE would,
but...)

Frank
```

- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <87l5q3pd85q64e27c24gopoulmqobforto@4ax.com> <0LGoj.23675$m_6.612@fe01.news.easynews.com> <47A42C57.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
Frank Swarbrick <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote:

[snip]

>Linux.  We have DB2 for LUW 9.5 running on zSeries Linux.

All right, now I have to ask... how has this acronym changed since the

the reason one issued a SYNCPOINT/COMMIT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-04T11:00:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<47A6F043.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <87l5q3pd85q64e27c24gopoulmqobforto@4ax.com> <0LGoj.23675$m_6.612@fe01.news.easynews.com> <47A42C57.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <fo35qs$bn1$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```
>>> On 2/2/2008 at 6:36 PM, in message <fo35qs$bn1$1@reader2.panix.com>,
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:
> In article <47A42C57.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>,
> Frank Swarbrick <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> the reason one issued a SYNCPOINT/COMMIT.

LUW does of course still mean 'Logical Unit of Work'.  In this case, though,
it means 'Linux, UNIX, and Windows'.  As in "DB2 for Linux, UNIX, and
Windows".  Formerly known as "DB2 Universal Database for Linux, UNIX, and
Windows".

Basically, there are four 'code bases' for DB2.

DB2 for z/OS
DB2 for Linux, UNIX, and Windows
DB2 for i5/OS
DB2 Server for VSE and VM (redheaded step-child, even though it was the
predecessor of all four (as SQL/DS))

Frank
```

```
magazine which pointed out that in a previous issue page (m) used acronym 
'ABC' to indicate one thing while page (n) used the same acronym to 
indicate something else; the editor responded that this kind of 
multiple-interpretations-per-acronym might indicate how the subject-matter 
at hand (computers) was still relatively new.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with tables

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-02-02T20:31:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2ra9q31kvbjgqivcb6o4avirc874lkhabm@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<47A053E0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <jof2q31shvbu6d9ia1i7gelptabq9slahe@4ax.com> <47A1B75A.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <c3p4q3tji85k6tegudomp2k4f4bmtk744o@4ax.com> <47A20516.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <kn65q3pual2uooc5j7skq4vm1mvatp4imq@4ax.com> <47A42B97.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
On Sat, 2 Feb 2008 08:36:39 -0700, "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
wrote:

>>>> On 1/31/2008 at 9:10 PM, in message
><kn65q3pual2uooc5j7skq4vm1mvatp4imq@4ax.com>, Robert<no@e.mail> wrote:
…[73 more quoted lines elided]…
>but not DB2 for Linux, Unix and Windows!

As a last resort, I looked in the DB2 For Linux manuals and found you are correct.

"Arrays are not supported by the COBOL precompiler."

"Scrollable cursors are supported only in CLI, JDBC, and SQLJ applications."

ftp://ftp.software.ibm.com/ps/products/db2/info/vr82/pdf/en_US/db2a1e81.pdf

"The SELECT INTO statement produces a result table consisting of at most one row,
and assigns the values in that row to host variables."
ftp://ftp.software.ibm.com/ps/products/db2/info/vr82/pdf/en_US/db2s2e81.pdf

"DB2 Call Level Interface (CLI) versus embedded dynamic SQL

DB2 CLI provides enhanced parameter input and fetching capability, allowing arrays of data
to be specified on input, retrieving multiple rows of a result set directly into an array,
and executing statements that generate multiple result sets."
ftp://ftp.software.ibm.com/ps/products/db2/info/vr82/pdf/en_US/db2l1e81.pdf

I believe you said CLI was the only way. A way to get CLI functionality without low level
calls to CLI is IBM OLE DB Provider.
```

```
On Thu, 31 Jan 2008 20:41:29 -0800 (PST), Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>On Feb 1, 4:23 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>> On Thu, 31 Jan 2008 11:47:51 -0800 (PST), Richard <rip...@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>correcting you is an 'insult'.

No, I think name calling is insulting.

>> >With Fujitsu Cobol the DLOAD directive tells the system that CALLs
>> >might be dynamic. In these cases there is no need for the library
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>then it is not 'dynamic' as was required by Charles.

You are confusing people with the term 'static linking'. Fujitsu's terminology is pretty
good:

static linking                           Old School, everything in one executable
dynamic program structure   Unix style libraries linked with -l
dynamic link structure           Cobol style Load On Demand

>> If any cannot
>> be found, the program will not start, which guarantees the program will not abort mid-run
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>for the program to abort.

True. How many programs have a graceful fallback? Ok, if called from a menu, they say
'Function not available'. Makes you wonder why they didn't grey it out.

>If there is a requirement to ensure that the libraries do exist at
>start of run, then yes, you can statically link them, or do an initial
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>The requirement is to have dynamic loading.

Why is Cobol the only language with a pressing need for dynamic linking? Huge bodies of
code written in C and other languages get by without it.

>As Charles discovered, when you link in a shared library it prevents
>the CANCEL working. I tested this by taking the working set of
>Charles's programs and adding a -l to the ld.  The test then showed
>the CANCEL did not work. Removing the -l and retesting showed the CALL
>and CANCEL to work correctly.

The NETCobol for Linux User's Guide says the same in Chapter 7.

>> Cobol can, and I believe should, use the normal Unix binding mechanism. There are several
>> advantages and no good reason not to.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>entirely valid.

CANCEL was created in the days of limited memory, same as overlays. Cobol dropped overlays
when they were no longer needed. It should have dropped CANCEL at the same time.

>> The problem with dynamic binding in Cobol is that Cobol has no concept of library FILE
>> NAME, it only knows about entry point names, which are essentially the same as program
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>It is not a problem to me at all, nor to any of my clients.

It complicates deployment, makes the system less reliable and slows execution.

>> It would be cleaner to
>> package his hundreds of programs into one or a few libraries that are linked to the main
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>advantages it bestows on the system.

The advantage disappeared when we broke the 640K barrier.

>> >As you should be able to tell from the results in charles' 2nd message
>> >your BAD and WRONG advice has meant that his program does not work as
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Charles results and my testing show that you are WONG once again.

I said IF Cobol issues a dlclose().  Fujitsu does not. It could.

>Instead of simply assumnming that what you 'know' is everything that
>is possible to know you should actually try testing stuff out.

Fair enough. If this gets any deeper, I'll install Fujitsu for Linux.

>> If it is packaged in a library with a hundred other programs, the whole library might be
>> unloaded, because the handle for NAME is the handle for libMYSUBS.so. Or Fujitsu might be
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>code will be running.

I reload with four keystrokes: esc, ctrl-P, Enter.

>> Incidentally, CANCEL is not guaranteed to unload the program your way, either. A
>> dynamically loaded library can only be unloaded when its user count is zero. If another
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>library has to be loaded.

That's not under the control of Unix. If Fujitsu does that, why doesn't it free the data
area of a dynamic program structure the same way?

>In fact I just ran another test.  I started one program until it
>CALLed the subroutine. I then on another console changed the source to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>exactly as _I_ expected.

That's not normal Unix behavior. I'd need hands-on to figure out why it loaded a second
copy.

>Your grasp of this is tenuous at best. Probably this is because you've
>never done it the right way.

My grasp is based on writing large make files and knowledge of Unix internals.

>> If you want to be hackerish, issue the CANCEL 100 times. Each
>> call to dlopen() subtracts 1 from the user count.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>at all.

Unix doesn't know who is connected to a shared library. All it knows is the user count.
Perhaps Fujitsu ignores CANCEL on a program that doesn't have a data segment.

>The second problem is that a dll in memory will be used in preference
>to loading from disk. The FreeLibrary may make it disappear from
>memory, but that is irrelevant to this topic.

Not if the dll is 'bound'.
```
