```
On Sat, 9 Feb 2008 16:49:34 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
>news:mjqpq396kr51o4ki7h4263c18srvujbr1b@4ax.com...
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
>with some of the management you guys seem to encounter, either. :-)

It doesn't come from management, it comes from lead programmers. When asked for a copy of
the shop standard, they say 'it's around somewhere, I'll have to find it.' It's obvious
the question hasn't been asked in years. After some digging, they find one written in the
'80s. To their chagrin, they discover it doesn't prescribe half the de facto practices
they thought it did. 

In some shops they hurriedly 'modernize' the standard to describe the obsolete practices
they wish to enforce, which sparks arguments between them. For example, one sees nothing
wrong with ODO while another thinks it's not Old Style enough. In other shops they simply
say "Use our existing programs as a model. Make it look like them."  They don't say
whether they mean only syntax limitations or also crippled logic. The answer is they want
mediocre logic too. Good logic written in Old Style syntax sets off their defenses. 

>> -----------------------------------------
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>scars to prove it... :-))

The worst offenders do not post on Usenet because they know their reactionary practices
would not stand up to scrutiny. Being ridiculed is their greatest fear. No one wants to be
caught in a lie. 

>> Defenders of Old Cobol in the real world are even more attuned to imagined 
>> threat. Their
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>their noses rubbed in their own inefficiency.

Especially when they know it's inefficient, but don't know what else to do.

> Attitude and corporate culture 
>have a lot to do with this. My experience is that new ideas CAN be 
>introduced, provided it is done tactfully and not forcefully or 
>superciliously.

My experience is management doesn't have patience to foster and wait for internal growth,
as it did in the past. Their quick fix is to outsource the application, which means
getting rid of the old timers. The outsource company will bring new technology as well as
providing immediate expense reduction. The change is forceful, not tactful.  Evidence of
that is the success of SAP, PeopleSoft, Lawson, et al.

Every contracting company calls itself a Solution Provider. Now, some of them actually can
provide plug-in solutions to common problems. 

>As for standards, they can be used to beat people about the head and neck, 
>or they can be used to encourage a certain style of coding that makes life 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>for certain constructs.

Reasonable people have left the Cobol scene, moved on to other languages. What's left are
turf defenders and neophytes from other programming cultures who are slumming for money.
Turf defenders run the show because management doesn't want to get involved and neophytes
have no opinion. 

>It was amazing how quickly people started conforming...:-)

I found the same, when I was writing good standards. 

>In later years when I went contracting and traveled all over, I saw many 
>sets of installation standards for COBOL. Some of them were like hundreds of 
>"Thou Shalts..." and "Thou Shalt nots", enforced by Nazis, others were just 
>general guidelines. It depended on the corporate culture and the local 
>management.

Nazis are the only ones remaining. (Oh no, is that a Godwin?)
```

On Fri, 1 Feb 2008 15:55:02 -0600, "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote:
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>square peg in a round hole.

Old-timers didn't think along those lines. They thought it was just another attack by New
Ideas, like the ones they had successfully fought off before. They thought 'this too will
pass.'

> I always knew that one of COBOL's greatest
>strengths was it's simplicity.

Cobol is more complex than other third generation languages such as C and Basic.

> Add OO, destroy the simplicity. 

The power of OO is not in the SYNTAX of the language. Java has almost the same syntax as
C. The power is in class libraries, which broaden the LEXICON of the language. The
programmer is no longer limited to the NOUNS and VERBS defined by the language standard.
Objects are the new nouns and methods are the new verbs. 

Cobol could have used the broadened lexicon as easily as any other language. 

I don't thnk the change of word order had any significance. Cobol is verb-initial; OO
languages are object-initial. So what? Either word order could have used the expanded
vocabulary.

>The things
>that make COBOL great for its heyday are also things that do not fit
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>XML, et al, those became irrelevant. 

XML doesn't write source code. The structures still must be expressed in the language's
terms in order for a program to access them. In Java, that means structs and typedefs.
Doesn't it? Am I missing something?

>Consider Pete Dashwood. He embraced
>and championed OO COBOL diligently for years. But Pete has abandoned
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>maybe even sooner. 

I disagree. <Assuming Rocky voice> I coulda bin a contenda.

Old timers had gravitas. They held the high ground. Losing it took a concerted screwup.

>Their openness to change would have propelled them
>inevitably to the same objective conclusions about current development
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>than I do.

If Microsoft had purchased Realia Cobol, as they tried to in the early '90s, we'd now have
.NETCobol alongside C# and VB, all supported by the same killer IDE.
```

On Feb 3, 4:10 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:

> If Microsoft had purchased Realia Cobol, as they tried to in the early '90s, we'd now have
> .NETCobol alongside C# and VB, all supported by the same killer IDE.

Microsoft had its own Cobol in the late 70s and early 80s. It also had
its own Pascal. Later it distributed versions of MF Cobol as its own
brand.

It dumped Pascal, it dumped Cobol. It has bought many products and
dumped them, sometimes just to remove them from being competition.

What guarantees do you have that MS would still be developing and
supporting Cobol if it had bought Realia ?

In any case MF and Fujitsu both have .NET Cobols.

>On Feb 3, 4:10 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:

…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

MS has been relatively unsuccessful in the corporate world, now dominated by  mainframes
and Unix. Cobol would make MS a more attractive choice. 

"Lombard, based in Toronto, is in the midst of a company-wide initiative to migrate
approximately 15 to 20 applications off z/OS to the Windows Server 2003 operating system
and SQL Server 2000. The company estimates the move will save it over $1 million a year as
the software licensing slice of its overall IT budget will drop from 12% to 2-4%.

For the Lombard migration, Micro Focus International Ltd. chipped in with software that
allowed Lombard to re-host mainframe applications on new boxes. And though the company is
working closely with Microsoft, Micro Focus is not hell-bent on driving customers away
from the mainframe, said Mike Gilbert, Micro Focus' vice president of marketing and
director of product strategy. It's all about choice, he said, and besides, Micro Focus
knows that most large mainframe customers aren't migrating anytime soon.

"Our position is not to drive people away from the mainframe, but to offer greater choice
to run mission-critical applications on," Gilbert said. "Microsoft's mission is to get as
many people on Windows as possible. ï¿½

"In the short term, the whole kit and caboodle is not realistic," said Microsoft's
Lenaghan. "But if you look at customers like Lombard, that's the sweet spot. ï¿½ It's a
strategy that's evolving and over time as our credibility grows the opportunities open up
for the large glasshouse with Microsoft technology."

"Over time, we're going to go after everything," Lenaghan added. 
http://searchdatacenter.techtarget.com/news/article/0,289142,sid80_gci1213548,00.html

I'm talking about this.

Business Wire,  June 30, 2004  

TEANECK, N.J. -- Cognizant (Nasdaq: CTSH), a leading global provider of IT services, has
joined Microsoft Corp's Mainframe Migration Alliance to help legacy customers seamlessly
migrate critical data and systems to Microsoft .NET-based systems.

Cognizant supports IT organizations seeking to redeploy legacy applications with proven
methodologies and tools for modernization based on the .NET Framework. "We're very excited
to enter this alliance with Microsoft, which broadens our ability to maximize customer
value and ensure a smooth transition to the Microsoft platform," said Gajen Kandiah, vice
president of Cognizant's Advanced Solutions Group. "Many of our larger customers are
looking to modernize their systems infrastructure by integrating with, or migrating from,
the mainframe. Our alliance with Microsoft ensures we bring the world-class capabilities
of both of our companies to our mutual customers."

Cognizant utilizes Microsoft .NET best practices based on its extensive application
development, integration, and support expertise on Microsoft .NET. Cognizant has developed
a legacy migration modernization methodology called ImpliNET, which is a rapid, structured
process that enables organizations to quickly move to the benefits of the Microsoft
platform and .NET technologies while still running their existing businesses. ImpliNET has
four key stages: Modernization Analysis, Modernization Definition, Modernization
Execution, and Modernization Go-Live. Each of these stages is optimized for Microsoft .NET
and leverages reusable frameworks, tools, and methods to streamline the modernization
process.


Cognizant's more than 11,700 employees are committed to partnerships that sustain
long-term, proven value for customers by providing high-quality, cost-effective solutions
through its development centers in India, and onsite client teams. Cognizant maintains
P-CMM and CMMi Level 5 assessments from an independent third-party assessor and was
recently ranked #1 in Forbes' Hot Shots 200 Up & Comers and BusinessWeek ranks Cognizant
as the top information technology company in their Hot Growth Companies and a top five IT
service provider globally. Further information about Cognizant can be found at
http://www.cognizant.com.
http://findarticles.com/p/articles/mi_m0EIN/is_2004_June_30/ai_n6089732

 Data migration and application conversion technologies provider MOVE2OPEN (m2o) has
announced that it has joined Microsoft Corporation`s Mainframe Migration Alliance (MMA).

MMA is a group of companies working together to help customers migrate workloads off of
the mainframe and onto the Microsoft platform. The MMA's goal is reportedly to help
customers reduce the cost of maintaining and modernizing their mainframe environments.

The MMA alliance is now expanding to include 11 new partners, among them m2o, bringing the
total MMA membership to 17 partners.
http://findarticles.com/p/articles/mi_m0ECZ/is_2004_Oct_4/ai_n6283149

MMA partners:

Fujitsu Systems
Hewlett-Packard
Unisys
Accenture
EDS
Micro Focus

Success stories:

"For Microsoft, success, at least for now, means grabbing the "low-hanging fruit." The
company plans to pluck customers from companies with small numbers of MIPS. The company
hopes this will establish a firm credibility base among zSeries users, which it can build
on.

According to Charles King, principal analyst for Hayward, Calif.-based Pund IT Research,
companies running at 200 MIPS and under are the "sweet spot" for the Microsoft, and there
are solutions in the Intel and x86 space that can offer near-mainframe performance. 

"[Microsoft] has some low-hanging fruit they can pick ï¿½ but to say that [x86] solutions
are every bit as good as a mainframe is just marketing hype," King said. "They might be
closer than in the past, but they still not coming close. ï¿½ "

On Tue, 05 Feb 2008 12:05:16 GMT, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>And do you think the larger corporations are still using real 3270 interfaces - 
>or Unix/Linux machines to run on?  You tell me what you think what "terminals" 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>those applications (if they weren't "pure batch").?

Ten years ago, the user interface was on Windows PCs (or Citrix) running VB or C++ using
MFC , COM or other proprietary Microsoft. 

The user interface is NOW on Windows PCs running a web browser. They could easily be
replaced by Unix PCs running a web browser. Users are no longer locked into Microsoft

>When you reply, remember my original statement included,
>  "It may (or may not) be true that the "back end" applications  often run on 
>mainframes and or Unix/Linux servers, but that hardly means that MF  has been 
>unsuccessful in that environment."

Microsoft isn't content with being a 'terminal company'; it wants to put Windows on back
end machines. IBM is vulnerable because a large portion of its revenues come from software
licenses rather than hardware. An end-user company can significantly reduce expenses by
replacing z/OS with either Windows or Linux. The reason WHY IBM promotes Linux is to fight
off Microsoft.
```

```
"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote:
>
> Microsoft isn't content with being a 'terminal company'; it wants to put Windows on back
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> off Microsoft.

All this talk of replacing mainframe OSs with Windows or Linux gives me
pause. One reason why Windows is so plagued by virus and other security
threats is that most of the PCs on the planet run Windows, which means
most of the hackers have Windows "in their living rooms" so to speak, on
which to develop such attacks. Mainframe OSs have been far less prone
to similar attacks, in part because of better security, but also because few
hackers have access to mainframe OSs on which to design and test their
attacks. If mainframe OSs are replaced by Windows and Linux, that will
no longer be an issue.
```

Splitting computing between database machines, servers, and terminals
can make security much easier.    Such isolation is more complete when
they really don't talk each languages natively - but have limited
things they can say to each other.   XML, SQL, etc are easier to
monitor, and need translation to the other's computer's tools.

>If mainframe OSs are replaced by Windows and Linux, that will
>no longer be an issue.

I don't understand this statement - What will no longer be an issue?

telephone networks run on Unix. We don't get hacked any more than mainframes, which is
very seldom.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW's statement - what am I missing (was: help with tables

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-02-05T11:53:34-08:00

- **References:** `<90aaq3lg86q0av59580abcvl3c8q1vd5kj@4ax.com> <54265709-a1ad-4a7a-bbb5-0468d669c6a1@k39g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <6kncq31fkqe0lo074lbfdiaom24lgo82u1@4ax.com> <J4Npj.106756$1x.26836@fe05.news.easynews.com> <4sjfq3lvrlvm2sj63pk29u4p7hgdt4u0q4@4ax.com> <IrTpj.50640$m_6.35398@fe01.news.easynews.com> <pv1gq351foe9qle4s7ovagae0qi788porj@4ax.com> <0iYpj.69316$9w2.37855@fe12.news.easynews.com> <mlpgq35vdgrb6u3umvkvgfdomagpr38q6n@4ax.com> <7a_pj.60795$k27.27331@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <d7ahq31tvdbufq8dpej665u2952mi55gi7@4ax.com>`

```
On Feb 6, 7:33 am, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Tue, 5 Feb 2008 08:13:24 -0600, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> >"Robert" <n...@e.mail> wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> In my opinion, the perception that z/OS is more secure than Windows is marketing BS.

Windows was designed to give 'a great user experience'. For example
opening an email in MS Outlook Express with a MS Word .doc could
automatically open MS Word. Macros in that doc could be automatically
run. Macros have a rich feature set. This makes it _so_easy_ and
convenient for the user. It also makes it easy and convenient for the
virus writer.

For (an older) example, setting up a netbios network exposed port 139
to make it easy for other users to find your shares. Connecting to the
internet without a firewall exposed that port to the world.

Unix, with its multi-user and acadaemic background (and its basis in
Multics) never went down the path of making it easy to do silly
things.

Mainframes never made it easy to do anything.

The reality that Windows _is_ less secure is _because_ of "marketing
BS", the marketing of 'so easy'.


> It
> is comparing apples with oranges, professionally managed back-end machines versus desktop
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> very seldom.

You don't run IE, Outlook Express, or MS Word on those machines.
```

On Tue, 5 Feb 2008 11:53:34 -0800 (PST), Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>On Feb 6, 7:33 am, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:

>You don't run IE, Outlook Express, or MS Word on those machines.

You are right. Neither would a Windows back-end server be running consumer software.

If Windows is so easy to hack, why aren't millions of corporate desktop machines a
security risk? They run IE, Outlook and Word. They have access to servers. Developers have
LOTS of access. 

Desktops are secure because they're protected by firewalls and virus scanners. The only
way to connect to them from the outside is via VPN. Servers have additional protection
from desktops, such as SecurID.

On Tue, 05 Feb 2008 17:41:58 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:

…[62 more quoted lines elided]…

As usual the devil is in the details.  How locked down is the Internet
access?  How locked down is the computer from upgrade?  Is there a USB
port on the desktop and what can be hooked to it?  Can CDs be read? Is
autoplay enabled?  Is boot from CD enabled?  What privileges is the
user allowed to boot up with?  Corporate networks are all over the lot
on this.  

I will agree that on the server side Microsoft proves a fair amount of
logging and I have been impressed with the information that I have
seen available for fixes that I have applied to my home systems.  I
also believe that many shops do not take advantage of the vast amount
of information their operating system(s) vendor(s) provide.  If the
complaints I read on bit.listserv.ibm-main are any indication, the
background and training of IBM mainframe people (and probably those of
other similar vendors) is to pay attention to these issues while their
PC counterparts are not.  My experience with Windows XP Home is that
some of the functions that should be in the all users, always
available, configurable only by admins are not.  When I log off
Webroot spysweeper shuts down and I "think" Zone Alarm Internet
Security might.  I do know that both are fired up after logon rather
than as a part of the boot process.  I know that on the IBM mainframe
and at least the HP system, security can be by user.  I also can
believe that security administration is a full time headache
regardless of operating environment.  I suspect that the varying
flavors of modern Unix as well as the long time mainframe systems from
various vendors are more armored and secure than Windows in part due
to different compromises in the design.  I do not envy people in small
shops regardless of system because there is so much to do and consider
in this area.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT security on mainframe versus that on Windows was Re: RW's statement - what am I missing (was: help with tables

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-02-06T16:43:09-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f6ckq3potti99s4dihiki59oiqedessqkp@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<IrTpj.50640$m_6.35398@fe01.news.easynews.com> <pv1gq351foe9qle4s7ovagae0qi788porj@4ax.com> <0iYpj.69316$9w2.37855@fe12.news.easynews.com> <mlpgq35vdgrb6u3umvkvgfdomagpr38q6n@4ax.com> <7a_pj.60795$k27.27331@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <d7ahq31tvdbufq8dpej665u2952mi55gi7@4ax.com> <bcc08afd-0f19-41c4-a6d8-a226902873f6@l16g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <edphq3lffm93i8clv1sojlg8qhmfjj6mq7@4ax.com> <95sjq312ed81q3sth6atchfi2kreocpm9o@4ax.com>`

```
On Wed, 06 Feb 2008 13:56:01 -0400, Clark F Morris <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote:


>How locked down is the Internet
>access?  How locked down is the computer from upgrade?  Is there a USB
>port on the desktop and what can be hooked to it?  Can CDs be read? Is
>autoplay enabled?  Is boot from CD enabled?  

Imagine a virus that completely disabled your Web browser. That would be some nasty
malware that no one would tolerate. Same for a virus that killed your USB devices or CD
drive. It would be stomped out within a week. 

Suppose the virus infected spread to every machine in the company, and was impossible to
remove. Frantic calls would go out to Symantec and McAfee.

Want to mount a denial of service attack? Don't bother writing viruses, sending in a
short-sighted administrator will cripple them more, without you having to lift a finger.
```

- **References:** `<IrTpj.50640$m_6.35398@fe01.news.easynews.com> <pv1gq351foe9qle4s7ovagae0qi788porj@4ax.com> <0iYpj.69316$9w2.37855@fe12.news.easynews.com> <mlpgq35vdgrb6u3umvkvgfdomagpr38q6n@4ax.com> <7a_pj.60795$k27.27331@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <d7ahq31tvdbufq8dpej665u2952mi55gi7@4ax.com> <bcc08afd-0f19-41c4-a6d8-a226902873f6@l16g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <edphq3lffm93i8clv1sojlg8qhmfjj6mq7@4ax.com> <95sjq312ed81q3sth6atchfi2kreocpm9o@4ax.com> <f6ckq3potti99s4dihiki59oiqedessqkp@4ax.com>`

```
On Feb 7, 11:43 am, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Wed, 06 Feb 2008 13:56:01 -0400, Clark F Morris <cfmpub...@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> short-sighted administrator will cripple them more, without you having to lift a finger.

Imagine a virus that was designed to never be noticed.

Many Windows viruses these days try to hide themselves, preferably
with rootkits, so that they can be used as spyware or as zombies on
the botnets.  When Windows is inactive the zombie will call home and
receive instructions. These are the source of most spam email these
days. Each botnet could run to millions of Windows machines, including
corporates.

The users have no idea that the spyware or botnet resides on the
machine and thus fail to try to get rid of it.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<5c6nq3tn8v6h5bkt59gufoihitapi5q9gi@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<0iYpj.69316$9w2.37855@fe12.news.easynews.com> <mlpgq35vdgrb6u3umvkvgfdomagpr38q6n@4ax.com> <7a_pj.60795$k27.27331@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <d7ahq31tvdbufq8dpej665u2952mi55gi7@4ax.com> <bcc08afd-0f19-41c4-a6d8-a226902873f6@l16g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <edphq3lffm93i8clv1sojlg8qhmfjj6mq7@4ax.com> <95sjq312ed81q3sth6atchfi2kreocpm9o@4ax.com> <f6ckq3potti99s4dihiki59oiqedessqkp@4ax.com> <544aeec3-6901-4236-9ddb-fa6f7c15727d@i12g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Wed, 6 Feb 2008 15:55:32 -0800 (PST), Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>On Feb 7, 11:43 am, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>> On Wed, 06 Feb 2008 13:56:01 -0400, Clark F Morris <cfmpub...@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>machine and thus fail to try to get rid of it.

All true.  Users cannot get rid of botnet viruses for two reasons:

.. Smart viruses detect that you're running a virus scanner and fool it with clean disk
segments. The scanner appears to be running normally and not finding anything. 

.. Virus scanners look for known patterns in files and in memory. Some viruses have no
identifying pattern because each copy is encrypted with a different key. 

Valentine's Day is the best day of the year for bad guys. They will send hundreds of
millions of Valentine emails with links to LEGITIMATE electronic card sites. If you click
the link, you'll be redirected to their site, which will install a dozen viruses.

Bad guys are not teenaged hackers; they are professional criminals. They have their own
communication protocol and encryption algorithm, dozens of servers, tens of millions of
bots. Last year they took down monster.com, Bank of India, Akami, and a primary DNS server
run by Dept of Defense. They can send well over a billion bits per second at a server, and
do it to several servers simultaneously. Nearly all email spam comes from their bots, not
their own machines. Their server cannot be traced or tapped because its IP address changes
every second.
```

Every corporate LAN I've ever worked on was connecting MicroSoft Windows 
based workstations.

Besides, King is wrong in his assessment. There ARE [x86] solutions that are

corporations who have replaced their mainframes (Unisys and IBM) with blade 
multi-processors, for a fraction of the mainframe cost and no decrease in 
processing power. Whether these companies are part of King's "low hanging 
fruit" is irrelevant. If all the fruit is hanging low, that's where the 
revenue is... :-)

Pete.

"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:4oc6q3d8r1acm34uj2rf24mpo244djrq5n@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 1 Feb 2008 10:43:08 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> abandoned Cobol. Note past tense; it already happened.

Yes, it did. And the resistance of the COBOL community to new ideas was 
certainly a major factor. However, it wasn't the ONLY factor, and many who 
were previously resistant are now aware that certan nettles will have to be 
grasped and bullets bitten anyway, with or without COBOL.

I have spotted a few things recently which make me think that COBOL may be 
twitching for a while yet. Too soon for optimism, but a "resurrection" of 
COBOL may not be out of the question. If it happens (and the chances are 
slight in my opinion) it will require an attitude shift on the part of many 
in the COBOL community (Managers, Analaysts and Programmers). However, that 
shift is not as unlikely today as it was 10 years ago...

Time will tell. :-)

Pete.
```

On Thu, 31 Jan 2008 11:54:52 -0600, "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:

>In view of this, I just spent half an hour scouring my library and actually
>found my authority!
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>PL

The book you mention is rhetorically excellent, factually poor. Filled
with numerous errors, mistatements and outright lies - a good looking
but hollow condemnation of the Kensington Stone.

Better to read these books by Robert Hall:
---The Kensington Rune-Stone Is Genuine, Columbia SC Hornbeam Press,
1982.  The first book I read on the subject, presents several
excellent arguments for authenticity. Or...
---The Kensington Rune-Stone, Authentic and Important, Lake Bluff IL,
Jupiter Press, 1994
A follow up to the original containing much of the same material, but
adds some additional evidence, including a strong criticism of
Whalgren's work.


>
>
…[101 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"It's a beautiful day for a night game."
--Baseball Radio Announcer Frankie Frisch
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve

> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

Nope - it just a simple logical deduction.  Adam was created as an 
adult; therefore, it makes perfect sense that the rest of creation (AKA 
"the world") was also created as mature, and is "older" than 6,000 
years, even though it may have only actually *been* around ~6,000 years.
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> Howard Brazee wrote:
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> *been* around ~6,000 years.

True. Assuming God did create the universe in 6 days, which is more
logical?

A. God created everything without the "implied prehistory" you suggest,
and Adam couldn't see a single star for 4 years, etc.

B. God created everything with an "implied prehistory", with everything
already in balance "as if" it had been there. There would be chickens,
eggs, and everything in between. Trees would have rings. Adam could see
all stars bright enough to be visible.

To my mind, assumption A is illogical, and criticizing the Creation
account on that basis is asinine. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 62)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-04T15:51:14-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> will be punished as part of a grand plan.

Duh! God *told* us what He did in the Bible. No "fooling" there.

> A better alternative is to ask what is important to learn from the
> scriptures.   Where should one's efforts be best used - to prove
> ourselves Right?   Or to follow Jesus' example?

What is important to learn from the Scriptures is what the Scriptures
actually say. The reason Jesus came into the world was to die in
payment for our sins (1 Timothy 1:15), not to be an example. Jesus
lived a perfect life *because* He did everything *right*. He followed
perfectly every principle and law of God, the Father. There is no
"follow Jesus example" apart from "being right"; that's an oxymoron.

Even so, no one is saved by their actions. Everyone has "fallen short"
of what God demands (Romans 3:21-23), and nobody is acceptable to God
except through faith in Jesus (Acts 4:10-12). Jesus is the only Man
Who ever lived a life acceptable to God, and no other human will ever
see heaven, apart from accepting God's grace through believing in the
atoning work of Jesus Christ. Our work is to accept God's plan of
salvation (John 6:29). We obey God to please Him *because* of what He
has done for us, and also because of Who He is.
```

those who claim to be prophets and messiahs and therefore anyone
setting themselves up as a prophet/messiah are unlikely to live a full
and useful life.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 65)_

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-05T08:27:30-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13qgsib4tom1f5c@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

>
> The question isn't "who died" but "who died, rose again and ascended
> bodily into Heaven?" Only been one of those. :-)

Uh, not exactly. Of the ~34 miracles attributed to Jesus (including - sort 
of - resurrection), He was not the first. All had predecessors or one kind 
or another.

Some, admittedly, are only similar. Jesus walked on water, Moses moved the 
water out of the way so the Israelites could walk on the mud. Heck, even 
mere mortals can match some of Jesus' spectaculars (such as reanimating the 
dead or restoring sight).

Regarding "ascending bodily into Heaven," I refer you to Enoch, who didn't 
even die before making the trip ("... and Enoch was no more for God took 
him...")

Anyway, that stuff is not terribly important. To the believer, Jesus was who 
He was because of who He was - not because of "miracles." God does not 
expect us to differentiate between miracles and magic. If "miracles" were 
THE test of divinity, we'd all be worshiping Siegfreud and Roy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 66)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-05T10:50:12-06:00

"HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> the mud. Heck, even mere mortals can match some of Jesus' spectaculars (such as reanimating the dead or restoring sight).

Next time you see someone raise a person who has been dead for
several days, and has begun to stink (John 11), let me know. :-)

However, believers in Jesus also sometimes "perform" miracles,
though the miracles are always done by the power of the Holy
Spirit, through faith in Jesus (John 14:12, Acts 3).

> Regarding "ascending bodily into Heaven," I refer you to Enoch, who didn't even die before making the trip ("... and Enoch was no 
> more for God took him...")

The Bible doesn't record the manner in which Enoch got from here into
heaven. To me, the phrase you quoted above, "was no more," implies that
Enoch vanished, but it doesn't specifically say. Elija was carried to
Heaven in a chariot of fire (2 Kings 2:11).

> Anyway, that stuff is not terribly important. To the believer, Jesus was who He was because of who He was - not because of 
> "miracles." God does not expect us to differentiate between miracles and magic. If "miracles" were THE test of divinity, we'd all 
> be worshiping Siegfreud and Roy.

I don't agree that it isn't important. I do agree that it isn't THE test
of divinity. Jesus fulfilled over 100 specific Old Testament prophecies
for the Messiah (e.g .virgin birth), and miracles only represent a few.
The fulfilled prophecies are the real proof.
```

```

Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:7t0qj.60879$k27.31684@bignews2.bellsouth.net...
> I don't agree that it isn't important. I do agree that it isn't THE test
> of divinity. Jesus fulfilled over 100 specific Old Testament prophecies
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Judson McClendon       judmc@sunvaley0.com (remove zero)


At least one of those prophecies was the one about going into Jerusalem on
an ass, specifically to fulfill the prophecy.  (You'll be able to tell us
where this is in the Bible).  The point is: if somebody deliberately acts
out a prophecy - is the prophecy fulfilled?  I'd say not.  Now being born of
a virgin birth - if that was prophesied then it would be a genuine
fulfillment, not to mention a miracle.

But the word used in the prophecy - almah - doesn't mean "virgin".  It means
maid, servant, or even concubine.  The Jerusalem Bible footnotes
specifically state that the original Hebrew uses the word for young
girl/young recently married woman.   Gen. 20:17: maidservants.  Gen 21:12:
bondswoman; Ex 23:12: handmaid.  (I don't have an example for concubine: I
only have some references, not a lexicon).  None of these three examples
require virginity.  The Greek version used the word "parthenos", taken to
mean virgin.  Perhaps somebdy that speaks Greek can confirm.

The slipperiness of "prophecy" is amply exemplified by Nostradamus' works.
Only a couple have a good claim to being fulfilled.

PL
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jG3qj.60968$k27.5952@bignews2.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <13qgsib4tom1f5c@corp.supernews.com> <7t0qj.60879$k27.31684@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <Li2qj.38944$Ft5.14067@newsfe15.lga>`

```
"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>
> At least one of those prophecies was the one about going into Jerusalem on
> an ass, specifically to fulfill the prophecy.  (You'll be able to tell us
> where this is in the Bible).

Zechariah 9:9

>  The point is: if somebody deliberately acts
> out a prophecy - is the prophecy fulfilled?  I'd say not.

I don't follow that reasoning. I understand how something a person
willingly does can be a fake, but it does not follow that not to be a
fake it cannot be knowingly done. Jesus was well aware that His
actions were fulfilling prophecy (Luke 4:16-21 note: His reading
was not the prophecy being fulfilled). How could He not? It does
not make sense, that the Son of God wouldn't know He was
fulfilling prophecy.

>  Now being born of
> a virgin birth - if that was prophesied then it would be a genuine
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> mean virgin.  Perhaps somebdy that speaks Greek can confirm.

The verse you refer to is Isaiah 7:14 (NKJV):

  "Therefore the Lord Himself will give you a sign: Behold, the virgin
  shall conceive and bear a Son, and shall call His name Immanuel."

I don't read Hebrew. My understanding, based on what has been written
by top Hebrew Bible scholars, is that the term is not precisely the
same word as "virgin" in English, in the sense that it is not referring
specifically to her not having had sex, but it is referring to a person
who, in the context of the customs and terminology of the day, would
be a virgin.

Anyway, just think about it. If this were not the case, the sentence
would have no meaning at all. What kind of "sign" is a "young woman"
conceiving a son and giving him a name, be it "Immanuel" (God is with
us), or anything else? Such names have always been common throughout
Jewish history.  In New Testament times, for example, there were lots
of people with the name "Jesus" (savior).
```

_(thread depth: 70)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-05T21:30:11-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7R9qj.61153$k27.5838@bignews2.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <13qgsib4tom1f5c@corp.supernews.com> <7t0qj.60879$k27.31684@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <Li2qj.38944$Ft5.14067@newsfe15.lga> <jG3qj.60968$k27.5952@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <e4lhq3lme3cdf4o5qfl5ope28sjok75t4c@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> nick-names back then?

Sure. Jesus called "Simon," "Peter" (Greek 'petros': small stone), and
named James and John, "Boanerges" which means "Sons of Thunder"
(Mark 3:16,17). "Jesus" is a slightly different form of "Joshua," a way
similar to "James" and "Jimmy."

The importance of the name "Immanuel," IMO, is that it means "God is
with us." In His life here, Jesus was, literally and physically, God with us.
So, Jesus' incarnation was a literal fulfillment of the name "Immanuel."

When the Bible speaks of God "giving a name" it has a far deeper
meaning and significance than our names have for us today. For God,
the name is the embodiment of whoever/whatever is being named. In
Philippians 2:9 where it says God (the Father) has given Jesus (the Son)
"a name which is above every name" it means Jesus has been given
authority and exaltation above all creation, which clear from the text.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 69)_

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-05T21:51:39-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13qibm3ecj72k61@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <13qgsib4tom1f5c@corp.supernews.com> <7t0qj.60879$k27.31684@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <Li2qj.38944$Ft5.14067@newsfe15.lga> <jG3qj.60968$k27.5952@bignews2.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>
> The verse you refer to is Isaiah 7:14 (NKJV):
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> day, would be a virgin.

I do read Hebrew. In the Hebrew text, the word is "Almah" and that word 
translates to "young woman," without a necessary connection to virginity. 
Hebrew has a perfectly good word for virgin, "bethula," and that word is 
found elsewhere in the Hebrew scriptures. Most scholars believe the writers 
of the New Testament were reading the Greek Septuagint, which, if you ponder 
on it, is kinda weird.

>
> Anyway, just think about it. If this were not the case, the sentence
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> of people with the name "Jesus" (savior).

The meaning of the sentence is ambiguous anyway. Isaiah IS giving a sign, 
that much is certain. The sign is either to clarify his message (see Ch 20) 
or to verify it (Judges 6.17-23). It is not clear whether the sign in 
question is the woman's pregnancy, the child's birth, his name, or his diet. 
Nor is it clear when the sign is supposed to occur - immediately (if the 
sign is the name), soon (birth), or a few years (food) into the future. 
Presumably the "sign" made sense to Ahaz (and, if Christian exegesis is 
correct, to the new Christians of the 1st and 2nd century).

Ambiguity is the coin of prophets.
```

- **References:** `<8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <13qgsib4tom1f5c@corp.supernews.com> <7t0qj.60879$k27.31684@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <Li2qj.38944$Ft5.14067@newsfe15.lga>`

```
On Tue, 5 Feb 2008 12:54:53 -0600, "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:

>
>Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>
From about 80 CE to the present time, most Christian faith groups have
taught that Jesus was conceived and born by his mother Mary, while she
was still a virgin. They believe that this happened by the action of
the Holy Spirit, without an act of sexual intercourse. 


Roman Catholicism has taught the doctrine of perpetual virginity --
that Mary lived, gave birth to Jesus, and remained a virgin through
her entire life.

Islam also teaches that Mary was a virgin when she conceived Jesus.

Some of the early leaders of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day
Saints -- the main Mormon denomination -- taught that God has a
physical body, and that he came down to earth, engaged in sexual
intercourse with Mary, and conceived Jesus. However, this was never
made an official church teaching and is rarely heard today, outside of
anti-Mormon groups.

The Anglican Communion, Eastern Orthodoxy, Protestantism and Roman
Catholicism have taught the "virgin birth," although the term "virgin
conception" would be much more accurate. This has long been one of the
Christianity's foundational beliefs, along with the inerrancy of the
Bible; the God's inspiration of the authors of the Bible; the
atonement, resurrection, and the anticipated second coming of Jesus,
etc. All of the commonly used major ancient church creeds have also
mentioned the virgin birth.

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"You can't kill your way to security, and you 
can't lead through scaring people."
--Bruce Springsteen
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
<snip>

> >
> >But the word used in the prophecy - almah - doesn't mean "virgin".  It
means
> >maid, servant, or even concubine.  The Jerusalem Bible footnotes
> >specifically state that the original Hebrew uses the word for young
> >girl/young recently married woman.   Gen. 20:17: maidservants.  Gen
21:12:
> >bondswoman; Ex 23:12: handmaid.  (I don't have an example for concubine:
I
> >only have some references, not a lexicon).  None of these three examples
> >require virginity.  The Greek version used the word "parthenos", taken to
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Who's arguing that?  The point is they all say that because of the prophecy.
And the prophecy, as written, doesn't specify "virgin".  Just like the
bandarlog: "We all say it so it must be so!"  80CE was long after any
eyewitnesses were alive; so there would be nobody that could question
anything that those in charge had to say.

Nobody has ever questioned that Jesus was born of Mary.

  Judson pointed out that there was no point in the prophecy if it doesn't
specify "virgin" because there's nothing special about a "young woman"
giving birth.  He's right.  Scrap the prophecy, then, because it's useless!

PL
```

On Tue, 5 Feb 2008 12:54:53 -0600, "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:

>
>Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>

And following up on my last post, Jesus was not fathered by any man,
since no man had been with Mary (Luke 1:34). And Joseph, Mary's
betrothed husband, at first struggled to accept this fact but later
accepted it (Matthew 1:19-25).

Also, Luke wrote in 1:26-38

26 Now in the sixth month the angel Gabriel was sent by God to a city
of Galilee named Nazareth,

27 to a virgin betrothed to a man whose name was Joseph, of the house
of David. The virgin's name was Mary.

28 And having come in, the angel said to her, "Rejoice, highly favored
one, the Lord is with you; blessed are you among women!"

29 But when she saw him, she was troubled at his saying, and
considered what manner of greeting this was.

30 Then the angel said to her, "Do not be afraid, Mary, for you have
found favor with God.

31 "And behold, you will conceive in your womb and bring forth a Son,
and shall call His name Jesus.

32 "He will be great, and will be called the Son of the Highest; and
the Lord God will give Him the throne of His father David.

33 "And He will reign over the house of Jacob forever, and of His
kingdom there will be no end."

34 Then Mary said to the angel, "How can this be, since I do not know
a man?"

35 And the angel answered and said to her, "The Holy Spirit will come
upon you, and the power of the Highest will overshadow you; therefore,
also, that Holy One who is to be born will be called the Son of God.

36 "Now indeed, Elizabeth your relative has also conceived a son in
her old age; and this is now the sixth month for her who was called
barren.

37 "For with God nothing will be impossible."

38 Then Mary said, "Behold the maidservant of the Lord! Let it be to
me according to your word." And the angel departed from her.  


Regards,

         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

can't lead through scaring people."

> On Tue, 5 Feb 2008 12:54:53 -0600, "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:

Steve, this does nothing for anyone who doesn't accept Holy Writ as being 
true.

You are quoting something that can only be believed by people who already 
believe and accept that particular authority.

Given that that is the case, a reference to it is more than enough. People 
who want to read it can do so; most homes (even mine) have at least one copy 
of the Bible.

Can you make an argument for the virgin birth WITHOUT relying on what it 
says in scripture?

That would be interesting. Even if you can't, it won't change your belief.

How would you feel if I quoted extensive passages of the Koran or Bhagavad 
Gita here (and, believe me, that last IS extensive...)? Would it persuade 
you to that "truth"?

No?  Then you understand why I don't do so and request that you don't quote 
Holy Writ as unanswerable Authority for your belief. It's fine in a group of 
believers; it isn't fine outside that, especially if you are trying to 
foster genuine discussion rather than acceptance of dogma.

<quote from Luke snipped>

Pete.

>Pete.

Maybe I went overboard here a little, but the point was whether Jesus'
being born of a virgin fulfilled a prophecy or not.  I think (and I'd
have to go look it up) that Matthew was the only one of the 4 gospel
writers to say it did.  Luke's account of how it all came to be
doesn't mention a prophecy at all.  Whether it fulfilled a prophecy or
not, in my opinion, is irrelevant.  The fact it happened and the
manner in which it happened is the relevant point.

And sure people who don't believe the Bible or the Koran wouldn't care
one way or the other.  But the prophecy point was being discussed
among many who do believe in what is written in the Bible, thus my
post was aimed at them.  

If I missed the boat on that, I apologize and shall crawl back into
lurk mode.

Regards,

- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <13qgsib4tom1f5c@corp.supernews.com> <7t0qj.60879$k27.31684@bignews2.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>
> I don't agree that it isn't important. I do agree that it isn't THE
> test of divinity. Jesus fulfilled over 100 specific Old Testament
> prophecies for the Messiah (e.g .virgin birth), and miracles only
> represent a few. The fulfilled prophecies are the real proof.

Heh! The Hebrew text translates as "THIS YOUNG WOMAN shall give birth..." 
The Christian version (Septuagint) translates as "A VIRGIN shall bear a 
son..." Isaiah was speaking of a particular person, present before the 
assembled elders at the time. The Christian apologists agree, but say that 
the words put into Isaiah's mouth had two meanings: one that made sense to 
the listeners at the time and one that would be discovered later. That's no 
big deal anyway.

Dozens of virgins give birth in this country every day. Lest you doubt it, 
ask any OB/GYN who did his residency in a large, metropolitan hospital.

As to promises of the messiah, the most important, to the Jews of the time 
(and today), was/will be the ushering in of God's kingdom on earth and the 
establishment of a single worship. In the Jewish teaching, there are TWO 
distinct messiahs, one secular and one spiritual. There is nothing to say 
they can't be the same individual. And there's nothing in the Jewish 
tradition that prevents Jesus from being THE messiah -  When he returns and 
does what He's supposed to do.

As you say, it's the FULFILLED prophecies that are the real proof.
```

- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <13qgsib4tom1f5c@corp.supernews.com>`

```

HeyBub <heybub@gmail.com> wrote in message
news:13qgsib4tom1f5c@corp.supernews.com...

> Some, admittedly, are only similar. Jesus walked on water, Moses moved the
> water out of the way so the Israelites could walk on the mud. Heck, even
> mere mortals can match some of Jesus' spectaculars (such as reanimating
the
> dead or restoring sight).
>

Moses didn't move the water.  The Bible clearly states that an east wind was
blowing all night.  Incidentally, that pretty well in and of itself rules
out the RED Sea as the crossing point.  It's far too deep and wide. The Gulf
of Aqaba couldn't have been the crossing point either as it's too far east
to be sensible, not to mention that it's the second large sea inlet they'd
have encountered.  That leaves the other gulf (nearest to Egypt - my Atlas
is too mingy to give the name) which is mostly north-south.  So if it was an
east wind that did the trick, then the water would have piled up against the
west side of the gulf.  All of which makes it plausible that "Red Sea" is a
misinterpretation, as has been suggested often enough.

Such wind-driven movements of water are common.  Lake Winnipeg, north of
here, is an enormous lake: but if a north wind blows long and hard enough,
the water level at the south end can go up by a yard or more.

This isn't a criticism of you, Pete, so much as a plaint that the Bible
simply doesn't get read, by anybody, really.  A f'rinstance: it's clearly
stated in the flood story that the waters prevailed upwards 15 cubits over
the face of the earth, and that the mountains were covered.  Yet that 15
cubits was enough to submerge Mt. Ararat (although the story says "mountains
of Ararat"), 17000+ feet.  There are two possibilities: either all the
mountains were lower than 15 cubits; or: somebody measured, somehow, that at
the peak of the tallest mountain available there were 15 cubits of water
above it.  Both are ridiculous.  The only possibility is that something has
been lost saince the story was first compiled, or spoken, or whatever.

PL
```

"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>
> ...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> been lost saince the story was first compiled, or spoken, or whatever.

That knowledge came through divine revelation, of course, just as Moses
knew about Creation and other early events. God has always told His
people things from time to time that they had no other way of knowing.
Most serious Christians I know have been given revelations from time
to time. For a Christian, communication with God is supposed to be a
two-way street (John 14:26), and it is for any true believer in Christ.

Some translations of Psalm 104 (e.g. Psalm 104:8 NASV) imply that the
mountains may have been lower before the flood, or perhaps the mountain
and continents were lowered and the sea floor raised during the flood,
and then the mountains raised and the seafloors lowered after the flood.
If this is the case, it certainly could explain why we find such huge
biomass deposits that turned into coal and oil. Imagine the enormous
waves of ocean water, perhaps kilometers high, that would pour in over
the continents. They would sweep away everything in their path, washing
billions of tons of biomass against the sides of mountain ranges,
covering them with silt, dirt, rocks and everything else. With such
extreme and rapid tectonic activity, there would surely be lots of
volcanism as well. The intense heat from volcanism and compression, and
the pressure from the water, could form enormous coal and oil deposits
very quickly. The blood and other liquids squeezed from the crushed
animal mass could have collected and risen to the top to form salt domes.
```

```
"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Of course: default answer if nothing else works.

Why should there be any other answer? Just because you don't like
it, doesn't make it any less valid or true. What's wrong with God
telling us things? :-)

>> Some translations of Psalm 104 (e.g. Psalm 104:8 NASV) imply that the
>> mountains may have been lower before the flood, or perhaps the mountain
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> looked upon the daughters of men and found them fair.

It's not an assumption, it is an interpretation of the clear language of
Psalm 104:5-9 NASB. Quote from Christianity.com: "The New American
Standard Bible (NASB or NAS), completed in 1971, is widely regarded as
one of the most literally translated of 20th-century English Bible
translations." Read it yourself, particularly verse 8:

  5 He established the earth upon its foundations,
    So that it will not totter forever and ever.
  6 Thou didst cover it with the deep as with a garment;
    The waters were standing above the mountains.
  7 At Thy rebuke they fled;
    At the sound of Thy thunder they hurried away.
  8 The mountains rose; the valleys sank down
    To the place which Thou didst establish for them.
  9 Thou didst set a boundary that they may not pass over;
    That they may not return to cover the earth.

True, I filled in a lot of detail that is not specifically stated,
and at most implied there, and those are assumptions on my part. But
it isn't an assumption that the passage above is clearly talking
about the flood, and that verse 8 says the mountains rose and the
valleys sank, and that it was as the waters receded.
```

1. It must be from God.
2. It must be in agreement with the Word of God, the Bible.

Applying those principles gets rid of the chaff. You may not know for
sure about #1, but you can know about #2, which can tell you about #1,
if you dilligently study the Bible (and believe it, of course). :-)

On 5 Feb, 19:58, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
> Some translations of Psalm 104 (e.g. Psalm 104:8 NASV) imply that the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> billions of tons of biomass against the sides of mountain ranges,

Actually, they would be washed into the basins of the lakes and
oceans. Receding water would have dragged the biomass back away from
the mountains.

> covering them with silt, dirt, rocks and everything else. With such
> extreme and rapid tectonic activity

Nothing to do with tectonics.


, there would surely be lots of
> volcanism as well.

No mention of that in the bible.

> The intense heat from volcanism and compression, and
> the pressure from the water, could form enormous coal and oil deposits
> very quickly.

It takes longer than 6000 years to form oil and coal.

> The blood and other liquids squeezed from the crushed
> animal mass

Much of the biomass would have been rooted plant lifeforms. These
would not have been swept away but would have been fossilised in situ.
Where are they?

> could have collected and risen to the top to form salt domes.

There would have been so little salt in the animal biomass that there
would not have been enough to form even a small salt dome.

So again you opt for a far-fetched fallacious option.

- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <13qgsib4tom1f5c@corp.supernews.com> <x%1qj.2879$5M1.559@newsfe23.lga> <Wd3qj.60954$k27.27488@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <40e1a9bf-1e1e-49a1-abf7-6bf2e37558b7@i29g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nothing to do with tectonics.

The water, no. The lowering of the continents and rising of the
sea floors to make the water overflow the mountains, yes. :-)

> , there would surely be lots of volcanism as well.
>
> No mention of that in the bible.

(Genesis 7:11)
  In the six hundredth year of Noah's life, in the second month,
  the seventeenth day of the month, on that day all the fountains
  of the great deep were broken up, and the windows of heaven
  were opened.

The phrase "all the fountains of the great deep were broken up"
sounds like something under the oceans was breaking up.

>> The intense heat from volcanism and compression, and
>> the pressure from the water, could form enormous coal and oil deposits
>> very quickly.
>
> It takes longer than 6000 years to form oil and coal.

They used to think the same thing about diamonds. Now they can
produce perfect diamonds quickly. Same with crude oil. In the
1990's the US DOE did a public demonstration where they placed
water and compost into a large drum, applied great heat and
pressure, and produced crude oil in about 40 minutes. They were
examining the feasibility and economics of producing crude oil
directly from plant material (compost) as an alternative fuel
source. I believe the problem was economics; it took too much
energy to be economical.

>> The blood and other liquids squeezed from the crushed
>> animal mass
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Where are they?

When hurricane Camille struck Mississippi and Lousiana in the 1960's,
there was a strip along the beach where *everything* was washed away,
including all vegetation, roadways, concrete foundations and underground
pipes for a mile or more inland. That was caused by ocean waves from a
big hurricane, which would be *nothing* compared to a 5 kilometer high
tsunami sweeping over the continent, followed by a flood of ocean water
thousands of feet deep, probably lasting for days, maybe weeks. That
would be sufficient to erode down to the bedrock, let alone remove all
vegetation. :-)

>> could have collected and risen to the top to form salt domes.
>
> There would have been so little salt in the animal biomass that there
> would not have been enough to form even a small salt dome.

According to wikianswers.com: "A 50 Kilo human has about 7 tablespoons
of salt within him." At that rate, several billion tons of dinosaurs in
an enormous pile several miles across and hundreds, maybe thousands, of
feet deep, is a lot of salt. There would also be salt in the plants too,
but I don't know if it would be enough to completely explain the salt
domes. :-)
```

On Wed, 6 Feb 2008 16:05:54 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>>"Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>Noah's ark. That was the purpose of the flood. :-)

During the Cretaceous period, which ended 65 million years ago, 90% of
the Earth's land mass was covered in water. Of course, that's not the
only time in geologic history that the ocean's have been that high but
it is one that's dicussed frequently.

As for Noah's ark, that story was written by Moses, who had no idea of
the size or scope of the world at that time. If there had been a flood
during Noah's time, it most likely would have been on a local level,
maybe a large local level, but small scale nonetheless. Not a large
enough scale to cause a global impact, like the Cretaceous flooding.

The story of Noah and the great flood is one that so permeates our
culture that generations of geologists have devoted their lives to
looking for evidence of a prehistoric worldwide flood. But it was not
until the 1990's that geologists William Ryan and Walter Pitman
gathered clues pointing to an actual ancient flood in the Middle East
about 7,500 years ago. Sediment core-samples the scientists took from
the bottom of the Black Sea revealed sections of once-dry, sun-baked
land.

Geologists Walter Pitman and William Ryan were the first to gather
evidence that the Black Sea flooded 7500 years ago

These sediments were then covered by sections of uniform mud, strongly
suggesting that these plains underwent a long-ago influx of saltwater.
Though not worldwide, this cataclysmic event occurred at what could
have been a locus of human activity at the time.

When reading the Bible, which has excellent advice on how to treat
other people and live your life, you have to keep it in a historical
context. About the people who wrote it and about the people who put it
together. How much its been translated and what's been lost over time.
The Protestants alone removed 7 books of the Bible. The men who wrote
the Bible were just that: men. And no matter how inspired by God they
were they could still be falliable, predjudiced, and may have gravely
misunderstood God.

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"You can't kill your way to security, and you 
can't lead through scaring people."
--Bruce Springsteen
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>"Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>in the bible).

Didn't say I proved anything. I said a worldwide genetic study of all
canines lasting several years proved beyond doubt that all dogs came
from the same original stock. Various canine breeds are virtually all
created by humans through selective breeding. This does prove that
the extensive variety of dogs were obtained in a relatively short time
through selective breeding, not evolution => the same principle can
do the same thing in humans, also in a relatively short time without
evolution. The study was also said to prove *conclusively* that
wolves are, indeed, dogs, which was previously thought not to be
the case. But I didn't do the study, or make the conclusions; the
geneticists who conducted the study did that. :-)

On 13 Feb, 15:00, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> >Au contraire. You have proven and disproven nothiong at all. My
> >conjecture stands: either not all men were wiped out or else the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> from the same original stock.

Dating back over 100,000 years but that doesn't fit with your view
that the world is 6000 years old.

> Various canine breeds are virtually all
> created by humans through selective breeding. This does prove that
> the extensive variety of dogs were obtained in a relatively short time

100,000 years or more. Short in geological terms or in the lifetime of
humanity. But much longer than 6000 years.

> through selective breeding, not evolution => the same principle can
> do the same thing in humans, also in a relatively short time without
> evolution.

It has taken modern man a similarly long period of time to lose his
black pigmentation to his skin. I expect that for humans to evolve the
varieties of skin colours available would take at least as long a
period of time (100,000 years or more).

> The study was also said to prove *conclusively* that
> wolves are, indeed, dogs,

You got that arse about face. You should have said that dogs are
really wolves. However, dogs are not wolves even though they are
capable of breeding with wolves (as are coyotes and something else
that runs around in the USA (can't remember what). But then lions and
tigers can be cross bred too.

> which was previously thought not to be
> the case. But I didn't do the study, or make the conclusions; the
> geneticists who conducted the study did that. :-)

So, assuming that you believe the study do you agree with their dating
of the divergance of the wolf and dog lines occured 100,000 years ago
or are you going to suggest that it only happened in the last 6000
years?
```

it directs to a regional event, affecting only the "people of Noah".

```
"tim Josling" <tejgcc_nospam@westnet.com.au> wrote:
> Alistair wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> are mentioned in genesis. In spite of all of the above, these survived.

There is no way to know if antediluvian landmarks were actually the same
landmarks that may be named the same now. After all, 8 people aren't
going to know and remember widespread geographic features, even those
not drastically changed during the flood. Especially in a day when most
people rarely travelled far from home. Current Mt. Ararat is in Eastern
Turkey and Russia, which is a very long journey on foot from modern
Iraq, where Noah was thought to live before the flood.. We don't even
know for sure if it is the same Mt. Ararat in genesis. Locations farther
away may not have even been visited until several generations later, with
only passed down verbal accounts of the original location. For a
postdiluvian name and locale to match the same antediluvian name and
locale would be a long shot, unless God told them.

I live in Birmingham, Alabama, but it was named for Birmingham, England.
Many location names in the US are old American Indian names, but they
do not necessarily coincide with the same old Indian location, or even
any Indian location.

Not all of the world would have been exposed to identical conditions, or
for the same duration. Centers of continents, for example, would probably
not have been eroded as much as areas near the coast. Also, God may
have mitigated the more violent effects near Noah's arc, to protect it.
```

- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6a823d8c-33fa-4b72-a94d-c16e75691d9c@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> <snip>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> mean that YOU would call them Christians, but they call themselves such.

Bill, it's not my business to say who is or who is not a real Christian,
that is entirely between them and God. However, it is clear that there
are many people who say they are Christians, but whose conduct is
so in conflict with the Bible that it is difficult to believe they really
do have faith in Jesus as their savior. Even then, only God can say.
At times, God may give spiritual discernment to someone that a
particular person is false. But the Bible makes it clear that there will
be many false Christians (e.g. Matthew 7:15).

I have known very well for many years a woman who claims to be
a Christian. Yet she is a malicious pathological liar (lies for no reason)
who probably couldn't tell you how many men she has slept with,
and has many other severe problems. Is she a Christian? I'm afraid
she isn't, but I honestly don't know. God knows, but He hasn't
told me. Her dad is a Christian, because years ago while I was
praying for him to be saved, the Holy Spirit gave me understanding
(don't know another way to say it) that he had been saved. The next
day I heard from his wife that he had been saved the day before.

> (The same point is true for the general approach to the Bible - and in fact this difference in understanding of theBible explains 
> the difference in their understanding of the "laws" in effect then (pre Jesus), then (post Jesud), and now.)

Even true Christians are not in perfect agreement on every point of
Scripture. None of us is perfect, and only God has perfect understanding.
But it is my experience that, among really sincere Christians, who diligently
study the Bible, have done so long enough to be mature and knowledgeable,
and are willing, even eager, to have their opinions corrected by God, these
disagreements are usually small and unimportant, relative to the vast areas
of complete agreement.

For example, my pastor, for whom I have great respect, and I do not agree
on the precise meaning of Revelation 20:11. I believe that the language
means the Earth will be utterly obliterated and cease to exist, then be
recreated. But my pastor (who reads Greek) believes the Earth will only be
"cleansed" and renovated. But this is a truly trivial issue, only of academic
interest. Of the vast number of Biblical doctrines he and I have discussed,
there are only a few small differences of interpretation, none of which is
really important.

The real problem comes when Christians decide their thinking is better
than God's, and discount some or a lot of the Bible. All bets are off at
that point, and they are going to be all over the place with strange and
indefensible interpretations based on human judgment, not Scripture.
It doesn't mean they aren't Christians, it just means they aren't very
knowledgeable, or wise. In many respects, they are "baby Christians."
```

On Tue, 05 Feb 2008 22:50:49 -0600, Judson McClendon wrote:

> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> knowledgeable, or wise. In many respects, they are "baby Christians."

How do you bootstrap this though? The decision of what texts are in or out
is a *human* decision. The fact that there are numerous competing books
all claiming to be the word of God shows this.

What if Paul was wrong in his claims above? His involvement in the
movement seems to have produced a radical change of direction. At the
time, some may have argued that he thought he knew better than the texts.

It is also not clear which parts of the OT are now superseded. We still
see people using parts of the OT eg the ten commandments. Other parts less
so, (thou shalt not suffer a witch to live, condoning of polygamy and
slavery of defeated enemies, genocidal campaigns, and the use of stoning as
punishment*, literal interpretation of genesis for example). 

*Jesus did seem to be critical of stoning as we know so this may be a
special case.

Tim
