- **Message-ID:** `<oMlqj.67305$Mu4.50053@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6a823d8c-33fa-4b72-a94d-c16e75691d9c@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <13qj0r3g7981eb@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"tim Josling" <tejgcc_nospam@westnet.com.au> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> all claiming to be the word of God shows this.

You're right, when people exclude parts of the Bible, it is a human
decision, and will never give you the right answer. However, Scriptures
aren't "in competition" which is another fallacious human position.
I've studied the Bible dilligently for decades, and the areas where
there are apparent contradictions are small and insignificant. I say
"apparent contradictions" because such issues have again and again been
resolved by further study. If Scriptures appear to you to contradict,
then you don't understand them yet. The rule of thumb for understanding
*any* communication is not to look for any place where it could possibly
seen as a contradiction, but to see if there is a way to understand it
without contradictions. Common speech is replete with such possible
contradictions, and people take it in stride. But if it were criticized
using the same criteria that Bible critics use, everybody on the planet
would appear by that standard to be inconsistent morons.

> What if Paul was wrong in his claims above? His involvement in the
> movement seems to have produced a radical change of direction. At the
> time, some may have argued that he thought he knew better than the texts.

If you assume that God is incompetent to get His Word written as desired,
then that might make sense. Does the universe function like it was created
by an incompetent?

> It is also not clear which parts of the OT are now superseded. We still
> see people using parts of the OT eg the ten commandments. Other parts less
> so, (thou shalt not suffer a witch to live, condoning of polygamy and
> slavery of defeated enemies, genocidal campaigns, and the use of stoning as
> punishment*, literal interpretation of genesis for example).

Actually, it's very clear. The Mosaic Law (even earlier times) required
God's followers to make sacrifices in atonement for their sins. Cain
killed Able because Able's sacrifice was acceptable to God and Cain's
was not. The new Testament makes it clear that those sacrifices were
like "down payments" on God's True Sacrifice that was to come, the
Messiah. Even Old testament Jews understood this. When Jesus' death and
resurrection stamped "paid" on that account, and God's people no longer
need to make those sacrifices, because Jesus already did it. This is
called Grace. This means that the Mosaic Law has been superceded. But
the principles of God and of righteousness and Godly conduct in the Old
Testament are just as true as ever. We just do not achieve acceptability
to God through doing those things, righteousness is "accounted to us"
through our faith in Jesus Christ. Faith has *always* been the principle
through which sinful humanity could be acceptable to God. It was not
Abraham's actions, but his faith that was "accounted to him as
righteousness" (Romans 4:9). Bottom line: Jesus did not come to do away
with the Mosaic Law, He came to fulfill it's requirements, so we wouldn't
have to (Matthew 5:17). We also no longer live under the theocratic
government defined by the Mosaic Law, so those aspects no longer apply.
The Ten Commandments and other similar codes of personal conduct not
relating to the Mosaic Law, are really principles of how a Godly person
should behave, and we should want to be obedient to them to please God.

> *Jesus did seem to be critical of stoning as we know so this may be a
> special case.

I assume your referring to the account in John chapter 8 of the
adulterous woman brought to Jesus.

It wasn't the stoning per se, but the conditions under which they were
trying to do it, that Jesus objected to. Under Mosaic Law, no one was
to be condemned without a fair trial before unbiased and compassionate
judges. Those who brought the woman before Jesus didn't do that, and
they were obviously not unbiased or compassionate. They also did not
bring the man with them, who had to have been with her if she was
"caught in the act." In other words, it was like an Old West lynch mob.
Further, Jesus did not come (at that time) to judge, but to redeem (John
12:47). It was not according to His purpose in being here to condemn
the woman. However, the woman was apparently guilty. His solution
was brilliant, of course, reminding me of Solomon and the two women
claiming the same baby. :-)
```

```
On Wed, 06 Feb 2008 11:04:20 -0600, Judson McClendon wrote:

> "tim Josling" <tejgcc_nospam@westnet.com.au> wrote:

>> How do you bootstrap this though? The decision of what texts are in or
>> out is a *human* decision. The fact that there are numerous competing
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> apparent contradictions are small and insignificant.

You're taking a narrower view of religious scriptures than I do. The
christian texts are just one subset.

I was once with a group of religious people and asked them to advise me
what, if anything, they agreed on about religion. After more than half an
hour there was not one thing they agreed about.

> If you assume that God is incompetent to get His Word written as
> desired, then that might make sense. Does the universe function like it
> was created by an incompetent?

Yes, quite clearly. As others have pointed out, consider the sheer
scale of pointless suffering of innocent people and animals. 10,000,000
children die under the age of 5 every year. That's more than died in 9/11
*every day*. But these children die slowly from starvation, disease, and
poverty.

Look at the flaws in the human genome - easily understood from a Darwinian
perspective but a mystery if you assume an intelligent design. Why does
malaria exist?

If we were in Universe V1.0, I would be calling and asking when Service
Pack 1 will be available.

>> *Jesus did seem to be critical of stoning as we know so this may be a
>> special case.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> trying to do it, that Jesus objected to.

Well I'm glad that's been cleared up.

Like many gurus, Jesus spoke in riddles, for various reasons. His answer
to the question about paying taxes to the Romans is one example.

Tim
```

Well, remember that, if the Bible is true, we aren't looking at the world

the consequences, for thousands of years. God even put a curse on the
Earth because of man's sin. Before that, there was no death or disease.

"tim Josling" <tejgcc_nospam@westnet.com.au> wrote in message 
news:13qkcnqi7gc3626@corp.supernews.com...
> On Wed, 06 Feb 2008 11:04:20 -0600, Judson McClendon wrote:
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ah, I was going to make a similar response (mainly for the sake of mischief 
:-)) but I read down and saw you had the same idea.

Obviously there are a HUGE lists of imperfections in the world we live in 
and the Universe in general, that would indicate some serious "design flaws" 
(if there WAS a design)...

I liked your examples and won't add to them... :-)

> Yes, quite clearly. As others have pointed out, consider the sheer
> scale of pointless suffering of innocent people and animals. 10,000,000
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pack 1 will be available.

Maybe there's an automatic upgrade after death :-)

>
>>> *Jesus did seem to be critical of stoning as we know so this may be a
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> to the question about paying taxes to the Romans is one example.

And some of his examples were exactly the same ones Gautama Buddha had used 
400 years earlier.  (Matthew 5:40 - 41. In the Persian Empire (539-331 BC) 
marching soldiers had the legal right to compel citizens to carry their pack 
one mile. The Romans, who ruled Palestine at Jesus' time, did the same.  At 
the time of the Buddha, the Indus valley, where his teachings were first 
disseminated, was part of the Persian Empire. Exactly the same words are in 
the sayings of the Buddha. I believe Jesus MUST have encountered Buddhist 
teaching, probably during the nearly thirty years of his life which is 
unaccounted for.

Pete.
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> The Bible doesn't say why Cain's sacrifice was not acceptable.  That
>> being the case it makes God look like a mean, irritable old man.  If
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You reveal a common misconception. Sacrifices in the Temple period were a 
combination of what we would call today tithing and fines. "Sacrifices" were 
often grain, oils, wine, and other products of commerce. Sacrifices to 
propitiate "sin" had a more comprehensive meaning. A "sin," back then, was 
anything from murder to overtime parking - simply a violation of law 
(remember, there was no distinction between secular and spiritual).

Only a teensy percentage of "sacrifices" were burnt. The rest went to the 
upkeep of the Temple and its bureacracy. That which was burnt was often 
eaten, a holy barbeque.
```

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message news:K0bqj.61182
<snip>
> The real problem comes when Christians decide their thinking is better
> than God's, and discount some or a lot of the Bible. All bets are off at
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> knowledgeable, or wise. In many respects, they are "baby Christians."
You seem to have missed my point.

The real problem that SOME of us who believe we are Christians have is with 
those who believe that "the Bible" is a static truth.

- There are some Christians who believe that God gave man an "apostolic 
succession" to help us in a CHANGING understaninding of what the Bible means and 
how to read it.

 - There are some Christians who believe that God gave humans reasonn so that 
they can take His past works (and words) and understand them in today's world.

 - There are some Christians who believe a variety of things that make them 
think that God did give humanity the Bible, but did not stop giving humanity 
revalations - even if they contradict previous (relevant to THAT time) 
inforamtion.

Personally, I cannot even begin to relate to anyone taking the Bible literally 
today.  I know good (and not so good) people who do, but it just has no 
relationship to me or my faith in God.

In another part of these discussions you have talked about "consistancy" and I 
fully accept that *IF* one starts from the belief in the Bible as "literal 
truth" then one ends up believing that the Bible must be literal truth.  It is 
equally true (to me at least) that if one starts from a belief (say in the 
beliefs of the Nicean Creed but) NOT in the Bible as literal truth, then one 
ends up not seeing that one needs to believe in it as literal truth in order to 
be a Christian.

Oh, well,
  Mostly I have tried to stay out of these discussions as I don't see them as 
very productive.
```

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> You seem to have missed my point.

No, we're talking about the same thing. :-)

> The real problem that SOME of us who believe we are Christians have is with those who believe that "the Bible" is a static truth.

God does not change, and His Word does not change (James 1:17).

> - There are some Christians who believe that God gave man an "apostolic succession" to help us in a CHANGING understaninding of 
> what the Bible means and how to read it.

Our teacher is the Holy Spirit (John 14:15-26).

> - There are some Christians who believe that God gave humans reasonn so that they can take His past works (and words) and 
> understand them in today's world.

(Proverbs 3:5)
  "Trust in the Lord with all your heart,
  And lean not on your own understanding;"

> - There are some Christians who believe a variety of things that make them think that God did give humanity the Bible, but did not 
> stop giving humanity revalations - even if they contradict previous (relevant to THAT time) inforamtion.

Those revelations come to us directly from God through His Holy Spirit
(John 14:15-26), not through a human appointed mediary:

(1 Timothy 2:5)
  For there is one God and one Mediator between God and men,
  the Man Christ Jesus,

> Personally, I cannot even begin to relate to anyone taking the Bible literally today.  I know good (and not so good) people who 
> do, but it just has no relationship to me or my faith in God.

Exactly. As the Scriptures above illustrate, such human philosophies
amount to placing human judgment above God's Word. The Bible is
very clear about how we should view it (Matthew 4:4, 2 Timothy 3:16)

> In another part of these discussions you have talked about "consistancy" and I fully accept that *IF* one starts from the belief 
> in the Bible as "literal truth" then one ends up believing that the Bible must be literal truth.  It is equally true (to me at 
> least) that if one starts from a belief (say in the beliefs of the Nicean Creed but) NOT in the Bible as literal truth, then one 
> ends up not seeing that one needs to believe in it as literal truth in order to be a Christian.

Well, yes. But consider what that implies. If you reject the accuracy and
authenticity of the Bible from the beginning, you aren't going to come to
the conclusion the Bible does, and you will not be in agreement with it.
Of course. :-)

What is required to be a Christian is to place our faith in Jesus Christ
and His redeeming sacrifice to make us acceptable to God. Only God
can judge a person's heart whether or not they have truly done this,
though the person can be sure. Beyond that, it is no longer about
salvation, but how faithfully we submit our lives to God, and how
willing we are to allow the Holy Spirit to conform us to the image of
Jesus Christ (Romans 12:2).

One is not a Christian because they're righteous, they're declared by
God to be righteous because it is accounted to them through their
faith in Jesus Christ. It is a free gift of God's Grace :-)

> Oh, well,
>  Mostly I have tried to stay out of these discussions as I don't see them as very productive.

Productivity isn't always immediately apparent. There are probably a lot
of people reading these posts who won't comment here, but who may
benefit in one way or another from seeing differing opinions articulated. :-)
```

On Wed, 6 Feb 2008 11:41:14 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>(1 Timothy 2:5)
>  For there is one God and one Mediator between God and men,
>  the Man Christ Jesus,

Arguments based upon the scripture can be convincing to people who
accept the scripture as authority - although even there I see lots of
arguments of substance.

With good knowledge of the shared parts of the Bible and the Q'uran,
some arguments can work with the religions of the Book.

But a different sort of argument altogether would be needed to
convince a Hindu that his mystical insights are not divine and yours
are.

I would be interested in knowing what arguments missionaries use to
convince them.  (LDS seems to be more interested in saving souls than
most other churches).

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-06T13:18:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IJnqj.67353$Mu4.9598@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6a823d8c-33fa-4b72-a94d-c16e75691d9c@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <mrgqj.67126$kw6.64750@fe10.news.easynews.com> <_imqj.67318$Mu4.22699@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6sujq35kufocj7og9nog3ab5kia2obe6sj@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> most other churches).

Though such arguments have their place, I believe the most common and
effective technique is simply telling people what belief in Jesus has
meant in your own life. Christians call this "giving your personal
testimony." Those hearing it will either believe or they won't (Mark
16:15).

In the final analysis, the result does not really depend on skill in
delivery or clever arguments, it depends on the person's willingness
to believe, and the influence of the Holy Spirit on the person's heart.
To illustrate my point, consider this. For a number of years a German
evangelist named Reinhard Bonke has been going around Africa preaching
revivals (www.cfan.org). The numbers of people coming to the meetings
is staggering - sometimes up to *two million* people at the same
meeting, at the same time, in small African cities. And over several
years *tens of millions* of people have signed cards saying they were
saved at his meetings. I don't think such consistently high numbers
have ever been seen in such meetings in the history of Christianity.
But (and believe me, I mean absolutely no disrespect to Bonke when
I say this; I highly respect him), he has the charisma of a potato.
What is happening at Bonke's meetings is not caused by his oratorical
skills or charisma; it is the anointing of the Holy Spirit on Bonke's
ministry.
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:_imqj.67318$Mu4.22699@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>
> God does not change, and His Word does not change (James 1:17).
>
I love it.  Perfect proof.  If you START with the assumption that the Bible is 
"unchanging" and must be read "literally" - then you end up with a commandment 
that the Bible must be "unchanging" (and read literally).

Can we say circular "reasoning"?  (Not to say that you should NOT start with 
that assumption, but only that when you do, then you shouldn't be surprised that 
you end up saying that the assumption is true.)

It's not circular reasoning. If you start with the assumption that any book
is true, and read it and believe it, you will wind up believing what it says.
Nothing circular in that. And if the Bible is God's true and unchanging
Word, why do you think it strange that the Bible declares itself to be so?
Only if the Bible were *inconsistent* and declared itself *not* to be true
would the situation you call "circular reasoning" not be true. :-)

On Wed, 6 Feb 2008 16:19:22 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>would the situation you call "circular reasoning" not be true. :-)

Therein lies a huge difference between myself (a Roman Catholic) and
Judson whom I would deem a fundamentalist Christian.

Some Christians take the bible literally, Catholics do not. There is a
worthy distinction to be made between historical or scientific truth
and spiritual Truth. The first refers to events that have and do
occur, the second to God's purpose and what they mean for our
decision-making proccess.

In the Catholic Catechism we are taught that science and technology
are precious resources when placed at the service of man and promote
his integral development for the benefit of all. By themselves however
they cannot disclose the meaning of existence and of human progress.
Science and technology are ordered to man, from whom they take their
origin and development; hence they find in the person and in his moral
values both evidence of their purpose and awareness of their limits.

If you wish to read more about that, read C.S. Lewis' "The Abolition
of Man" or "Summa Theologica" of St. Thomas Aquinas.

When talking about creation, for example, many parts of the bible do
refer directly to historical events, the purpose of the bible is to
reveal spiritual Truth. What we know from the story of creation is
then the following:

(1) God made all things; in some special way, 
(2) He made the first human pair; 
(3) He gave them some command (we do not know what the command was -
the garden and the fruit are part of the stage setting); 
(4) They violated the command and fell from favor. 

The time frame is not important, for "there is no difference in the
Lord's sight between one day and a thousand years; to him the two are
the same" (2 Peter 3:8). 

If God wanted to create the world in six days, He certainly could
have, but the evidence suggests that He did not. In addition, theories
of the big bang, evolution, the expanding universe, etc. do not
transcend God, for if they are true, God himself causes them occur.
"Long ago God gave a command, and the heavens and the earth were
created... But the heavens and the earth that now exist are being
preserved by the same command of God" (2 Peter 3:5, 7). 

In any event, that does not change the gist or purpose of the story,
which as Pope John II has said, is that

"...the human's spiritual soul is directly created by God. According
to the hypothesis mentioned [evolution], it is possible that the human
body, following the order impressed by the Creator on the energies of
life, could have been gradually prepared in the form of antecedent
living beings. But the human soul, on which man's humanity
definitively depends, cannot emerge from matter because it is of a
spiritual nature." (Pope John Paul II (April 16, 1986) qtd. in
Drummey, 38).

If you take everything in the Bible , especially in the old testament,
literally, you'll end up in these discussions with fundamentalists
that are, as was described in earlier posts, circular in nature.  The
whole problem is, that in these discussions one loses the meaning of
what was being written, one loses the lesson being taught, and that is
a sin.

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"You can't kill your way to security, and you 
can't lead through scaring people."
--Bruce Springsteen
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6a823d8c-33fa-4b72-a94d-c16e75691d9c@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <mrgqj.67126$kw6.64750@fe10.news.easynews.com> <_imqj.67318$Mu4.22699@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Lnqqj.67823$Mu4.58818@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <ifgmq3la10u9mbjlj93p8dkatnh9rn1919@4ax.com>`

"SkippyPB" <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote in message 
news:ifgmq3la10u9mbjlj93p8dkatnh9rn1919@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 6 Feb 2008 16:19:22 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
> <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[72 more quoted lines elided]…
> preserved by the same command of God" (2 Peter 3:5, 7).

This is a very interesting post, Steve.

So. you are saying that even if the Bible is NOT literally true, the gist of 
it is what matters.

What then becomes the basis for belief?

Is it the Creed of the Roman Catholic church?

Does God exist because the Pope says so?

What basis for belief is there, other than the dogma handed down by the 
church, and the idea that the Pope is the earthly representative of God and 
therefore Holy and infallible?

The faithful believe through faith. But there has to be some basis for the 
faith, surely?

I'm not having a go at the Roman Catholic church; these are serious 
questions intended honestly and not meant to offend.
>
> In any event, that does not change the gist or purpose of the story,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Drummey, 38).

Ah, the critical phrase there is "cannot emerge from matter"... The Pope 
accepts that Evolution may have occurred, but it affects only the temporal 
body and was directed by God anyway.

If you believe in God, that seems like a pretty good resolution for 
reconciling observable evidence with faith.

If you don't believe in God, then such reconciliation is not necessary.

So the Pope draws a distinction between the temporal and the spiritual and 
points out that the spirit cannot emerge from matter, and the spirit is what 
makes us human.

It's a very nice argument.

Here's what I think:

1. It is demonstrable through the feeling of emotions and observation of 
humans dealing with disaster and triumph, that there IS a part of us that 
seems to transcend the merely physical. We call it spirit. Can it be 
explained by purely physical processes?

Maybe.

We all know about endorphins and such, and there has been enough 
experimentation with "mind altering" drugs, on a mass scale during the last 
100 years, for us to reasonably conclude that our moods and perceptions 
(some claim our entire "being") can be altered by physical means. If our 
"spirit" really transcended the temporal and was God given, it would be 
immutable and unable to be affected by drugs. If it is separate and detached 
from physical reality, how can physical things affect it?  While this is not 
a conclusive argument against the spirit, it at least causes a "maybe" 
rather than a "definitely yes".

2. The Pope says the spirit cannot emerge from matter. And yet, given enough 
time, can we really say that emotions and feelings may not evolve as a by 
product of increasing complexity? It is a long way (and a VERY long time...) 
from a uni-cellular animalcule to a human being. As the organism evolves 
more and more complex processes, stress is caused. Maybe emotion is a 
response to these stresses. Most of us have experienced joy and relief when 
something causing us intense worry or fear  is resolved. That wave of relief 
may be a necessary purging process for the organism. There are many 
explanations for the behaviours and feelings of humans, and the book is far 
from closed, with more understanding arriving every day. The point is that 
the case for it being "spiritual" is not the only explanation.

CONCLUSION:

Human beings are complex creatures. Throughout the centries we have looked 
for something to raise us from the mud and to give meaning to our lives. 
Spirituality has done this and provided a coherent focus for us to grow and 
improve. Unfortunately, it has some very negative side effects: religion and 
consequent persecution and division. Maybe we invented God to deal with this 
part of our nature, rather than God actually imparting it into us.

I believe that when I die, that's it. (My memorial - just as for all of us - 
is the people I touched and the ripples I caused...:-)) I see a cycle of 
creation, persistence, and destruction in all things (even the Universe as a 
whole). I have no problem with this, and live my life deriving as much 
pleasure as I can, without doing so to the detriment of others. Eternal life 
is not for me (it would seem to be against the natural order of things and 
if I believe in anything, I certainly believe in that...). I believe that 
nothing is eternal and I'm perfectly happy to be here, in this space, at 
this time, watching the show and participating in it.

Like Robert Louis Stevenson, when the time comes, I'll "lay me down with a 
will".

The case for "spirit" (in the sense the Pope referred to it) as far as I'm 
concerned, is not made.

>
> If you take everything in the Bible , especially in the old testament,
> literally, you'll end up in these discussions with fundamentalists
> that are, as was described in earlier posts, circular in nature.

Agreed.

> The
> whole problem is, that in these discussions one loses the meaning of
> what was being written, one loses the lesson being taught, and that is
> a sin.
>

I don't accept it as a sin, but I agree it is regrettable.

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6a823d8c-33fa-4b72-a94d-c16e75691d9c@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <mrgqj.67126$kw6.64750@fe10.news.easynews.com> <_imqj.67318$Mu4.22699@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Lnqqj.67823$Mu4.58818@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <ifgmq3la10u9mbjlj93p8dkatnh9rn1919@4ax.com> <611jrlF1tf590U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> So. you are saying that even if the Bible is NOT literally true, the gist of it is what matters.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> The faithful believe through faith. But there has to be some basis for the faith, surely?

Very interesting. You articulate very well the same objections I have to
taking that position in regard to the Bible. :-)

Either the Bible is completely true and trustworthy, or it is not. If it is
not, then who is to say which parts are, if any? Once you depart from
inerrancy, you have destroyed the basis for your faith. This is logically
inescapable, and a formal logical proof would be trivial. Believers who
don't realize this are in for a real surprise when their faith becomes
seriously challenged.

If you examine the Scriptures carefully, you will discover that the great
believers in the Bible treated the Scriptures as inerrant; Jesus being the
prime example. Paul based a theological proof on the fact that an Old
Testament noun was singular, not plural (Galatians 3:16).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: You know you're a Christina when .. (was: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 75)_

- **From:** tim Josling <tejgcc_nospam@westnet.com.au>
- **Date:** 2008-02-08T06:35:14
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13qnu12qlcs3k9b@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6a823d8c-33fa-4b72-a94d-c16e75691d9c@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <mrgqj.67126$kw6.64750@fe10.news.easynews.com> <_imqj.67318$Mu4.22699@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Lnqqj.67823$Mu4.58818@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <ifgmq3la10u9mbjlj93p8dkatnh9rn1919@4ax.com> <611jrlF1tf590U1@mid.individual.net> <%%Oqj.82048$rc2.49212@bignews1.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Thu, 07 Feb 2008 20:20:41 -0600, Judson McClendon wrote:

> Either the Bible is completely true and trustworthy, or it is not. If it
> is not, then who is to say which parts are, if any? Once you depart from
> inerrancy, you have destroyed the basis for your faith.

Many people take this view, but many others do not.

Leaving religion aside for a moment, there is nothing we really do know for
certain. But we believe various things with various degrees of certitude.
Just because you don't know something for certain does not completely
destroy all basis for belief. I believe that one day computers will be
built that will be indistinguishable from human beings in practical terms,
but I can't prove it. We don't fully understand the human brain and mind
so there is some uncertainty. Other people regard this as an impossibility.

Getting back to religion, one could take the view that there are
various reasons for thinking that there is more than just the material
world. 

What do we know about these other things? We have the evidence of
introspection or meditation or prayer, which many religious people believe
puts them in touch with something more. One does not need to know all the
details to believe there is something.

There is also Judson's point that if religious belief results in a better
and more productive life, this is a point in its favour. Malcolm X
converted to Islam, and his life turned around. So maybe there is some
truth in Islam.

Others point to miracles, prophesy and so forth. But the evidence for
these things is, to most people, weaker than it seemed a few hundred years
ago.

Then there is the argument that there is no other explanation than
religion for many things. The first cause argument is a variant of this.
There are things we don't understand, so the explanation offered is a god.
But as science progresses, this "god of the gaps" is taking a smaller and
smaller role. We don't need gods to explain comets, or disease any more.

The irreducible fact of human existence is ambiguity and uncertainty. Our
pride in our species' achievements should not blind us to the fact that we
know very little, especially about things that are important to us. I don't
know how long I or those I love will live. I don't know if my wife will
get up and leave tomorrow. I don't know if George Bush will launch an
attack on Iran or North Korea that may escalate into nuclear conflict. I
don't know if the world will plunge into a severe recession in the next few
years. I don't know if religious fanatics will get control of Pakistan's
nuclear weapons. I don't know if Ray Kurzweil is right about the coming
singularity.

Many or most people are deeply uncomfortable with ambiguity. When I
studied criminology, I learned that many of wrongful convictions stemmed
from the jurymen and women feeling uncomfortable with saying, "we don't
know", and instead feeling they had to decide if the accused was guilty or
innocent. Because the jury felt he or she was probably guilty, innocent men
and women have gone to premature deaths and long terms of imprisonment. 

So I say it is important, though difficult, to hold onto the ambiguity,
uncertainty, weakness, and ignorance that is our lot.

As Primo Levi said "we too are so dazzled by power and money as to forget
our essential fragility, forget that all of us are in the ghetto, that the
ghetto is fenced in, that beyond the fence stand the lords of death, and
not far away the train is waiting".

Tim
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:%%Oqj.82048$rc2.49212@bignews1.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> inerrancy, you have destroyed the basis for your faith.

Surely that depends on the basis for your faith, which is the point I was 
trying to examine.

Consider someone who cannot read. The Bible is not open to them. Say said 
person attends a Billy Graham meeting and is moved to accept Jesus.

Is faith any less real if you come to it by hearing a small inner voice that 
says: "I am here...", rather than poring over the pages of the Bible?

To such a person the inerrancy of Scripture is completely irrelevant.

The faithful believe through faith. It isn't necessarily logical and it 
doesn't necessarily depend on the inerrancy or fallibility of the Bible.


> This is logically
> inescapable, and a formal logical proof would be trivial. Believers who
> don't realize this are in for a real surprise when their faith becomes
> seriously challenged.

I don't believe you have any right to say that. The faith of others, even if 
it is not derived from the same source as your own, is a matter between them 
and their God.

Do you think the faith of the hypothetical person I described above is any 
less "valuable" to God than your own?

Who made you the judge?

For myself, the fact that I consider the Bible to be fallible, is not the 
reason I have no faith in a God of Hosts. Even if it WERE correct I'd still 
have a problem with it (in fact, even more than I have now), because I 
couldn't worship the petty-minded, spiteful, insecure God described in it.
(I said as much in response to your "challenge" but you considered this 
honest statement to be "wriggling out").


>
> If you examine the Scriptures carefully, you will discover that the great
> believers in the Bible treated the Scriptures as inerrant;

That's a tautology. The believers in the Bible believed in the Bible.

> Jesus being the
> prime example.

Jesus only believed in the OLD Testament scriptures, (not the WHOLE Bible as 
we understand it) and he "overrode" it when it suited him.

> Paul based a theological proof on the fact that an Old
> Testament noun was singular, not plural (Galatians 3:16).

I don't see anything by Paul as being credible.

Leaving that aside, how does scholars finding it OK, help anyone who DOESN'T 
find it OK?

Are we supposed to gain faith based on what scholars believe?

Faith is personal. Different people will come to it by different routes. 
Some won't come to it at all (me, f'rinstance...). (Assuming you intend to 
go there; I don't...), the destination is what matters, not the journey.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: You know you're a Christina when .. (was: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 76)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5G0sj.30067$1f.17922@bignews9.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6a823d8c-33fa-4b72-a94d-c16e75691d9c@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <mrgqj.67126$kw6.64750@fe10.news.easynews.com> <_imqj.67318$Mu4.22699@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Lnqqj.67823$Mu4.58818@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <ifgmq3la10u9mbjlj93p8dkatnh9rn1919@4ax.com> <611jrlF1tf590U1@mid.individual.net> <%%Oqj.82048$rc2.49212@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <612uioF1s0b69U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wroteL
>> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> is a matter between them and their God.

It's more than that, as I'll explain below.

> Do you think the faith of the hypothetical person I described above is any less "valuable" to God than your own?

I'm not talking about "valuable," I'm talking about "reliable" to the
believer himself.

You are only looking at faith superficially. Let's examine it more fully.
What is faith? The Bible definition of faith is:

Hebrews 11:1
  Now faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of
  things not seen.

I believe the essential elements of faith, in practice, are belief and trust.
Strong faith means strong belief and trust. You have to believe it, and
you have to trust it. Without those two elements, faith is nothing, and
cannot be truly said to even exist. How can you "have faith" in
something you don't believe? How can you "have faith" something
you don't trust? You can't; it's an oxymoron.

So, for a person to have "real faith" in anything, it has to be something
they truly believe, and trust.

Now to my point. How does this play out in individual believers?

For someone who believs the Bible is inerrant, they have that vision of
an inerrant, totally dependable Word of God to believe and trust. They
vest their faith in God's character as described in the Bible to assure
that His Word is absolutely reliable. Believers have literally been burned
alive without disavowing belief. Most of the Apostles were willing to be
martyred rather than rejecting Jesus because they *knew* Who He was.

But once you depart from Biblical inerrancy, you have destroyed any
solid foundation for such belief. Because you have your own self
picked and chosen which parts you believe, you *know for a fact*
that the specific things you choose to believe cannot be the exact
Word of God, because you, not God, picked and chose them. You
also rejected other parts of the Bible *through your own judgment*,
and have lost any claim to any kind of *totally reliable* Word of
God. As long as everything is running smoothly, people continue like
this indefinitely. But let their faith be greviously challenged; let them
have to choose  between imprisonment or execution and denying
God, and what "absolute" will they cling to then? They will have no
absolute, for they rejected that possibility when they rejected inerrancy.
All they will have left is a human made patchwork, they *know* is a
human made patchwork. Why do you think some believers appear to
be Christians for years, until they lose a beloved spouse or child, then
reject God? They may have been true Christians, but they chose to
put their faith in a human patchwork of beliefs instead of the infallable
Word of God.

I don't say these things as a person whose faith has not been tested
to the limit. I won't bore anyone with details, but a psychiatrist whom
once I went to for a while for anti-depressants once said "Wow! It's
like your life has turned into a horror story!" and that was because
of events transpiring over only a couple of months, out of decades.
When you see your life apparently being flushed down the drain,
over and over again, and you have to endure serious pain and other
physical and emotional suffering, you had better have more to hang
on to than a patchwork of beliefs you or someone else picked out
from some selection of possible beliefs. I tell you from long and
difficult personal experience that you have better have something
yoiu can put your trust in as the absolutely dependable and authentic
Word of God to cling to, or you will be in serious trouble. :-)

> Who made you the judge?

(1 Corinthians 6:2,3)

> For myself, the fact that I consider the Bible to be fallible, is not the reason I have no faith in a God of Hosts. Even if it 
> WERE correct I'd still have a problem with it (in fact, even more than I have now), because I couldn't worship the petty-minded, 
> spiteful, insecure God described in it.
> (I said as much in response to your "challenge" but you considered this honest statement to be "wriggling out").

Pete, I did not mean by that expression, that you were being disengenuous.
I myself quoted "wiggled", and the common usage means that I didn't mean
it literally. I admittedly gave you that out and you went that way. My original
challenge, which I clearly and repeatedly stated (though not, unfortunately,
in every single instance) was "if the proof was there, would you accept Jesus
as your savior and submit to God" which was not what you answered. You
are free to answer that now, of course. :-)

>> If you examine the Scriptures carefully, you will discover that the great
>> believers in the Bible treated the Scriptures as inerrant;
>
> That's a tautology. The believers in the Bible believed in the Bible.

I meant "great believers" whose lives are "recorded in the Bible". :-)

>> Jesus being the
>> prime example.
>
> Jesus only believed in the OLD Testament scriptures, (not the WHOLE Bible as we understand it) and he "overrode" it when it suited 
> him.

Jesus clarified and sometimes extended the Old Testament, but he only
"overrode" people's interpretations of it. Jesus never contradicted
anything in the Old Testament.
```

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:5G0sj.30067$1f.17922@bignews9.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wroteL
>>> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>>>>
<snip>>>>
>>> Either the Bible is completely true and trustworthy, or it is not. If it 
>>> is
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

I have snipped your long rationalization of faith although I take no issue 
with it.

Given YOUR definition, based on quoted scripture, your concern seems to be, 
not with what God would find "acceptable",  but rather with the depth and 
strength of a believer's faith when it is tested in times of adversity.

Summarising your argument, the Bible is necessary so that faith can be 
reinforced. That certainly works for you. It may not work for people who 
cannot read, and they may have other (perfectly valid) ways of reinforcing 
their faith. (Like attending Church, discussing things with their Pastor and 
fellows, or anything at all that works for them...watching sunsets on a 
mountain top can be just as real for some people as your reading scripture 
is for you...)

I believe your fanatical focus on one RIGHT way (faith based on the 
inerrancy of scripture) does you no credit, and it doesn't help others to 
find faith either, if that is your purpose. It is divisive and alienating, 
just as insistence on ANY SINGLE road to faith is.

Over many days and thousands of words here you have really failed to 
persuade anyone (including committed Christians) here, that the Bible IS 
inerrant. You have dismissed any argument that refutes your own arguments 
(polonium in rocks, effects of the flood, evolution, etc.) and simply droned 
on about the inerrancy of the Bible. (When most of us can see that it isn't 
inerrant, by even the most casual inspection).

Personally, I have no problem with you finding faith on this basis (or on 
any other basis...); my problem is when you promote this as being the ONLY 
way, and in doing so dismiss everyone who also has faith but arrived at it 
via a different route.

You have indicated that your faith has served you well and helped you 
through hard times. Good. You see your faith as unshakeable and "deep and 
reliable, and trustworthy". All good.  (I feel the same way about what I 
believe, although I accept there are some areas that are not 100% and so 
subject to ongoing review...:-))

So there is no reason why you as a faithful Christian, and me as an atheist, 
shouldn't be able to accept that we each have something working for us and 
leave it at that. I can do this, why can't you? Why are you so driven to 
propound your view? Are you really trying to convince yourself?

I have no desire to undermine or shake your faith (I know you believe that 
nothing could do that, anyway...), so where does the problem arise?

It arises when you are not content to simply have what you've got, but find 
it necessary to promote it as the ONLY way. Your zeal to give to others what 
you have found, leads you to sow on stony ground. (Even Jesus warned against 
doing that...)

Your arguments have been rejected consistently here, yet you persist. The 
persistence, rather than wearing people down, simply makes them more 
resistant and inflames anti-religious feeling by restimulating past bad 
experiences for them.

Ask yourself what is being achieved by all the effort and time you have 
invested.  Can you see even a single poster whose mind has been changed? Is 
there likely to be one?

>>
>>> This is logically
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> believer himself.

OK, then is it any less "reliable" to the believer himself, than your own? 
Can you not accept that people can find (and lose...) faith in diverse ways?

I have met and spoken at length with people who are every bit as ardent in 
their faith as you are (some of them came to it by amazing twists of 
circumstance). They would be offended by your suggestion that there is only 
ONE way. (Especially when  that way, to a dispassionate observer, is 
patently nonsensical. You have failed miserably to make your case for Bible 
inerrancy, despite thousands of words and many hours labouring it)

<snip>

> So, for a person to have "real faith" in anything, it has to be something
> they truly believe, and trust.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> an inerrant, totally dependable Word of God to believe and trust.

That's fine, and it is the way that YOU refresh your faith.

But it ISN'T the ONLY way.


> They
> vest their faith in God's character as described in the Bible to assure
> that His Word is absolutely reliable. Believers have literally been burned
> alive without disavowing belief. Most of the Apostles were willing to be
> martyred rather than rejecting Jesus because they *knew* Who He was.

They weren't martyred for their belief in Bible inerrancy...

>
> But once you depart from Biblical inerrancy, you have destroyed any
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Word of God, because you, not God, picked and chose them.

Suppose you were inspired by God at the time? Is it anymore unlikely than 
claiming that the people who wrote it were inspired by God?

>You
> also rejected other parts of the Bible *through your own judgment*,

And you were the one who was telling us that Free Will was given to us so we 
could exercise it.

Now, it's a bad thing?

(I warned you previously that there are logical repercussions from this 
"Free Will" thing... you still persist, unable or unwilling to see that if 
we have free will, then God doesn't know what the outcome will be, (if He 
did, there is no point in giving us free will).
If there is something that God doesn't know, then He isn't omniscient. If He 
isn't omniscient, why should we accept any of the other attributes He is 
supposed to have?)

Make up your mind: Is the exercise of Free Will by us  a good or bad action? 
Did God give us free will so He could test us, or because He didn't know 
what the choice made by each individual would be?

If it was to test us (He seems pretty fond of testing people...), and He 
already knew what the choices would be, why not just snuff out the ones who 
will choose "wrong" before they even choose (maybe at birth...)? Why make 
the "right" ones have to suffer living with the "wrong" ones?

Better still, why even have Free Will at all? Why not just have everybody be 
"right" and full churches every Sabbath?

What about the ones who vacillate? Find and lose faith several times in 
their lives (or even every other year...)? Does God get confused as to what 
their status is :-)?

> and have lost any claim to any kind of *totally reliable* Word of
> God. As long as everything is running smoothly, people continue like
> this indefinitely. But let their faith be greviously challenged; let them
> have to choose  between imprisonment or execution and denying
> God, and what "absolute" will they cling to then?

Presumably, the same criteria that brought them to faith in the first place.


> They will have no
> absolute, for they rejected that possibility when they rejected inerrancy.

Perhaps the "absolute" is the tiny inner voice that says: "I'm here..."  Who 
knows why people believe what they do? The fact is that they do, and it is 
as real for them as your faith is for you.

It is certain that there are many paths to truth (and "truth" is not 
absolute). To adopt tunnel vision in this regard is simply denying the 
validity of anyone's faith except your own. That's a pretty arrogant 
position to maintain.

> All they will have left is a human made patchwork, they *know* is a
> human made patchwork. Why do you think some believers appear to
> be Christians for years, until they lose a beloved spouse or child, then
> reject God?

Perhaps awful reality causes the scales to fall from their eyes; perhaps 
they find that the mercy they expected was not forthcoming, perhaps this 
crisis of faith makes them question, and the answers turn them from faith... 
Who knows? It really doesn't matter. If someone is going to lose faith, the 
inerrancy or infallibility of the Bible won't change it. Faith is a lot more 
than believing the Bible is true. Faith defines your relationship with God.
(Do you really need an atheist to remind you of this...?)

>They may have been true Christians, but they chose to
> put their faith in a human patchwork of beliefs instead of the infallable
> Word of God.

Not necessarily. How they came to faith is a matter for them. Why would you 
choose the "infallible word of God" if God is whispering in your ear every 
day?

>
> I don't say these things as a person whose faith has not been tested
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Word of God to cling to, or you will be in serious trouble. :-)

A passionate plea based on unfortunate experience. Every single person in 
this forum (posters and lurkers) has met with disaster and misfortune.

Whether you believe it or not your own "long and difficult personal 
experience" is not unique. There are people here who have had to endure 
almost unendurable events, people have lost loved ones, seen their lives 
wrecked, buried their children, suffered anguish of all kinds.  And yet, 
they don't emerge through it convinced that all hearts are wicked and babies 
are born into sin because of some 3500 year old mythology.

How many of us are in "serious trouble"?

What doesn't kill us makes us stronger. We use whatever works for us to get 
through hard times. Belief in the inerrancy of the Bible (for most of us) is 
the last thing on our minds when trouble comes. Kindness and support from 
our friends and families are often found to be helpful (despite the 
wickedness and deceit that they are born with, according to your view...)

I have never needed to see a Pyschiatrist (I mention it in passing; I don't 
think there is anything wrong with people getting help...) and I don't take 
anything that affects the nervous system except alcohol, in moderate doses, 
(not uppers, downers, anti-depressants, painkillers, not even Aspro...I've 
never had a headache in my life.) I sleep well and I enjoy my life. Serious 
trouble? I think not...

Now, your view of "serious trouble" is burning forever in a lake of fire. 
That is real for you; it isn't for me.

"Sufficient unto the morrow the evils thereof."

I'll take each day as it comes and if, having shuffled off this mortal coil, 
I find there is continued existence, I'll deal with that then, not waste the 
life I have now worrying about it.

The mistake you make is in projecting your own belief onto everyone else. In 
a way, I think you even revel in it.

You kind of like the idea that you have joined the select few who will pass 
the final judgement (even though Revelation says it is limited to a few 
hundred thousand, and I think the LDS have first dibs, so you may find 
yourself at the back of a very long line :-)...)

It makes me wonder how you can hold such a belief, knowing that with every 
day that passes more people are going to burn. If Judgement occurred 
tomorrow, there would be around 8 billion people (everyone who has ever 
lived and is living now), of whom less than 400,000 will get into Heaven. 
That's an awful lot of furnace fodder, and, as time goes by, the number 
increases... Your inerrant Book of Revelation has the figures... (I reckon 
it was the postprandial afternoon dream of a guy living in a cave in the 
first century, who had no TV or other diversion, but for you, it is the Holy 
Word of God...I sincerely hope you're wrong; not particularly for myself; 
for all of Humanity.)

As for what happens next, we'll have to wait and see...(Although, speaking 
for myself, I'm not in any particular hurry... :-))

I'm really OK with whatever outcome happens...but, I know which is the most 
likely.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: You know you're a Christina when .. (was: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 78)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> be just as real for some people as your reading scripture is for you...)

Pete, there is a vast difference between only knowing part of the truth
(e.g. someone who has only heard enough about Jesus to be saved) and
actively rejecting part of the truth (discounting part or all of the Bible as
untrue). No one but God knows the whole truth. No human can even
completely know and understand all that is in God's Word, the Bible,
even if they memorized every word. My point is that you cannot have
true and solid faith, while at the same time attack the very foundations
of that faith. Jesus is the living Word of God; the Bible is the written
Word of God (yes, many Scriptures to back that up). The situation of
a believer who rejects part of the Bible is similar to Jesus' statement
that "A house divided against itself cannot stand." This is not some
opinion I just pulled out of the air, the principles are clearly defined in
Scripture. The fact that a large number of believers don't realize this
is simply a sign of their ignorance, just as the fact that a large number
of believers live lives that are in contradiction to the teachings of
Jesus is also a sign of their ignorance. Jesus said "If you love Me,
follow my commandments." If they haven't gotten that down, why in
the world would we think they "have down" more subtle points of
Scripture, even when such are clearly defined?

Pete, you yourself have frequently pointed out some of the bad, even
horrible, things that have been done by believers, or in the name of God.
Surely it would be obvious, even to you as a non believer, that there
must be a reason for this? When people who claim the name of Christ
do not act as Christ says they should act, then they have obviously
departed from the teachings of Jesus. Why? It is because they are not
believers, or they are believers who are ignorant of the principles taught
by Jesus, or they don't care what Jesus says, or they are weak and
succumbed to temptation, or some other similar explanation. If they
aren't believers or don't care, then there is no remedy other than repent
and change. But for ignorance, the remedy is to know the truth. The
truth is found in the Bible, the Word of God. Every Christian on the
planet should know or learn this, for it is a fundamental tenet and truth
of Christianity. For believers to claim they don't know this is alarming,
and clearly part of the reason the Church (true believers) are doing
such a poor job these days.

> I believe your fanatical focus on one RIGHT way (faith based on the inerrancy of scripture) does you no credit, and it doesn't 
> help others to find faith either, if that is your purpose. It is divisive and alienating, just as insistence on ANY SINGLE road to 
> faith is.

If there is any truth taught in the Bible, is is that there is *one way*, and
that is God's way. Individual Christians experience many different ways
of coming to Jesus, but there is only one Jesus, and one salvation. There
is only one way to be acceptable to God, and that is through faith in
Jesus Christ. The Bible teaches this as clearly as it teaches anything.

> Over many days and thousands of words here you have really failed to persuade anyone (including committed Christians) here, that 
> the Bible IS inerrant. You have dismissed any argument that refutes your own arguments (polonium in rocks, effects of the flood, 
> evolution, etc.) and simply droned on about the inerrancy of the Bible. (When most of us can see that it isn't inerrant, by even 
> the most casual inspection).

You "can see?" You see God? You know Him? You observed the
universe being formed? You are certain of these things, you have
absolutely unrefutable evidence?

I didn't see the universe being formed any more than you or anyone else.
But I do know God. I don't know everything about God, and I don't
understand everything I do know about God. But I do know God. I talk
to Him, He talks to me. I can feel His presence inside me day to day. I
know these things as surely as I know anything. And I know that the
Holy Spirit who gives me assurence that God loves me also gives me
assurance that the Bible is God's Word. Jesus called Him the Spirit of
Truth (John 14:15-17). Every true believer has this Spirit of Truth, but
not all believers listen to Him, just as not all believers listen to Jesus.
There is clear evidence in the disobedience of many believers that they
do not always listen to and obey Jesus. Why is it strange to say they
also do not always listen to the Holy Spirit? It would be stranger still
if they did the one and not the other.

> Personally, I have no problem with you finding faith on this basis (or on any other basis...); my problem is when you promote this 
> as being the ONLY way, and in doing so dismiss everyone who also has faith but arrived at it via a different route.

I'm not talking about how people come to faith in Jesus. I'm talking
about how they treat the authority for that faith, the Bible.

> You have indicated that your faith has served you well and helped you through hard times. Good. You see your faith as unshakeable 
> and "deep and reliable, and trustworthy". All good.  (I feel the same way about what I believe, although I accept there are some 
> areas that are not 100% and so subject to ongoing review...:-))

Believe me, I would hardly characterize my faith as "unshakable." It
has been pushed to the breaking point (and seemingly beyond) more
times than I can count.

> So there is no reason why you as a faithful Christian, and me as an atheist, shouldn't be able to accept that we each have 
> something working for us and leave it at that. I can do this, why can't you? Why are you so driven to propound your view? Are you 
> really trying to convince yourself?

Because if you are right, you and I will cease to exist at death, and it
doesn't matter in the long run what I believe. But if I am right, you will
spend an eternity in torment unless you accept Jesus before you die.
I do not want that for anyone, and I don't want to stand before God
and hear Him say "Jud, if you had persevered longer, Pete Dashwood
(or someone else reading these threads) would have accepted Jesus
and be standing beside you now."

> It arises when you are not content to simply have what you've got, but find it necessary to promote it as the ONLY way. Your zeal 
> to give to others what you have found, leads you to sow on stony ground. (Even Jesus warned against doing that...)

Not so. The parable was about how different soil (people) received
the seed (Gospel), not about whether or not the sewer should have
sewn seed there. Jesus also said:

(Mark 16:15,16)
  And He said to them, "Go into all the world and preach the gospel
  to every creature. He who believes and is baptized will be saved;
  but he who does not believe will be condemned.

>>>> This is logically
>>>> inescapable, and a formal logical proof would be trivial. Believers who
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> thousands of words and many hours labouring it)

Again, I'm not talking about how one comes to faith in Jesus. That
happens in a vast variety of different ways. :-)

>> So, for a person to have "real faith" in anything, it has to be something
>> they truly believe, and trust.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> But it ISN'T the ONLY way.

No, one is free to attack the foundation of their faith, if they choose
to do so, or they can cling to the foundation of their faith. As in
most things, they will experience the consequences of that decision.

>> They
>> vest their faith in God's character as described in the Bible to assure
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> They weren't martyred for their belief in Bible inerrancy...

Doesn't matter what they were martyred for. To face martyrdom
without failing requires faith in something solid. Which is why
believers should not attack the foundations of their faith.

>> But once you depart from Biblical inerrancy, you have destroyed any
>> solid foundation for such belief. Because you have your own self
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> God?

Inspired by God to deny His Word that Jesus zealously supported?
I don't think so. :-)

>>You
>> also rejected other parts of the Bible *through your own judgment*,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Now, it's a bad thing?

Free will, no. Making bad decisions, yes. :-)

> (I warned you previously that there are logical repercussions from this "Free Will" thing... you still persist, unable or 
> unwilling to see that if we have free will, then God doesn't know what the outcome will be, (if He did, there is no point in 
> giving us free will).
> If there is something that God doesn't know, then He isn't omniscient. If He isn't omniscient, why should we accept any of the 
> other attributes He is supposed to have?)

Warn away. :-)

I did not suggest that God was unaware of what would happen when
He gave man free will. On the contrary, God knew precisely what
would happen, every step of the way, down through history. But
knowing and permitting is not the same as causing. You may know
if you permit your children freedom they will abuse it, but that does
not mean you caused the abuse. God permitted it because it served
His purposes. I do not claim to know what all of God's purposes are,
though the Bible reveals some of them.

> Make up your mind: Is the exercise of Free Will by us  a good or bad action? Did God give us free will so He could test us, or 
> because He didn't know what the choice made by each individual would be?

Nothing God does is "bad." :-)

> If it was to test us (He seems pretty fond of testing people...), and He already knew what the choices would be, why not just 
> snuff out the ones who will choose "wrong" before they even choose (maybe at birth...)? Why make the "right" ones have to suffer 
> living with the "wrong" ones?

It's not all about us, and dealing with us. There are a vast number of
other beings whom God created who are observing this. Part of what
God is doing is revealing characteristics of His nature to His creation,
of which we are only a part. For example, the angels who were
subverted by Satan to rebel could not be saved. I don't know why,
but they weren't. Perhaps it was because they had clearer and more
definite knowledge of God than we do. God showed His justice in
dealing with them, but He apparently could not (or perhaps just did
not) show His mercy there. But God is revealing is mercy and grace
and forgiveness in dealing with mankind. God has a purpose in
everything, and those purposes will be revealed in His time.

> Better still, why even have Free Will at all? Why not just have everybody be "right" and full churches every Sabbath?

I can only surmise that God wanted people who *chose* to believe
and love Him, even when there were apparent reasons not to. This
would explain many things which you see as reasons not to believe.

> What about the ones who vacillate? Find and lose faith several times in their lives (or even every other year...)? Does God get 
> confused as to what their status is :-)?

We might, but God doesn't, of course. :-) Only God can say what
a person's precise state is.

>> and have lost any claim to any kind of *totally reliable* Word of
>> God. As long as everything is running smoothly, people continue like
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Presumably, the same criteria that brought them to faith in the first place.

Most people come to faith only knowing a little about God. That little
gave them enough faith to accept Jesus and be saved. But who would
want to stay there and never grow in knowledge and faith?

>> They will have no
>> absolute, for they rejected that possibility when they rejected inerrancy.
>
> Perhaps the "absolute" is the tiny inner voice that says: "I'm here..."  Who knows why people believe what they do? The fact is 
> that they do, and it is as real for them as your faith is for you.

"Real" doesn't equate to "mature" and "full." A baby is born with
only a few abilities. It is in the nature of babies and Christians to
grow into maturity with a multitude of abilities. A baby who doesn't
grow is stunted in some way, which is a problem. The same goes
for a Christian. A person isn't "dogmatic" to say such things, or
to be able to recognize a baby who isn't mature, or a Christian who
isn't mature.

> It is certain that there are many paths to truth (and "truth" is not absolute). To adopt tunnel vision in this regard is simply 
> denying the validity of anyone's faith except your own. That's a pretty arrogant position to maintain.

Paths vary. Absolute truth doesn't. God is truth. God's Word is Truth.
Every Christian should know this.
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
