```
I agree discussions of the soul would be a sticky wicket :-)

Thanks for responding honestly and at length on the questions I raised, 
Steve.

You have certainly given me more insight into what Roman Catholics believe.

I had a problem with Papal Infallibity when I was at High School and 
answered a History exam question concerning Unification of Italy. The Papal 
Bull on Papal Infallibility was issued around that time. I was pretty 
scathing about this (I was 16 at the time and not the gentle, wise, 
non-confrontational sage I am today :-)....(yeah, right...)). It turned out 
my History teacher was a devout Roman Catholic... Being a decent man, 
although he was offended by my comments, instead of marking me down, he 
called me in for a little chat. He explained that Catholics to this day 
accept that doctrine (I was shocked :-)) and that I should be gentler about 
people's religious beliefs, then he gave me an A-.

I was therefore very interested to see your description of this and the 
conditions attached to it. It makes sense in the context in which you put 
it. Any leader of a church SHOULD be able to rule on matters of church 
doctrine and have those rulings accepted by the faithful.

I don't share your regard for Saint Paul but that is understandable, given 
our different positions on his teachings.

Nevertheless, there was much of interest and food for thought in your post.

Pete.
```

```
God is not limited to, or by, your (or my) simplistic human ideas of
perfection or unchagining. When God's Holy Spirit moved the 40 or so
men who penned the Scriptures, God knew, with absolute perfection and
complete detail, exactly what would be the result of every word and
letter, every different translation, and every other factor, down
through all history. God is also soverign; and what God determines
will be accomplished. There is no way to "get around" God, or to "out
think" God, or "catch God in a mistake." God is God, the Creator, the
Almighty. This is the part that completely escapes you, because God's
ways are beyond human comprehension, except as God Himself explains
His actions to us. God probably makes decisions with a billion billion
variables, maybe orders of magnitide greater. And even by that overly
simplistic description, no human being could possibly comprehend even
the number of variables, to say nothing of understanding the decision
(Isaiah 55:8,9,11 also Psalm 2). :-)
```

```
>
```

```
god's angels had erred.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<2fpsj.523$f8.177@newsfe23.lga>`
- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6a823d8c-33fa-4b72-a94d-c16e75691d9c@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <mrgqj.67126$kw6.64750@fe10.news.easynews.com> <_imqj.67318$Mu4.22699@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Lnqqj.67823$Mu4.58818@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <d3cee647-45da-4e15-8e72-f4d1433a0795@d21g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <HR%rj.30045$1f.10265@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <48ee7010-852d-427b-9db3-6724432ac3cf@b2g2000hsg.googlegroups.com>`

```

Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:48ee7010-852d-427b-9db3-6724432ac3cf@b2g2000hsg.googlegroups.com...
> On 11 Feb, 17:46, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> > "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> > through all history.

This is the same as saying that all of history is predetermined.  Or, at the
very least, already known to God.  Therefore, unless there is some part of
existence that is not affected by the Bible, everything we do is
predetermined or known to God.  Predestination.

Which brings up an interesting question.  I was out golfing last summer and
(as is always the case, damn it) drove into a nasty thicket.  I found my own
ball and another one with the logo of a local radio station.  Later on I
used that ball and lost it in another thicket.  So, if predestination is a
fact, God must have wanted that ball moved from one thicket to another, by
ME, on such and such a date and at a particular time.  No woder he must be
infinite if he's micromanaging at that level!  :-)

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: You know you're a Christina when .. (was: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 76)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-13T11:04:53-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RqFsj.98980$L%6.58586@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6a823d8c-33fa-4b72-a94d-c16e75691d9c@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <mrgqj.67126$kw6.64750@fe10.news.easynews.com> <_imqj.67318$Mu4.22699@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Lnqqj.67823$Mu4.58818@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <d3cee647-45da-4e15-8e72-f4d1433a0795@d21g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <HR%rj.30045$1f.10265@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <48ee7010-852d-427b-9db3-6724432ac3cf@b2g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <2fpsj.523$f8.177@newsfe23.lga>`

```
"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
> Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> predetermined or known to God.  Predestination.

Predestination and free will are both true. When viewed in a framework
where every individual is free to make their own decisions, but where
God sees all time from His perspective and allows for every decision
and every action, and decides precisely when and how He intervenes,
there is no conflict between free will and predestination. People who
see a conflict, simply have too limited a view. When Jesus was asked
how He could be greater than Abraham, Jesus responded "Before
Abraham was, I Am." God told Moses to say that "I Am" sent him.
With God, at least for this universe, all time is in the present tense for
Him. God *is* at the Creation, just as He *is* at the Judgment, and
just as Jesus *is* before Abraham.

> Which brings up an interesting question.  I was out golfing last summer and
> (as is always the case, damn it) drove into a nasty thicket.  I found my own
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> infinite if he's micromanaging at that level!  :-)

That doesn't even scratch the surface of what I believe to be the reality.
I believe that God is constantly, not only aware of every subatomic
particle in existence, but constantly willing it to exist (Colossians 1:17).
```

```
On Wed, 13 Feb 2008 12:07:13 -0800, Alistair wrote:

> On 13 Feb, 17:04, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> So Jesus was doing a Billy Graham on his flock.

One thing that has always puzzled me in the New Testament is this
passage from Matthew 13

"A Prophet Without Honor

When Jesus had finished these parables, he moved on from there. Coming to
his hometown, he began teaching the people in their synagogue, and they
were amazed. "Where did this man get this wisdom and these miraculous
powers?" they asked. 55"Isn't this the carpenter's son? Isn't his mother's
name Mary, and aren't his brothers James, Joseph, Simon and Judas?
56Aren't all his sisters with us? Where then did this man get all these
things?" And they took offense at him. But Jesus said to them, "Only in
his hometown and in his own house is a prophet without honor."

And he did not do many miracles there because of their lack of faith."

What does this mean? Why did the people who knew him best not believe in
him?

Tim
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: You know you're a Christina when .. (was: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 79)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-13T16:37:16-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<siKsj.71463$vt2.3560@bignews8.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6a823d8c-33fa-4b72-a94d-c16e75691d9c@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <mrgqj.67126$kw6.64750@fe10.news.easynews.com> <_imqj.67318$Mu4.22699@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Lnqqj.67823$Mu4.58818@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <d3cee647-45da-4e15-8e72-f4d1433a0795@d21g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <HR%rj.30045$1f.10265@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <48ee7010-852d-427b-9db3-6724432ac3cf@b2g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <2fpsj.523$f8.177@newsfe23.lga> <RqFsj.98980$L%6.58586@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <06a5efda-4c18-4486-a4ff-ca41d683008e@m34g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <13r6p4bfs31b951@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"tim" <TimJ@internet.com> wrote:
> Alistair wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> him?

Because they couldn't get around seeing Jesus as "that carpenter's son"
they remember running around as a small boy. Anyone who has had a
measure of success and notoriety has seen this kind of thing. When I
was younger and at the peak of my career, I was fairly widely known
and respected in this area among computer professionals. Clients called
me when they knew it was difficult, or maybe impossible. People who
knew me or my reputation almost never challenged my opinion on
technical matters. In their eyes, if I said it, then it must be true, because
they saw that proven out day after day. Yet my wife and children saw
me simply as husband and dad. My wife, who knew virtually zip about
computers, even argued with me sometimes about how computers
work. Professionals I worked with who knew a thousand times more
about computers than she wouldn't have considered doing that, for
they saw me as the professional who always had his ducks in a row
before opening his mouth. This is just typical human behavior. :-)
```

```
On Wed, 06 Feb 2008 11:00:34 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>
>"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message news:K0bqj.61182
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>how to read it.

As a Baptist who is not into apostolic succession, I find that
Christians are a very diverse group.  Most of those in the US
traditional Protestant churches probably don't believe in the
inerrancy of the Bible but rather that it is the inspired word of God.
Most of those in the Atlantic Baptist Convention (Atlantic provinces
of Canada) do believe in the inerrancy with my wife and me being
exceptions in our church.  Based on internal contradictions and the
prefaces of most Bibles I have seen, I can't believe in inerrancy when
the translators are uncertain of the translation and most of the newer
translations include footnotes about verses not in all versions.  I
was a member of a church that was a member of the American Baptist
Churches of the USA before moving North and I would guess that maybe
half believed in inerrancy.  I do have major problems with some of the
Old Testament including the discrepancy between David being a man
after God's own heart and many of David's actions.  Based on internal
contradictions, I do not believe in the Adam and Eve story.  My take
on the Virgin Birth is that if you accept the idea of God as Creator
of everything, the Virgin Birth is possible but not a major factor in
whether to believe in Christ considering that 2 out of 4 Gospel
writers don't mention it.

In terms of Intelligent Design or Creationism, it is at least as
plausible as the alternative although both alternatives have major
philosophical problems.  I define both Intelligent Design and
Creationism as meaning that an entity or entities started the whole
process that created the Universe as we know it with knowing intent
and a design which may or may not be perfect, may or may not be well
implemented or thought out, etc.  It says nothing about the intent or
"goodness" of the Creator.  I believe in Christianity and the goodness
of God partly by logic but mostly by faith.  I believe there is as
much faith involved in believing that evolution can explain all of the
changes in animal forms as there is in believing much of Christianity.

For those of us who are Christian, ponder the teaching of how we treat
the least of these our brethren and whether people who have
deliberately not accepted Christianity but who have rendered service
(fed the hungry, cared for the sick, etc.) are more likely to be saved
than those who profess the faith but have not.  I am not certain on
which side of the fence I am. 
>
> - There are some Christians who believe that God gave humans reasonn so that 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>very productive.
```

- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6a823d8c-33fa-4b72-a94d-c16e75691d9c@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>>  Do you accept the fact that there are those who CLAIM they are
>> "Christians" who do not view this the same as you do? This doesn't
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
> knowledgeable, or wise. In many respects, they are "baby Christians."

Regarding your differences with your pastor over interpretation, perhaps an 
overview would be helpful.

Judaism holds that the mechanism and authority for interpretation of the 
scripture, lies in the scripture itself ("if a matter comes before you that 
is too hard to decide, take the matter to the sages of the generation..."). 
The Catholic tradition holds that the authority and method of interpretation 
is vested with the Church as an institution. The non-Catholic Christian 
teaching usually asserts that the authority for interpretation is vested 
with the individual guided by the Holy Spirit.

In the non-Catholic world, "Good Christian" should be a hyphenated word. 
Christianity is kinda like Barbeque; there's no such thing as a "bad 
Christian." Conversely, one can be a "fallen-away Catholic," or even a "bad 
Jew" (heck, one can be an athiest and still be a "good Jew").

These differing methodologies often reach the same conclusions; murder is 
wrong. These techniques sometimes reach different results. As an example, 
take abortion: The Jewish view is that sometimes abortion is required. The 
Catholic view is that abortion is always wrong inasmuch as no good can EVER 
come from an immoral act. The non-Catholic view is equivocal. Very, very few 
(if any) mainstream Protestant denominations have taken an official position 
on the subject, prefering to allow their members to decide for themselves.

Polygamy is another condition generating differing results. In Judaism, in 
at least one case, polygamy is required. In Catholicism it is banned. In the 
non-Catholic Christian tradition, serial polygamy is permited (you can have 
as many spouses as you want, but only one at a time).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: You know you're a Christina when .. (was: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 70)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-06T12:27:16-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8_mqj.67334$Mu4.62029@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6a823d8c-33fa-4b72-a94d-c16e75691d9c@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <13qjbf7k5kuq425@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> Polygamy is another condition generating differing results. In Judaism, in at least one case, polygamy is required. In Catholicism 
> it is banned. In the non-Catholic Christian tradition, serial polygamy is permited (you can have as many spouses as you want, but 
> only one at a time).

The Bible is clear that one is free to remarry after their spouse dies. The
area where the issue is not as clear-cut is whether or not a person may
divorce and remarry, or marry a divorced person. On casual reading of
Matthew 5:31,32, it appears that Jesus is saying such divorces are invalid,
unless it is for the cause of "sexual immorality" (NKJV). On careful reading,
and in the context of other related Scriptures, it is not quite so clear cut.
My pastor, who is fluent in Koine Greek, did a thorough study of the related
Scriptures, and the writings of just about every Greek Bible scholar he could
find, taking several years. His conclusion is that Jesus was not referring to
all divorce, but was addressing a specific problem where husbands would see
some other desirable woman, then divorce their wife to marry the other woman,
and He was saying, in essence, that you can't do that, unless your wife is
guilty of "sexual immorality." When my pastor shared his findings with me,
and the basis of his conclusion, I completely agree; in the context of all
the related Scriptures, that interpretation makes perfect sense.

I won't go into details to explain why I agree above, but here is an analogy.
There are times when people misinterpret Scripture because they make a wider
application of a passage than the Scriptural context warrants. For example,
people have used the account in Acts 16:31, where Paul and Silas tell a man
that if he would believe in Jesus, he and his entire household would be
saved, to mean that if *anyone* believed, their family would also be saved.
But this was a prophetic word spoken to a particular individual, not a
blanket statement that would, if true, violate the principle of free will.
Interpreting Jesus' statements in Matthew 15:31,32 as a blanket statement
about all divorce is a more complex, but similar situation.
```

```
>>>  Do you accept the fact that there are those who CLAIM they are
…[83 more quoted lines elided]…

I didn't know the bit about being an atheist and a Jew at the same time :-)

I have a number of Jewish friends and they are all excellent people whose 
company I enjoy. Some are more orthodox than others, but all of them have 
made me welcome in their homes and I have attend Shabbat and Passover 
dinners (the food is outstanding, and so is the wine...Not to mention the 
company; I find them generally good humoured and there is much laughter in 
their homes :-))

Never had a problem with belief and none of them have ever made any attempt 
to push their belief on me (wish I could say the same for my Christian 
friends, but most of them have given up now and it is less stressful for all 
of us... :-))

Thanks for a thoughtful and interesting post, Jerry.

Pete.
```

```
Also: given that you have a brain and are capable of independant

rubbish contained in the bible without once considering the veracity
of the material? If I had created a creature and given it independant
thought and subsequently found that it was incapable of exercising
that independance (as you appear to be) then I would wipe it off the
face of the earth and go back to step two: chimpanzees.
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qt%rj.29998$1f.15889@bignews9.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6a823d8c-33fa-4b72-a94d-c16e75691d9c@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <ea5d6927-bc19-4474-8107-5a2ccf67e0f2@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <Bgqqj.67802$Mu4.58660@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <febf2a80-2a91-473d-ae22-fcdf02d8c052@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> No. You claim to be a good christian.

Eh, I don't think I have ever made such a claim. :-)

> Others, who behave in a manner
> that you find an anathema and un-christian, also claim to be good
> christians. One of you is mistaken or else there is no such thing as a
> good christian.

What part of "Christianity is about accepting Christ as one's savior,
not about acting perfect" don't you understand? Christians are told
to do good works and to act in a Godly manner, *because* they
have been saved by God's grace, but that is not what *makes* them
Christians. There is only *one thing* that *defines* a person to be a
Christian, and that is faith in Jesus Christ for their salvation. Paul
discusses that, though Christians can "get away" with sin because
we've been forgiven, we should not use that liberty as an excuse to
sin (Galatians 5:13), and see also 1 Peter 2:15,16. Christians are even
specifically said to sin, but that we should confess and be forgiven
(1 John 1:8,9). A very good friend of mine is a devout Christian and
has been for many years. But he tells me that when he was first
saved, he happily smoked pot while reading the Bible, because he
didn't know any better. He was just as much a Christian then as now;
but he is a lot more mature now.

The Bible defines what it is to be a "good" Christian. A good sample
verse is 1 John 5:1-5, which also says (I'm paraphrasing) that the real
issue is faith in Jesus.

> Also: given that you have a brain and are capable of independant
> thought don't you think it strange that god expects you to swallow the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> face of the earth and go back to step two: chimpanzees.

The Bible is only "rubbish" in your eyes because you are blinded to the
truth. As I've explained before, when a person accepts Jesus as their
savior, God sends the Holy Spirit, Who lives inside the believer, and
teaches them the truth. I know first hand that this is absolutely real. See
Romans 8:1-11 for a contrast between the "carnally minded" (secular
minded) and "spiritually minded." I know you don't like it or accept it,
but it is simply a fact, as millions of true believers can attest. Your own
disbelief only verifies what the Bible says about the "carnally minded".
As I've said here before, the Bible didn't make much sense to me either,
before I accepted Jesus and received the Holy Spirit. Unlike yourself,
I've been on both sides of this fence, and on that basis, my witness
should be more credible. :-)

>> >> The real problem comes when Christians decide their thinking is better
>> >> than God's, and discount some or a lot of the Bible. All bets are off at
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> above?

Simply passing on something said by authority is not "setting yourself
up as judge." I'm not the arbiter of such things; God is the only Arbiter.
I'm simply stating what the Bible says. When I state things that are my
opinion, I clearly state they're my opinion. But when the Bible says "the
wages of sin is death" (it does) I can repeat that as objective fact, not
opinion.

As far as my comments above concerning putting human judgment
above Scripture, see Matthew 15:1-9 and Mark 7:1-7, also Ephesians 4
and Colossians 2 as a few examples. These verses don't even scratch
the surface, for the topic is common throughout the whole Bible. That
the Bible always condemns this is beyond question.
```

_(thread depth: 75)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

- **Message-ID:** `<q5c1r3tiirjngk1tpuakatpkaqohsnuhat@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <ea5d6927-bc19-4474-8107-5a2ccf67e0f2@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <Bgqqj.67802$Mu4.58660@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <febf2a80-2a91-473d-ae22-fcdf02d8c052@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <qt%rj.29998$1f.15889@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <0e21r317a3m0v2i2aj2ig630i97v2emngp@4ax.com> <n11sj.30080$1f.25757@bignews9.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Mon, 11 Feb 2008 13:07:04 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>My friend did not say specifically what Bible passage made him aware
>that he should not smoke pot, but it may have been verses telling
>Christians to obey even man's laws. There are a lot of Scripture that
>would lead one to the same conclusion. :-)

In general, I'm bothered when people associate their faith with
conforming with authority.   At least we aren't ruled by Ayatollahs
here.

The press has belatedly discovered that young evangelicals have
somewhat different priorities than what they have assumed all
evangelicals have.    Much higher on the list are lifestyle choices -
they don't like it when they see Brittany Spears type role models and
people assuming that's OK for them.   Bumped down are legal issues
such as proscribing gay marriage.   That isn't about them.

Prioritizing those issues which are about personal responsibility over
creating laws is, IMHO, a good thing.
```

```
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> here.

It's not "associating faith with conformity to authority." It's simply
that a good person is also a law abiding person, just as a good person
should be kind and care for others. Most laws are there for a good
reason. However, one should not obey human authority when doing
so would mean disobeying God. And, yes I can show you Scriptures
to back that statement up. :-)
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FZ5sj.186399$1x.124765@fe05.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <ea5d6927-bc19-4474-8107-5a2ccf67e0f2@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <Bgqqj.67802$Mu4.58660@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <febf2a80-2a91-473d-ae22-fcdf02d8c052@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <qt%rj.29998$1f.15889@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <0e21r317a3m0v2i2aj2ig630i97v2emngp@4ax.com> <n11sj.30080$1f.25757@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <q5c1r3tiirjngk1tpuakatpkaqohsnuhat@4ax.com> <En3sj.30322$1f.19619@bignews9.bellsouth.net>`

```
Does a "good person" fight laws that not only allow slavery, but allow for the 
"recapture" of slaves in a "free state".  (US history - for those who aren't 
familiar with it.)

How about a "good person" fighting rules that disallow women to vote? black to 
vote? Blacks and whites to serve in the same military unit? gays to openly serve 
in the army?  (As a basically "pro-Choice male", I can even ask what your view 
is of fighting laws protecting abortion rights? capital punishment?)

   ***

I was always amused by the statement that when it comes to marriage" there is 
almost NOTHING that some place/time has found to be illegal that another 
place/time had as legal if not required.
```

_(thread depth: 78)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-12T06:47:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qzgsj.70599$vt2.15855@bignews8.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <ea5d6927-bc19-4474-8107-5a2ccf67e0f2@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <Bgqqj.67802$Mu4.58660@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <febf2a80-2a91-473d-ae22-fcdf02d8c052@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <qt%rj.29998$1f.15889@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <0e21r317a3m0v2i2aj2ig630i97v2emngp@4ax.com> <n11sj.30080$1f.25757@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <q5c1r3tiirjngk1tpuakatpkaqohsnuhat@4ax.com> <En3sj.30322$1f.19619@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <FZ5sj.186399$1x.124765@fe05.news.easynews.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
> Does a "good person" fight laws that not only allow slavery, but allow for the "recapture" of slaves in a "free state".  (US 
> history - for those who aren't familiar with it.)

God would have us disobey human laws that would require us to disobey
God. 'Fighting laws" is another thing.

> How about a "good person" fighting rules that disallow women to vote? black to vote? Blacks and whites to serve in the same 
> military unit? gays to openly serve in the army?  (As a basically "pro-Choice male", I can even ask what your view is of fighting 
> laws protecting abortion rights? capital punishment?)

In general, the Bible does not direct Christians to "change laws" or "change
government." In a democracy, goverments are (at least theoretically) formed
and directed by the will of the people, and Christians should participate in
democracy to promote government that supports the principles they believe
the government should support, just as others do the same. But in the final
analysis, it is God Who permits people and governments to come to power,
and Who takes them from power. That's why Jesus said "Render unto
Caesar..." (Matthew 22:17-21). Caesar (Rome) only had power because
God permitted it.

The Bible is clear that there is complete equality among Christians, with no
racial, gender or social distinctions (Romans10:12, Galatians 3:28). However,
the Bible also makes it clear that, as far as roles in the family, God
created men and women for different roles. These gender roles have to do with
the family, here on Earth, and have nothing to do with Heaven (there is no
gender or marriage in Heaven), or a person's worth before God. It is the
influence of Christianity, more than any other factor, that has given women
respect and equality in modern Western culture. But even way back in Jewish
history, during the time of the Judges, a woman named Deborah was a judge
and a prophet (Judges 4,5); a position of high distinction and authority.
Proverbs 31 is a description of a "virtuous wife". It describes her, not as
a "house slave", but a wise woman who manages the household and deals in
property.

As far as the pro-death abortion laws, AFAIK, they do not compel anyone
to have an abortion. I do support pro-life legislation, to protect the
lives of innocent babies being slaughtered by the tens of millions. Our
society grieves over a mistreated puppy, yet condones such slaughter of
our own flesh and blood. May God forgive us.

As far as gays in the military, there are liars and adulterers in the
military, and there's no law against them being there. I'm more
concerned about society condoning and promoting sin in all forms, be it
homosexuality, promiscuity, greed or dishonesty. The business of Jesus,
and His followers, is not to be condemning sinners, for we are all
sinners. It is to be inviting sinners to accept Jesus Christ so their
sins will be forgiven, as our sins have been forgiven (John 3:17).

The morality of a society will not be improved simply by passing laws,
but by changing the citizen's hearts. That will require an act of God;
a revival. The U.S. has seen several sweeping revivals in the past, and
I'm praying and hoping for a big one soon. :-)
```

```
>
> As far as gays in the military, there are liars and adulterers in the

There's no law against gays being in the military. They just can't practice 
their gayness, same as lying and adultering (West Point oath: "I will not

>
```

```
> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> > "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sure. Let women do that in a Muslim country and see what happens. :-)

I don't see how misogyny in muslim states is any justification for
smug complacency and misogyny on your part. Modern muslim attitudes
towards women and suppressing their rights are much the same as those
expressed by many christians in some churches to this day.

>
> Overcoming the social inertia of an entire culture took time (yes, a lot
> more time than it should have, just as in slavery),

More than 6000 years?

> but it was Christian
> influence that brought it about.

Because the emancipation of women happened in christian or secular
states does not necessarily mean that the christian movement was
responsible for it. You are assuming too much.

>
> >> As far as gays in the military, there are liars and adulterers in the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Not if you reject the Bible.

Does the bible explicitly state that men who lay down with other men
are sinners? or is it just your interpretation of the bible that leads
you to believe this.

> Who defines sin anyway, other than
> the One who created humanity in the first place?

There is no such thing as sin. There may be transgression of laws but
not sin. Don't bother quoting Grimms' Fairy Tales in justification, it
won't work on me.

> If humans can
> define morality, what about those who would kill you and take
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> any "moral" standard other than, if it feels good, do it?

My basis: I avoid doing to others that which I would not wish them to
do to me. That is all.

I choose not to commit crimes because I don't want others to do so to
me (or my family).

>
> >> The business of Jesus,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> sin, along with many other sins. I did not single homosexuals out,

Yes you did. Near the top of this post you said:

<QUOTE>
I'm more concerned about society condoning and promoting sin in all
forms, be it homosexuality ...
</QUOTE>

> I was
> specifically asked about homosexuals. *All* of humanity has sinned, with
> the one exception of Jesus Christ.

Did Jesus own the fig tree that he blighted? Destroying the property
of others must be a sin in your book. JC sinned.

>
> >> It is to be inviting sinners to accept Jesus Christ so their
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> understand? :-)

I know christians are not perfect. They never have been nor will they
ever be. Similarly, no adherent of any religion is perfect nor will
they ever be. Jesus wasn't perfect either so why do you think that I
should think people could be perfect? I just expect that they should
be honest and say that if you trangress the law then you won't make it
in to whichever variant of heaven you believe in. I could never
believe in any religion which at it's base has the notion of sin-
confess-sin-again... as so many have.

If you read the bible then you will know that it requires more than a
blind faith to enter the kingdom of heaven. You quoted a passage where
JC said something along the lines of 'those who know me...'.
That doesn't mean that all you have to do is believe that there was a
person called Jesus and all your previous transgressions will be
forgiven. It means that you have to live a life as Jesus did in order
to know him and to be accepted into heaven. Anything less than
commitment of that order is a sham and will not qualify you, or anyone
else, to enter heaven.
```

```
>> If humans can

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

(Matthew 7:12 - words of Jesus)
  "Therefore, whatever you want men to do to you, do also to them,

> I choose not to commit crimes because I don't want others to do so to

But your not doing it doesn't stop others from doing it to you. How
does that equate to a moral definition (other than the Bible quote above).

In the final analysis, if there are no absolutes in morality, then there is
no basis for a moral code other than opinions, and you know what

it or dice it any way you like, but that is the bottom line.

>> >> The business of Jesus,

…[16 more quoted lines elided]…

>> I was
>> specifically asked about homosexuals. *All* of humanity has sinned, with
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> of others must be a sin in your book. JC sinned.

Jesus created and owns everything, including the fig tree, the property
it was on, the human owner of the property, the planet, the sun, and
universe. I don't know why He did it, but I think He had the right. :-)

> If you read the bible then you will know that it requires more than a
> blind faith to enter the kingdom of heaven. You quoted a passage where
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> else, to enter heaven.

I never said anything about "blind faith," that's your term. The Bible says
it requires faith in Jesus, and specifically points out that faith is more than
simple intellectual belief.
```

```
instead of fresh lime, rap music...whatever) may NOT be considered a "sin" 
by God.

states that homosexuality is a SIN, then your statement above cannot be 
verified.

I say this as someone who is neither ignorant, nor an idiot, nor a liar.
```

```
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 

>> In the final analysis, if there are no absolutes in morality, then there 
>> is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> it or dice it any way you like, but that is the bottom line.

This is a common misconception among Christians who have not deeply
considered the alternatives. There are a number of possible and reasonable
bases for morality. There is a whole branch of philosophy (Ethics) that
deals with this topic.

The notion that morality can be based on belief in a personal God boils
down to "because God says". This is the argument from authority and has
been comprehensively debunked.

So morality possible without reference to God, and reference to God does
not establish morality.

What if, for example, we had been created by evil demons for their
amusement? Should we take our morality from them?

Tim
```

```
"tim" <TimJ@internet.com> wrote in message 
news:13r7v06etg7674d@corp.supernews.com...
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tim

Yeah, this one REALLY bugs me... :-)

When I first encountered (many years ago) the "How can you have morals if 
you don't believe in God?" jibe, I was pretty hurt by it. (I take Ethics 
pretty seriously, and always have. I don't believe in the supernatural, but 
I reckon there's a fair probability that Karma is real (no idea why or how 
it works, but have observed it many times over the years...). Nevertheless, 
whether I believed in Karma or not, I would STILL hold the same Ethical and 
Moral values that I have evolved. I live those values.)

Now I just get annoyed by it. Especially when it comes from people who have 
less moral fibre in their entire bodies than I have in my little finger, but 
can be so smug because THEY have the backing of Almighty God. Some of these 
same Christian people who are claimimg such moral high ground are dishonest 
in their businesses, immoral in their personal lives, and have no idea of 
doing what is right. Of course, it's all OK as long as they are absolved on 
Sunday so they  can do a repeat performance the following week.

Even Jesus said:"Go, and sin no more..." but that part doesn't seem to 
matter in some churches...

I'm not saying this dismal picture applies to ALL Christians, many of whom 
are fully decent folk, but I know from first-hand experience it certainly 
applies to SOME, and they are usually the first ones who point the finger at 
people like me and make the accusation which started me off on this rant... 
:-)

Pete.
```

```
> boils down to "because God says". This is the argument from authority
```

```
"HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote in message 
news:13r8i07tflvn647@corp.supernews.com...
> tim wrote:
>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> commit perjury, etc. The end justifies the means.

I think the argument from authority has been debunked, simply because it IS 
an argument from authority. As no-one can provide a higher authority than 
God, it cannot be refuted by the normal means such an argument would be 
refuted (quoting another authority of equal standing, with a different 
opinion, or discrediting the quoted authority...) So it comes down to 
whether you ACCEPT the authority or not. If you believe in God, you will; if 
you don't believe in God you won't.

I don't believe in God so I don't have that particular authority for the 
moral code by which I live.

However, I do NOT believe the end justifies the means, my morality does NOT 
depend on circumstances, (it is NEVER permissable to commit perjury (and 
even under pressure there have been several occasions in my life when I 
refused to do so), for example...), and I do NOT consider myself a "moral 
relativist" in any way shape or form.

So the argument from authority is NOT debunked ONLY by the "moral 
relativists" (here's at least one person who doesn't accept the argument 
form authority and is NOT in that group).

You probably don't realise how offensive I find this and I know you didn't 
set out to wound, but I am really heartily sick of people telling me I must 
be wicked because I don't happen to believe in God.

It is demonstrably not so. I refute it by my life.


>
> To the moral absolutist, no good can come from an immoral act - the end 
> does not justify the means.

OK, but to be a "moral absolutist" does NOT necessarily require belief in 
God or the acceptance of moral values, ONLY from that authority.

Pete.
```

```
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>However, I do NOT believe the end justifies the means, my morality does NOT
```

- **References:** `<FZ5sj.186399$1x.124765@fe05.news.easynews.com> <qzgsj.70599$vt2.15855@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <58fc7877-f326-458c-8ebc-a9ecb31e8db8@f10g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <KZFsj.98999$L%6.52980@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <0f2f3a36-3057-481d-a1dc-a60be59b8ea6@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <FDJsj.71441$vt2.48814@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <61i4emF1uf2l6U1@mid.individual.net> <13r7v06etg7674d@corp.supernews.com> <13r8i07tflvn647@corp.supernews.com> <61jvbkF1vm2qaU1@mid.individual.net> <cas9r397f0c7u23jks3p1u45lmmhqbv0ee@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:cas9r397f0c7u23jks3p1u45lmmhqbv0ee@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 15 Feb 2008 11:57:53 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> to kill for the general good, is by definition, a moral relativist.

I'm not sure about that, Howard. It is not immoral to defend the nation, and 
there are moral sanctions made by almost all religions to cover this 
contingency.

It may be that your personal moral code is broader than simple black and 
white rules. (Mine certainly is...) But that doesn't mean that you vary the 
rules when pressure is applied. It is a complex issue, but I don't believe 
killing an enemy in combat, knowing full well he would do the same to you, 
makes you evil. It probably does, if you actually relished doing it and did 
so with blind hatred in your heart. If it is a matter of necessity, and you 
don't prolong it for fun, I think it has to be acceptable. (If he ran away, 
he'd still be alive... it is the nullification of the threat that is 
important.)

>
> I don't consider all sins to be equal.   Neither does our justice
> system.   I often go 5 mph over the speed limit (on purpose - I  seem
> to be one of the rare drivers who knows what the speed limit is).
> That's breaking the law, but I won't go to prison for doing so.

Sure. I definitely believe in shades of grey, but that is not the same thing 
as vacillating over a moral code.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: You know you're a Christina when .. (was: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 89)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-02-15T07:50:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lp8br35bi7bf9t3vaof34g9kce8g53jugq@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<58fc7877-f326-458c-8ebc-a9ecb31e8db8@f10g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <KZFsj.98999$L%6.52980@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <0f2f3a36-3057-481d-a1dc-a60be59b8ea6@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <FDJsj.71441$vt2.48814@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <61i4emF1uf2l6U1@mid.individual.net> <13r7v06etg7674d@corp.supernews.com> <13r8i07tflvn647@corp.supernews.com> <61jvbkF1vm2qaU1@mid.individual.net> <cas9r397f0c7u23jks3p1u45lmmhqbv0ee@4ax.com> <61lea9F1vi4uoU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Sat, 16 Feb 2008 01:19:17 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>Sure. I definitely believe in shades of grey, but that is not the same thing 
>as vacillating over a moral code.

"Moral relativism" is a philosophy that says moral values are
dependent upon culture and society.    For instance, killing is bad
unless society condones it (war).   Or bigamy was OK in the Bible but
is sinful now.    Or good Christians could be slave owners or bigots
in the past but not now.    Beating a spouse used to be good, but now
is bad.

I take it to mean "I follow the law except when it conflicts with a
greater law", or "I am willing to kill for the greater good".   Or
even being generous to the needy - except when it's inconvenient.

Certainly authorities have come out to say that it's OK to kill when
the authorities authorize it.    God never meant His commandments to
override their authority in such matters.   Therefore Thou Shalt Not
Kill meant "Thou Shalt Not Kill unless authorized by those in power".
Those in power have determined this to be True.

Moral absolutists believe that what we believe right now are what God
always intended.   Those who came before us were simply wrong.   Those
who will come after us will also be wrong.   They shall be punished.
```

```


"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:lp8br35bi7bf9t3vaof34g9kce8g53jugq@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 16 Feb 2008 01:19:17 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Then, given those definitions, I have to divorce myself from both views.

(I assumed the terms were used by Judson to make his argument more succinct; 
I did not know they had precise meanings as you describe.)

While I would tend towards "moral relativism" under the definition above, it 
is not exactly what I believe. I guess much of my own moral code is 
subconscious. I know what is right when presented with a situation and 
ALWAYS try to follow that measurement. I don't vacillate, depending on 
circumstance, but all the factors in the circumstance are considered before 
deciding a plan of action. It is hard to express in words and is best 
examined through examples.

Suffice to say, the code I use gives me a consistent moral guide and allows 
me to sleep at nights.

I take it VERY seriously and do not consider myself to be immoral or amoral. 
(But neither am I prudish or repressive... like I said, it's hard to express 
in words. Fortunately, it doesn't have to be. In fact, just as in the 10 
Commandments for example, expressing a moral code in simple black and white 
can lead to more problems than it solves. When it comes to morality, 
simplistic approaches are not the best.)

Pete.
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-16T10:48:28-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ttEtj.74685$Mu4.58290@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<58fc7877-f326-458c-8ebc-a9ecb31e8db8@f10g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <KZFsj.98999$L%6.52980@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <0f2f3a36-3057-481d-a1dc-a60be59b8ea6@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <FDJsj.71441$vt2.48814@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <61i4emF1uf2l6U1@mid.individual.net> <13r7v06etg7674d@corp.supernews.com> <13r8i07tflvn647@corp.supernews.com> <61jvbkF1vm2qaU1@mid.individual.net> <cas9r397f0c7u23jks3p1u45lmmhqbv0ee@4ax.com> <61lea9F1vi4uoU1@mid.individual.net> <lp8br35bi7bf9t3vaof34g9kce8g53jugq@4ax.com> <61mp57F1vq4qvU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> describe.)

Please, don't take an atheist's definition of my terms to be what I mean
by them. :-) If I ever knowingly use a word that has a special meaning
for Christians, other than what is generally understood, I always try to
point that out.

As far as legitimate killing vs. murder, the Bible makes it clear that God
judges all acts to be sin or not by what is in the person's heart. I could
list dozens of Scriptures to show this. A person who kills by accident
with no mal-intent is not judged guilty. Further, there are valid reasons
for a person to be put to death, like by a court for certain crimes. Since
there were times that God clearly sanctioned, even prosecuted, certain
wars between the Jews and their enemies, and nowhere does the Bible
say that soldiers are guilty of sin, it is consistent to believe that killing in
war is necessarily a sin. When the Bible does not specifically say, the
principles the Bible does state are to be used; in this case, the guilt or
innocence is based on the heart of the soldier. Similar principles also
apply to law enforcement. If a policeman kills in the line of duty, and
without hate or malice, it can be a righteous act. Hate or malice would
make it murder, even if the act was justifiable in the line of duty. The
case of defense of the innocent, such as a parent killing someone trying
to kidnap a child, is similar, because the parent is the primary authority
God delegates to protect children. Self defense is not quite as clear-cut.
The only Scripture I am aware of that can reasonably be understood to
speak directly against self defense is Jesus command to "turn the other
cheek" in Matthew 5:38-47. However, Jesus did not mention someone
trying to kill you. In my opinion, self defense against mortal threat is
permissible, so long as there is no hate. But I also believe that Jesus'
statements can be reasonably interpreted as forbidding killing in self
defense, even against mortal threat. At any rate, a person who believes
God forbids killing in self defense, yet does so anyway, is definitely
sinning, because in their heart they are being disobedient to God. An
analogous situation is described in 1 Corinthians 8.
```

```
On Sat, 16 Feb 2008 13:30:29 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>Then, given those definitions, I have to divorce myself from both views.
>
>(I assumed the terms were used by Judson to make his argument more succinct; 
>I did not know they had precise meanings as you describe.)

I have to admit that I don't know that they have a single meaning
either.   I see different people using the term to mean what they want
it to mean.

But thinking about it, I see that we all put different values on
different moral behavior.   We don't count all sins as being equal,
and we are willing to do things that are otherwise considered sins
when the circumstances dictate.    It appears that "moral relativism"
can be used as a label to judge members of more liberal churches - as
long as we define it carefully enough to exclude what we do ourselves
that fit that definition.
```

```
On Sat, 16 Feb 2008 13:30:29 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>While I would tend towards "moral relativism" under the definition above, it 
>is not exactly what I believe. I guess much of my own moral code is 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>examined through examples.

Brain scientists had a recent study - they hooked people up to sensors
while asking some ethical questions.    One of the situations involved
being in a bus teetering off the side of a cliff.   There's someone at
the edge, if he falls off, the rest of the bus would be saved.    If
we could let him fall through a trap door, everybody would be saved.
People would even push the lever to open that trap door.   But people
wouldn't push him off the side to result in that same rescue of
everybody else.     The sensors showed conflicts with various parts of
the brain as it appeared to be working in some kind of ethical
election.    Actually touching the person to kill him got stronger
votes than touching a lever (and much harder than simply not saving
him as he slipped).
```

- **From:** tim <TimJ@internet.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<13r9ifd6foijr6c@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <ea5d6927-bc19-4474-8107-5a2ccf67e0f2@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <Bgqqj.67802$Mu4.58660@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <febf2a80-2a91-473d-ae22-fcdf02d8c052@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <qt%rj.29998$1f.15889@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <0e21r317a3m0v2i2aj2ig630i97v2emngp@4ax.com> <n11sj.30080$1f.25757@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <q5c1r3tiirjngk1tpuakatpkaqohsnuhat@4ax.com> <En3sj.30322$1f.19619@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <FZ5sj.186399$1x.124765@fe05.news.easynews.com> <qzgsj.70599$vt2.15855@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <58fc7877-f326-458c-8ebc-a9ecb31e8db8@f10g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <KZFsj.98999$L%6.52980@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <0f2f3a36-3057-481d-a1dc-a60be59b8ea6@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <FDJsj.71441$vt2.48814@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <61i4emF1uf2l6U1@mid.individual.net> <13r7v06etg7674d@corp.supernews.com> <13r8i07tflvn647@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Thu, 14 Feb 2008 07:54:25 -0600, HeyBub wrote:

> tim wrote:
>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> perjury, etc. The end justifies the means.

Do your research. The argument from authority is not valid. For a summary
and links see http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Appeal_to_authority or any
philosophy text.

The fact that the argument from authority is invalid means that you cannot
argue that something is true merely because someone you admire says it
is true.

It does not mean that the claimed fact is not true, it just means that you
cannot prove it by this particular argument.

For example, if I were to say "George Bush is a great man, and he says
Osama bin Laden is evil, therefore ObL is evil." This is an invalid
argument (even if you accept the premise). But it may well still be the
case that ObL is evil.

In the case of religion, we could imagine a world that had been created by
evil demons for their amusement. That would certainly help explain the
problem of evil. Would we be obliged to accept the moral views of these
demons, by the same argument from authority. I would not.

Tim Josling
```

```
"tim" <TimJ@internet.com> wrote:
>
> Do your research. The argument from authority is not valid.

Tim, I'm sorry, but that is simply absurd. Consider:

Postulate: God exists, has a moral standard and will judge and punish
all created beings who do not follow it.

Conclusion: Any moral standard not based on God's law will be punished
by God Who, being omnipotent, overrules all human authority.

Morality based on authority can *only* be invalid in three situations:
    1. If there is no God.
    2. If God sets no absolute moral standard
    3. The specified moral standard is not God's

Duh!
```

```
>>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> deals with this topic.

So, how is what you said not "based on opinion?" Either there is
a moral standard, and morals are based on that, or morals are
based on opinion. Or, are you saying that there is a consistent
set of moral values, not based on any authority, that humans
ubiquitously agree to? If not, why not? Because their opinions
differ. Which is what I said. Duh!

> The notion that morality can be based on belief in a personal God boils
> down to "because God says".

So? If God is Creator, Who else should set moral standards?
Who else is better equipped?

> This is the argument from authority and has been comprehensively
> debunked.

Really? No doubt the some ones who comprehensively proved there
is no God, no? :-)

> So morality possible without reference to God,

Never said it wasn't. Said it was necessarily based on opinion.

> and reference to God does not establish morality.

Not necessarily, no. So? Reference to "morality" does not
necessarily "establish morality" either, no?

> What if, for example, we had been created by evil demons for their
> amusement? Should we take our morality from them?

Why not? If you're convinced that this is indeed the case, then go for it.
Been lots of examples of it down through history. :-)
```

```
organised society and Satan's moral code to be that which applies
where there is no societal application of moral codes. However,
continuing in this vein, I would view that Satan's law is universal
and that god's law is only a small sub set of Satan's.
```

```
>> "tim" <T...@internet.com> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…

Wrong! Are you drinking/smoking pot again, or do you have trouble
following a logical proof? :-)

It's very simple. Try to follow along without pictures. Tim insisted that
"The argument from authority is not valid." That is a blanket statement
that there can be no valid argument for a moral code based on authority.
This is a preposterous claim, and I demolished any possible argument
to that effect with a 30 second logical proof.

And in response to your rambling nonsense above, if there is a God
who imposes His moral code on you, then you can reject all you like,
but will still face that God's judgment. Objections against absolute
power are irrelevant. If there is no God, or God does not impose a
moral code on you, or your moral code is the same as God's, then
you won't fall under divine judgment. Otherwise, you're toast. :-)
```

```
On Mon, 18 Feb 2008 11:12:47 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>> Your "proof" did nothing of the kind.
>
>A proof must be logically consistent and complete to be correct,
>which mine is. 

Unless I'm reading the wrong "proof", this is what you said:
>
>Postulate: God exists, has a moral standard and will judge and punish
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>    3. The specified moral standard is not God's

A specified moral standard can be God's - such as "thou shalt not
kill", but created by someone who doesn't have God's interest.

With your postulate, it is possible that God doesn't mind some other
moral standards as long as they don't conflict with His greater
authority.  

And there are lots of Christians who accept God's authority but who do
not accept your postulate.

Even with this postulate, all you can reasonably infer is that there
are some invalid moral authorities - not that there are valid moral
authorities.    Who do *you* accept as a moral authority with the
ability to override what you have determined to be God's will
according to the scriptures as you read them?

>Causing people to believe it is a different matter,
>because people believe what they choose to believe, evidence,
>even logical proof, to the contrary. :-)

Agreed.
```

- **References:** `<58fc7877-f326-458c-8ebc-a9ecb31e8db8@f10g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <KZFsj.98999$L%6.52980@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <0f2f3a36-3057-481d-a1dc-a60be59b8ea6@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <FDJsj.71441$vt2.48814@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <61i4emF1uf2l6U1@mid.individual.net> <478e4e68-458d-422b-92eb-449d35f71ad4@k2g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <3kJtj.74807$Mu4.53036@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <4v7jr35utlg7ilr0iesv8e3f6rrel3ubur@4ax.com> <a0juj.85888$N67.79941@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <d4gjr3h768hmu362sq0jal29hn10272ov5@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> kill", but created by someone who doesn't have God's interest.

So? Irrelevant to my proof, or the point it addresses.

> With your postulate, it is possible that God doesn't mind some other
> moral standards as long as they don't conflict with His greater
> authority.

Irrelevant. The proof deals only with the possibility of a valid
authority based moral code.

> And there are lots of Christians who accept God's authority but
> who do not accept your postulate.

Did you even read the post my proof was in answer to? The claim was
made that it had been *proven* that *no* moral standard based on
authority was valid. My post was made for the singular reason of
proving that such a claim was logically impossible. The proof
showed that conditions could be postulated for which, not only
could an authority based moral code be valid, but that *only* a
specific authority based moral code would be valid. The point of the
proof *was not* that the postulate was necessarily true. To invalidate
my proof, you would have to prove that the postulate is impossible,
and doing that is impossible. :-)
```

```
On 16 Feb, 22:19, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> >"Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> to that effect with a 30 second logical proof.

So quickly that you neglected the fourth clause that I outlined
above.

Tim is correct.

>
> And in response to your rambling nonsense above, if there is a God
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> you won't fall under divine judgment. Otherwise, you're toast. :-)

It is for the individual to decide what form their moral code should
take. It is not for the state or for your celestial fairy to dictate
to me how I should behave. If your god exists then he has a moral duty
to make himself personally known to each individual otherwise he has
no legal or moral right to sit in judgement upon them.

If a state passed a law and kept it secret you would be justified in
breaking that law and objecting to any punishment handed out to you. I
do not accept any authority based on Grimms' Fairy Tales (you call it
the bible) nor do I consider the "inerrant" text to be celestial in
origin or design or inspiration. Unless your god makes himself known
to me I am justified in rejecting all moral codes and laws based upon
the notion of the existence of the celestial fairy.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <ea5d6927-bc19-4474-8107-5a2ccf67e0f2@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <Bgqqj.67802$Mu4.58660@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <febf2a80-2a91-473d-ae22-fcdf02d8c052@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <qt%rj.29998$1f.15889@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <0e21r317a3m0v2i2aj2ig630i97v2emngp@4ax.com> <n11sj.30080$1f.25757@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <q5c1r3tiirjngk1tpuakatpkaqohsnuhat@4ax.com> <En3sj.30322$1f.19619@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <FZ5sj.186399$1x.124765@fe05.news.easynews.com> <qzgsj.70599$vt2.15855@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <58fc7877-f326-458c-8ebc-a9ecb31e8db8@f10g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <KZFsj.98999$L%6.52980@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <0f2f3a36-3057-481d-a1dc-a60be59b8ea6@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <FDJsj.71441$vt2.48814@bignews8.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Wed, 13 Feb 2008 15:51:37 -0600, Judson McClendon wrote:

> Duh! You said Christianity did not result in better status for Women,
> and I pointed out Islam as an example of what typical non Christian
> religions are like toward women. Please list any major religions that
> give higher place to women than does Biblical Christianity. There are
> None.

Having read all the Daoist books I can lay my hands on, they are entirely
consistent in their view that differences of race, sex etc are nothing. We
are all one in the Dao, they say. From about 1100 AD however this was
overtaken in actual practice in China by the increasing dominance of the
Confucian ways of thinking.

If you contrast this with what the Christian texts say, the difference is
striking.

"As regards the individual nature, woman is defective and misbegotten, for
the active power of the male seed tends to the production of a perfect
likeness in the masculine sex; while the production of a woman comes from
defect in the active power...." Thomas Aquinas, Summa Theologica,Q92, art.
1

"And a man will choose...any wickedness, but the wickedness of a
woman...Sin began with a woman and thanks to her we all must die"
Ecclesiasticus, 25:18, 19 & 33.

"And I find more bitter than death the woman, whose heart is snares and
nets, and her hands as bands: whoso pleaseth God shall escape from her;
but the sinner shall be taken by her." Ecclesiastes 7:26

"...Christ is the head of every man, and a husband the head of his wife,
and the head of Christ is God." 1 Corinthians 11:3

"...women should remain silent in the churches. They are not allowed to
speak, but must be in submission, as the Law says, If they want to inquire
about something, they should ask their own husbands at home; for it is
disgraceful for a woman to speak in the church." 1 Corinthians 14:34-35

The bible is not entirely consistent on these points, but the above was
the ruling interpretation for thousands of years.

The role of women in biblical times seems to have been not unlike that of
women in strict Islamic societies today.

Slavery also has biblical backing. Not to mention the notion of the divine
right of kings - the holy roman empire and so forth.

The emancipation of women, the abolition of slavery, democracy, science -
all these things happened only *after* the iron grip of religious zealots
on society was considerably weakened. They happened in spite of Christian
religion not because of it.

Tim
```

```
"tim" <TimJ@internet.com> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1

Pointedly not from the Bible.

> "And a man will choose...any wickedness, but the wickedness of a
> woman...Sin began with a woman and thanks to her we all must die"
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> but the sinner shall be taken by her." Ecclesiastes 7:26

See why the Apocrypha *should not* be considered part of the Bible?

> "...Christ is the head of every man, and a husband the head of his wife,
> and the head of Christ is God." 1 Corinthians 11:3

As I said, simply setting the pattern for families. Not degrading women.

> "...women should remain silent in the churches. They are not allowed to
> speak, but must be in submission, as the Law says, If they want to inquire
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> the ruling interpretation for thousands of years.

In the Roman Catholic church, perhaps. Most Protestants I know of
interpret this as specific language to the women in the church at Corinth,
where they had been commonly disrupting the services. Even then, it's
still only talking about church services. Far more Scriptures indicate the
respect and equality before God of women. Paul, who wrote that letter
to the church at Corinth, frequently commended many women, and
spoke very highly of their value in His and their own ministries.

> The role of women in biblical times seems to have been not unlike that of
> women in strict Islamic societies today.

Duh! Baby girl's weren't commonly given clitorectomies. Circumcision
of males is commonly done even today for health reasons.

> Slavery also has biblical backing. Not to mention the notion of the divine
> right of kings - the holy roman empire and so forth.

Giving the Jews a king was *their* idea, not God's idea (1 Samuel 8,9).

> The emancipation of women, the abolition of slavery, democracy, science -
> all these things happened only *after* the iron grip of religious zealots
> on society was considerably weakened. They happened in spite of Christian
> religion not because of it.

They happened in spite of secularly oriented power hungry people
ruling *in the name* of Christianity. Not the same thing. Jesus said
very bitter words to the same kind of people who were ruling during
His ministry. True Christianity has always been about freedom, not
secular power (Luke 4:18, in which Jesus quotes from Isaiah 61:1).
```

```
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

But not a sin?

> references to homosexuality in the Bible (and there are many) are totally

as homosexuality, as well as incest and paedophilia were widely
practice at that time I wouldn't classify it as a sin.

BTW, I'm straight.

> does that equate to a moral definition (other than the Bible quote above).

It is as good as any code of morality as any and better than most.

>
> In the final analysis, if there are no absolutes in morality, then there is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> it or dice it any way you like, but that is the bottom line.

You are right. There is no moral code. Choose to live your life as you
see fit but be aware that others may choose to visit upon you in a
manner you would abhor.

Ultimately there is no morality other than personal morality. My
personal morality does not stop others from doing evil unto me but it
does provide a good base for law.

> >> > So why have you just condemned homosexuals? Why single them out for

…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> greed or dishonesty."),

So you mentioned three other sins, prime amongst the sins was

Bollocks

> including the fig tree, the property
> it was on, the human owner of the property, the planet, the sun, and

I don't agree. What he did would be classed as a criminal act today.

>
> > If you read the bible then you will know that it requires more than a
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> simple intellectual belief.

I use 'blind faith' to describe accurately your approach to your

and commitment (amongst many other things required) to know Jesus and
```

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Still, it is the word of god relayed through a True Christian.

You say this because you have personal knowledge of the state of their
faith in Jesus, or because those words, which directly conflict with the
words of Jesus and the Bible in general, are provable truth, or ... what?

>> > "...Christ is the head of every man, and a husband the head of his wife,
>> > and the head of Christ is God." 1 Corinthians 11:3
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> of itself, a form of abuse and degrading to women.

You don't even believe that yourself! Are you "downgraded" because you
work for someone who has authority over you? Are you "downgraded"
because you must submit to other authorities? Designating authority
*does not* imply that those under that authority are "inferior", and you
know this. You do not believe that yourself in any other area of your life,
yet you spout it here knowing it is false. In essence, you are lying, and
you know you are lying. :-)

> Interestingly, the Jehovah Witnesses have exactly the same attitude.
> The woman must obey her husband in all things even where he is abusive
> and/or a lapsed JW. Excepting, of course, where the church elders give
> her position to disobey or leave her husband.

You once again display ignorance and/or dishonesty. The above is vestly
different from the Biblical Christian position, and one of the *many*
reasons why JW's are a cult, not Christianity.

> Muslim women subjected to rape only count as one-half of a man in
> Sharia courts. Recent case have had women whipped for allowing
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> very far.

In the Bible, in cases where the question is rape or adultry and there is no
evidence that the woman willingly submitted, she is to be held innocent
(Deuteronomy 22:23-29). The woman is clearly to be given the benefit
of the doubt in such cases, and the man is held more strictly responsible.
Hardly "demeaning" of women, or comparable to Islam!

>> > The role of women in biblical times seems to have been not unlike that of
>> > women in strict Islamic societies today.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> babies are circumcised without any medical pre-condition.

Male babies are ubiquitously circumcised in the United States, unless the
parents specifically request otherwise. This has been true for a long time,
certainly since the early 20th century, at least. Many male non-Jewish
Americans were suspected or accused of being Jews by the Nazis in the
30's and 40's because of this. The reason it is done is entirely for health
reasons; if a male's foreskin isn't kept thoroughly clean, it can promote
cervical cancer in their wives, and other problems. Probably more of an
issue 100 years ago than now, but the practice still continues, and is not
likely to change.
```

```
keep the foreskin and glans clean then the infection likely to be
transmitted is thrush (Candida albicans) which is a yeast infection.

medical conditions, which are very rare, which can necessitate
circumcision in the male.
```

```
> I don't need the Bible to tell me what's moral., and I can have fun without needing to kill people and take what they have.

Ah, but other people disagree (see prison population for many
examples). What moral standard says your moral opinions are
those which are correct? You have often pointed out that there
is vast difference between cultures on what is moral. Who is to
say which moral standards are good or bad?

> I behave decently because I know it makes sense, not because I'm afraid I'll burn in Hell if I don't.

I accept that. But what about others? Is it a majority rule kind
of thing?

>>>> The business of Jesus,
>>>> and His followers, is not to be condemning sinners, for we are all
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Fine example of "Christian charity"...

Fine example of "selective editing" to leave only the critical comments
and remove my actual comments. So, does your moral code include
a standard for honesty in posting? ;-)
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> Didn't suggest you didn't have morals. I'm saying how do you
> (and everyone else) know those morals are correct, or best, or
> even better, than someone elses?
> 

Nobody else is.  Everyone else is saying they have a set of morals that 
they have personally arrived at.  You are saying you have a set of 
morals given to you by god, checked out by god, and confirmed by god as 
being superior to everyone else's.

I think you are a liar.  I do not believe you have ever personally met 
god, frankly, and also think you make your claims to give yourself a 
sense of superiority.

Donald
```

- **From:** Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2008-02-16T04:26:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bead0cb4-0888-4a14-843c-9323ed3caa20@j28g2000hsj.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <ea5d6927-bc19-4474-8107-5a2ccf67e0f2@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <Bgqqj.67802$Mu4.58660@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <febf2a80-2a91-473d-ae22-fcdf02d8c052@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <qt%rj.29998$1f.15889@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <0e21r317a3m0v2i2aj2ig630i97v2emngp@4ax.com> <n11sj.30080$1f.25757@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <q5c1r3tiirjngk1tpuakatpkaqohsnuhat@4ax.com> <En3sj.30322$1f.19619@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <FZ5sj.186399$1x.124765@fe05.news.easynews.com> <qzgsj.70599$vt2.15855@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <58fc7877-f326-458c-8ebc-a9ecb31e8db8@f10g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <KZFsj.98999$L%6.52980@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <61i3cuF1uus15U1@mid.individual.net> <ChZsj.73466$Mu4.61093@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
On 14 Feb, 15:40, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> > If we all ran around doing what you suggest, society would collapse. It has nothing to do with God or moral compasses or you
> > claiming the moral high ground.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

>
> Didn't suggest you didn't have morals. I'm saying how do you
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> say which moral standards are good or bad?

Judson, you appear to be advocating Anarchy (which is a valid belief
system).

Only individuals may judge whether a behaviour is moral or not and
that can only be done from their personal perspective. Morality is not
absolute but is relativistic in that one person's moral behaviour can
be viewed as immoral by others and punished by the law. Not long ago
people like you would have had people like me arrested and charged
with blasphemy because I deny the bible and the concept of god. Even
to this day I can be imprisoned in the UK for what I have written on
this newsgroup. Fortunately, that moral law is deemed archaic and is
likely to be removed from the statute books in the next decade
(earlier if some righteous do-gooder attempts to take me to court). So
even moral people (true christians) can be immoral (by persecuting non-
christian creeds).

From my perspective, the worship of some celestial fairy is the
ultimate immorality possible whilst people are fighting wars and dying
from starvation, disease and poverty.

>
> > I behave decently because I know it makes sense, not because I'm afraid I'll burn in Hell if I don't.
>
> I accept that. But what about others? Is it a majority rule kind
> of thing?

It seems that humans like to live in social groups. Those groups
impose a moral code of their own which may be abbhorent to some of the
groups members. Witness christian law where a significant (not
statistical) minority are muslim. Fortunately, in the UK, we are
moving away from the narrow minded blinkered imposition of christian
only law to a more secular state. We do need to divest the religious
lords and inherited seats from the House of Lords so we still have
some way to go.

It irks me that in making an application for a passport the photograph
can be witnessed by Christian Science Practitioners and certain other
religious persons. What makes them a more reliable witness than any
other professional?

>
> >>> So why have you just condemned homosexuals? Why single them out for
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> a standard for honesty in posting? ;-)

The post I made was honest and decent. Your sentence had pointed out
three or four sinners including the gays. I was only interested in
pursuing a point about gays because I know that there are only three
references accepted by some (bigots) to denounce homosexuality and two
of those are so obscure that one can be taken misinterpreted as advice
to farmers!

As I have pointed out elsewhere, homosexual does not appear in the
bible but the word gay does and that in a form requiring us to support
gay men.
```
