"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:brr9r3ddorduru74l0nnh7u9vk7rav7ehv@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 12 Feb 2008 13:55:56 -0800 (PST), Alistair
> <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> showing that their primary agenda is lifestyle, not God.

One thing I feel strongly about is keeping GOD out of "government".  I think 
that I previously indicated, that I would agree that "gay marriage" should be 
kept out of government *IF* straight marriage were as well. If all types of 
"unions" were treated as contracts and there were no discrimination based on 
sexual orientation, that would be fine with me.  If, however, "straight 
marriage" (rather than "unions") is what government deals with, then the "life 
style" issue of gay marriage belong in the discussion as well.  None of this has 
to do with "God" in my opinion (and I hope that it doesn't in the future.)  If 
you want to discussed whether "blessings of God" (or sacraments) or anything 
else should be offered to gay couples, then that certainly SHOULD be discussed 
in relationship to religion and God - but that should be dealt with in your 
"organized religion" and not as a topic for government handling (change or 
status quo).
```

On Fri, 15 Feb 2008 06:34:28 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>One thing I feel strongly about is keeping GOD out of "government".  I think 
>that I previously indicated, that I would agree that "gay marriage" should be 
>kept out of government *IF* straight marriage were as well. If all types of 
>"unions" were treated as contracts and there were no discrimination based on 
>sexual orientation, that would be fine with me. 

The state should not be in charge of sacraments.   Period.

If I have a housekeeping spouse for 40 years while I pay into social
security, and get divorced and remarry, why should my spousal social
security benefits go to my new spouse?    If I live with my sick
brother taking care of him all of my life, why can't I set him as my
social security beneficiary?

We have child support and implied contracts with palimony.   Let the
churches define marriage, and let the state handle contractual law
resulting from them.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<q5c1r3tiirjngk1tpuakatpkaqohsnuhat@4ax.com> <En3sj.30322$1f.19619@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <FZ5sj.186399$1x.124765@fe05.news.easynews.com> <qzgsj.70599$vt2.15855@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <58fc7877-f326-458c-8ebc-a9ecb31e8db8@f10g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <brr9r3ddorduru74l0nnh7u9vk7rav7ehv@4ax.com> <Unatj.227291$rl3.8637@fe02.news.easynews.com> <2d2cr3dq2abs49kn0n359vmjper7lrc49d@4ax.com> <F8qtj.203345$Ib6.55625@fe03.news.easynews.com>`

```
On Sat, 16 Feb 2008 00:30:30 GMT, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:


>
>As I stated in an earlier note (not in this thread), I agree completely.  I have 
>NEVER understood the Supreme Court decision on Polygamy (make from the 19th 
>century as I recall and dealing with Mormon marriage practices of the time).

You are referring to Reynolds v. US (1878). The US Supreme Court recently (2005) refused
to consider a challenge from Holm, after the Utah Supreme Court ruled again that polygamy
is illegal. 

The reason in all cases is "the state has an interest in protecting monogamous marriage."
That's hard to disprove. Libertarians claim individuals have the right to screw whomever
they want, but that sets up a contest between individual and state interests. Courts are
agents of the state, which means their protection of state interest takes precedence over
championing individual human rights. 

In simple terms, if it's you versus the state, the state wins. 

In Lawrence versus Texas (2003), the US Supreme Court ruled that homosexual sex is OK. Is
there a conflict here? I don't see one. Homosexuals are not part of the reproductive
system; polygamists are.  

When gays adopt children, that opens a new arena. Family courts are about looking after
the welfare of children when parents prove incompentent to do so. Their mission is not to
be Divorce Court splitting assets between separatng partners. That's the domain of
contract or quasi-contract, in the absence of a written contract. 

It gets sticky when politicians grant tax preference to married couples. Ideally, they
would not do it, but as a practical matter, there's no way of stopping them.The question
is how gays can get any equity. 

I think the answer is simple. Just claim that 'Georgia' is your wife. George can claim
'Wilma' is his wife. Who will question the legitimacy of the marriage? Will they ask to
see a marriage certificate? In my experience, I've NEVER been asked to produce a piece of
paper. Not once. Not even at the driver's license office. Nor at a bank when opening a
joint account. Nor at a doctor's office. Certainly not when filing a joint tax return.
'They' take your word for being married. 

So long as the two of you don't show up together.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pljfr3l9h8gfbgke9l8ehgtdb52g3fhshi@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<En3sj.30322$1f.19619@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <FZ5sj.186399$1x.124765@fe05.news.easynews.com> <qzgsj.70599$vt2.15855@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <58fc7877-f326-458c-8ebc-a9ecb31e8db8@f10g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <brr9r3ddorduru74l0nnh7u9vk7rav7ehv@4ax.com> <Unatj.227291$rl3.8637@fe02.news.easynews.com> <2d2cr3dq2abs49kn0n359vmjper7lrc49d@4ax.com> <F8qtj.203345$Ib6.55625@fe03.news.easynews.com> <djlcr3pnvhqv5n727fekvcv3jli2g6m980@4ax.com> <13rffrukb6k1j8a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Sun, 17 Feb 2008 05:00:46 -0000, tim <TimJ@internet.com> wrote:

>On Fri, 15 Feb 2008 22:20:33 -0600, Robert wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>wife's passport still had her maiden name on it.

There's no legal requirement for a wife to change her surname. Personally, I don't think
she should. Under English Common Law, you can use whatever name you want, or multiple
names, so long as you're not defrauding anyone. You don't need a court order or license to
change your name. Conversely, there is no law saying people must call you by your 'new'
name. Drivers Licence agencies usually won't without a court order. Getting a court order
is so simple you can do it yourself without an attorney. I did.

You don't need a license to be legally married in 11 US states (most of Canada and
Australia, but not England). Unless state law affirmatively says otherwise, common law
marriages are legal. ALL of the 39 states that require a license recognize common law
marriages from the 11 that don't (Full Faith and Credit). All you have to do is say you
were in Texas or Colorado when you started living together. It won't work LEGALLY for
same-sex marriages, but will for practical purposes if you're artful.

Urban myth holds that if you live together for X years, you're legally married. That's not
true in any state. The test is whether you "hold yourself out to be married", which can be
accomplished in an hour.

For ten years, we've filed joint tax returns with two surnames; the IRS hasn't questsioned
it. Nor have landlords on 15 apartment leases. The only ones who questioned it were
medical insurance companies, so she uses my surname for doctor visits covered by
insurance.
```

On Mon, 18 Feb 2008 08:36:00 -0700, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On Fri, 15 Feb 2008 22:20:33 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>common-law married.

The law is the same in the other 10 common law states. It won't work for same-sex
'marriage', though.

>I wonder how Palimony suits would work if they find out that the
>person being sued never got divorced from his earlier marriage.

By definition, palimony is a "non-marriage relationship contract." Because it's not a
quasi-marriage, family courts have no jurisdiction. It's handled like any other contract. 
It there's no written contract, the court will 'construct' one. 

>As far as reproductive rights go - the fact that more and more people
>choose not to marry while having children might be changing how the
>state handles things. 

The way states and Divorce Lawyers handle things (badly) is the reason WHY the marriage
rate has fallen so much. The people have voted with their feet.
```

Tim

```
On Wed, 13 Feb 2008 09:02:52 -0500, donald tees wrote:

> tim wrote:
>> On Tue, 12 Feb 2008 13:29:27 -0800, Alistair wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Donald

Historically volunteers are better fighters than conscripts. In WWI,
Australian soldiers voted against conscription for this reason. They did
not want to be fighting alongside conscripts.

The problem with volunteers is getting enough of them. Word is getting
around about how veterans are treated. High suicide rates, homelessness,
unemployment, poor medical care etc.

Unemployment in the civilian economy is low which hinders military
recruitment. This may change soon, as anyone following the economic news
will know.

Tim
```

>On Wed, 13 Feb 2008 12:38:08 -0500, SkippyPB wrote:
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>Tim

I doubt either Obama or Hillary would institute a draft as they both
want us out of Iraq within a year or two of their taking office.
McCain, who has said we could be there 100 years or more, might be
more likely to institute a draft though it would be political suicide
for him to say so.

I read an interview of someone, whose name I can't recall at the
moment, who suggested that the US should do like Israel and other
countries.  A person, man or woman, must be conscripted for two years
as soon as they turn 18 or graduate from high school.  It wouldn't
have to be in the Armed Forces, it could be in the Peace Corps or some
other humanitarian agency.  But you'd have to give 2 years to your
country.  I think it is a good idea.  We might get less apathetic
voters and the younger generation would take more interest in what was
going on around them.

I was drafted in 1967, began serving in the Army in 1968.  I barely
knew there was a war going on and I surely didn't understand the
reasons for it.  Of course I got a quick lesson but I wish I'd been
more aware at the time.

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

On Thu, 14 Feb 2008 22:48:02 -0000, tim <TimJ@internet.com> wrote:

>On Thu, 14 Feb 2008 11:44:44 -0500, SkippyPB wrote:
>> I doubt either Obama or Hillary would institute a draft as they both
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bad timing and judgment on the Democrats part for Vietnam.  Truman was
the real instigator of the Vietnam War though it was Eisenhower who
sent the Green Berets to Vietnam and it escalated from that point
forward.  Truman did bring an early end to WW2 by dropping the atomic
bombs on Japan.

LBJ used the draft because it was already in place.  We also have to
keep in mind that JFK had papers on his desk in Washington DC that
would have effectively ended the Vietnam War but he was assassinated
before he signed them.  LBJ was under pressure by the defense
contractors in his home state of Texas to prolong the war as they were
making money hand over fist supplying the forces.

>For those who weren't around at the time it was the Democrats.
>
>A lot of things have happened in the last few years that no-one would have
>expected. So I can't rule out Clinton doing this.

We must also remember that the Clinton's are baby boomers and that
Bill was an active war protester in his college days.  I'm not sure
about Hillary, but I just can't see her even considering a draft
purely for stocking the armed forces with bodies.

The draft was suspended in 1973 under Richard Nixon.  But the law that
was in place during the Vietnam era was enacted in 1940 during
Franklin Roosevelt's term.

>
>Tim

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)

On Fri, 15 Feb 2008 12:00:05 -0500, SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:


>We must also remember that the Clinton's are baby boomers and that
>Bill was an active war protester in his college days.  I'm not sure
>about Hillary,

When Hillary was a college student at Wellesley, her 92 page honors seniors thesis was
titled ""There Is Only The Fightï¿½": An Analysis of the Alinsky Model. " Saul Alinsky was a
far left radical, called communist by some, who is regarded as the father of community
organizing or 'take it to the streets' activism. Basically, he advocated organizing the
poor and powerless and coaching them to use any means necessary to be heard. He criticized
mainstream middle and upper class liberals (like the Clintons) as being ineffective. 

When the Clintons entered the White House in 1992, they asked Wellesley to make the thesis
unavailable to everyone, which was done. You can now find (copyright violating) copies in
the internet and read about it at the link below. The thesis is NOT the smoking gun
critics hoped for; Hillary Rodham did not enthusiastically jump in bed with Alinsky,
rather she thought his ideas were anachronistic (out of date). 

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%22There_Is_Only_The_Fight...%22:_An_Analysis_of_the_Alinsky_Model

In 1971, while a law student at Yale, Hillary interned at Treuhaft, Walker and Burnstein,
a radical law firm in Oakland CA. Two of its four partners were former communists. Bill
followed her there and they lived together in nearby Berkeley. At the end of summer, they
retuned to Yale (New Haven).  What happened in the following years is best explained in
this Wikipedia article:

"During her post-graduate study, Rodham served as staff attorney for Edelman's newly
founded Children's Defense Fund in Cambridge, Massachusetts,[53] and as a consultant to
the Carnegie Council on Children.[54] During 1974 she was a member of the impeachment
inquiry staff in Washington, D.C., advising the House Committee on the Judiciary during
the Watergate scandal.[55][56] Under the guidance of Chief Counsel John Doar and senior
member Bernard Nussbaum,[35] Rodham helped research procedures of impeachment and the
historical grounds and standards for impeachment.[56] The committee's work culminated in
the resignation of President Richard Nixon in August 1974.[56]

By then, Rodham was viewed as someone with a bright political future; Democratic political
organizer and consultant Betsey Wright had moved from Texas to Washington the previous
year to help guide her career;[57] Wright thought Rodham had the potential to one day
become a senator or president.[58] Meanwhile, Clinton had repeatedly asked her to marry
him, and she had continued to demur.[59] However, helped by her having passed the Arkansas
bar exam but having failed the District of Columbia bar exam,[60] Rodham came to a key
decision. As she later wrote, "I chose to follow my heart instead of my head."[61] She
thus followed Bill Clinton to Arkansas, rather than staying in Washington where career
prospects were best. Clinton was at the time teaching law and running for a seat in the
U.S. House of Representatives in his home state. In August 1974, she moved to
Fayetteville, Arkansas, and became one of two female faculty members at the University of
Arkansas, Fayetteville School of Law,[62] where Bill Clinton also taught. Even then, she
still harbored doubts about marriage, concerned that her separate identity would be lost
and her accomplishments would be viewed in the light of someone else's accomplishments."
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hillary_clinton

Someone quoted her as saying she dated Bill because he was the only man in Yale Law School
who wasn't intimidated by her intellect. She graduated at the top of the class and on the
board of the law review; Bill was in the middle of the pack.
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Personally, I support Hillary because I think Obama would be another Jimmy Carter. He's
too smart and, more importantly, too PRINCIPLED to play the Washington insider game.
They'd give him no support and, as a result,he would get nothing done. 

As in Corporate America, Washington doesn't want 'the best', it wants someone who doesn't
appear to threaten the status quo. Hillary knows how to play that game whereas Obama
doesn't. Some of us have seen that dynamic, on a smaller scale, in IT. Human nature is the
same everywhere.
```

I had never thought about Obama being another Jimmy Carter.  I really
liked Carter because he was one of the smartest men to ever become

That is a good point.  Our primary where I live is March 4th and I
still haven't made up my mind!  I generally don't even vote in the
primaries because the outcome is a usually a foregone conclusion by
the time they get to where ever I happen to live.  I was going to vote
in the Republican primary so I could cast a vote for Ron Paul.
However, since I generally vote Democratic and the race is so tight
this time around, I'll vote in that primary.  I'm just not sure for
whom as yet.

No.  There is no conscription in the USA.  It was totally suspended in
1975.  You still have to register when you turn 18 with the Selective
Service System but there is no draft.  Even when there was a draft,
the Peace Corps was not an alternative.

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Howard Brazee" <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> must obey even men's laws and that the bible says THOU SHALT NOT KILL.

"Thou shall not murder" is better. God would not have said "never
kill" then instruct people to be killed for certain crimes. If you
understood the Scriptures, this would be clear to you. Jesus said
if you have hate in your heart you are guilty of murder. God judges
the heart. A person who kills buy accident isn't guilty. Neither is a
person who kills for justice or protecting the innocent. A policeman
or a soldier or executioner can kill to uphold the law, or protect the
innocent, and have no hate in their heart. If a policeman or soldier
kills from hate, that is murder. If they kill without hate because it
is their duty, there is no sin. Jesus never instructed His followers to
avoid those occupations, or to condemn them. :-)
```

They pre-date JC and, in the absence of any commentary from JC, are

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> not say thou shalt not murder. It says thous shalt not kill.

No, that's simply a common older translation. The actual Hebrew is
talking about wrongful killing, i.e. murder. Someone else just made a
post explain that. It is one of the few areas where the King James
translation was not very good. Virtually all modern translations reflect
this. For example,  Exodus 20:13 in the New King James Version,
the New American Standard Bible, the New International version and
the Revised Standard Version, all read "You shall not murder." This
change was made because of the original Hebrew, not to fit doctrine.
```

- **References:** `<K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <ea5d6927-bc19-4474-8107-5a2ccf67e0f2@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <Bgqqj.67802$Mu4.58660@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <febf2a80-2a91-473d-ae22-fcdf02d8c052@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <qt%rj.29998$1f.15889@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <0e21r317a3m0v2i2aj2ig630i97v2emngp@4ax.com> <n11sj.30080$1f.25757@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <1dc1ba3b-dfc5-4375-830d-91edeed4256a@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Tue, 12 Feb 2008 13:29:27 -0800 (PST), Alistair
<alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>On 11 Feb, 19:07, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Howard Brazee" <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>must obey even men's laws and that the bible says THOU SHALT NOT KILL.

In the newer translations of Exodus (20:13) the Sixth Commandment
says, "Thou Shalt Not Murder". This is more accurate because the
Hebrew word so translated does not refer to killing in general but to
malicious and unlawful killing.

The Roman Catholic Catechism teaches that legitimate defense can be
not only a right but a grave duty for one who is responsible for the
lives of others. The defense of the common good requires that an
unjust aggressor be rendered unable to cause harm. For this reason,
those who legitimately hold authority also have the right to use arms
to repel aggressors against the civil community entrusted to their
responsibility.

The efforts of the state to curb the spread of behavior harmful to
people's rights and to the basic rules of civil society correspond to
the requirement of safeguarding the common good. Legitimate public
authority has the right and duty to inflict punishment proportionate
to the gravity of the offense. Punishment has the primary aim of
redressing the disorder introduced by the offense. When it is
willingly accepted by the guilty party, it assumes the value of
expiation. Punishment then, in addition to defending public order and
protecting people's safety, has a medicinal purpose: as far as
possible, it must contribute to the correction of the guilty party.

 
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

SAM: "What's shaking Norm?"
NORM: "All four cheeks and a couple of chins."
--From U.S. TV Show, "Cheers"
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6a823d8c-33fa-4b72-a94d-c16e75691d9c@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <ea5d6927-bc19-4474-8107-5a2ccf67e0f2@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <Bgqqj.67802$Mu4.58660@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <febf2a80-2a91-473d-ae22-fcdf02d8c052@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <qt%rj.29998$1f.15889@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <13r28s8q6fadg3d@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"tim" <TimJ@internet.com> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> is simply your opinion.

It is not circular, which I clearly refuted earlier. Accepting something
as true, then believing that something, *is not* circular reasoning.
Neither is using internal evidence to strengthen your conviction that
something is true. If that were circular reasoning, then the whole of
science woud be, by that definition, circular reasoning. :-)

> It may or may not be right, but to express your views - and that is what
> they are - as dogmatic truths, is just going to annoy people.

Yes, it is my opinion that the Bible is God's inerrant Word. Not
everyone believes as I do. I am certainly aware of these facts.

What you are missing is that I am addressing different audiences here.
Of course an athiest like Pete or Alistair doesn't believe what the Bible
says. But those who claim to be Christians should believe what the
Bible says, because the Bible is their source and authority for believing
in Jesus as their savior in the first place. For them to reject part or all
of the Bible attacks the very foundation on which their faith rests, and
makes as much sense as dynamiting the foundation of their home,
whether they realize that or not. For the athiests or other non believers,
relating what the Bible actually does say is useful to refute bogus claims
of about what the Bible says, inconsistency, etc. For the believers, it is
useful to refute false beliefs.

My beliefs are simply my beliefs. When the Bible clearly says a thing,
then reporting that the Bible says that thing is not being dogmatic, but
simply telling the truth. I back up what I say with Bible references, as
you well know. I fully understand that some people reject part or all
of the Bible. But since the Bible is a primary foundation of my beliefs,
it is always useful in explaining why I believe as I do. That's not being
dogmatic either. :-)
```

On Tue, 12 Feb 2008 07:26:42 -0600, Judson McClendon wrote:

> "tim" <TimJ@internet.com> wrote:
>> Judson McClendon wrote:

>> What you are missing is the circularity of your views. The notion
>> that everything the bible says is an absolutely correct, objective fact
>> is simply your opinion.

> It is not circular..

Saying this doesn't make it so. No matter how many times you say it.

> 
>> It may or may not be right, but to express your views - and that is
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> believing in Jesus as their savior in the first place.

Again this is your opinion but not one shared by many others including
Christians, many of whom have thought and researched deeply about these
issues. I have seem a number of people move from a position of
"bible/koran/other scripture is literally and completely true" to a more
adult view. incorporating shades of gray.

Does not the fact that there are millions of people who hold similar views
to yours, but concerning different books that say very different things,
give you some pause to think? The reason you are a Christian is
statistcally almost certainly that you were brought up in a Christian
country. An accident of birth. If you had been born in Saudi Arabia you
would probably be saying the same things about the Koran.

> When the Bible clearly says a thing,
> then reporting that the Bible says that thing is not being dogmatic, but
> simply telling the truth.

True but saying it is therefore an indisputable fact is not simply telling
the truth.

Tim

```
"tim" <TimJ@internet.com> wrote in message news:13r4clsrubis65@corp.supernews.com...
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>> "tim" <TimJ@internet.com> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Saying this doesn't make it so. No matter how many times you say it.

Correct. The fact that it isn't circular argument makes it so, even if
no one ever says it. :-)

>>> It may or may not be right, but to express your views - and that is
>>> what they are - as dogmatic truths, is just going to annoy people.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> adult view. incorporating shades of gray.

If that is simply my opinion, then in what authority would a Christian
place their faith? How do they know who Jesus is? How do they know
that it is their faith that God requires? Tell me, without the Bible as
authority, how does one even *define* what a Christian is, let alone
be a Christian and have faith? Either supply such another authority,
or admit that it is not just my opinion that the Bible is the only one.

> Does not the fact that there are millions of people who hold similar views
> to yours, but concerning different books that say very different things,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> the truth.

That's not what I'm saying. I'm not so stupid as to think that unbelievers
put any credence in the Bible as an authority. When I use the Bible as
an authority, I am speaking to believers, who have no other authority
for their faith, even if they do not realize that.
```

```
On Wed, 13 Feb 2008 11:53:36 -0600, Judson McClendon wrote:

> If that is simply my opinion, then in what authority would a Christian
> place their faith? How do they know who Jesus is? How do they know
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

It is not easy. It's not easy to know the truth about anything. This
problem is made particularly difficult in a religious context because
religious people have not placed a lot of emphasis on the theory of
knowledge. 

How do you know a religious claim is true or not? There is not
a lot in religious literature about this. For example, the Taoist books say
that if you empty your mind of distractions and look deeply within you
will see the truth. They don't really address the question of dealing with
two people coming up with different answers to this question.

Others say certain books are the truth. Others that certain people speak
with infallible authority. These claims contradict each other.

Your assumption above is that there must be some source of authoritative
knowledge that one can easily refer to for answers. There just isn't as far
as I can tell.

Tim
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: You know you're a Christina when .. (was: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 74)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-13T10:49:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LcFsj.98974$L%6.74629@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6a823d8c-33fa-4b72-a94d-c16e75691d9c@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <LV8qj.107618$KG4.102857@fe09.news.easynews.com> <K0bqj.61182$k27.3644@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <ea5d6927-bc19-4474-8107-5a2ccf67e0f2@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <Bgqqj.67802$Mu4.58660@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <febf2a80-2a91-473d-ae22-fcdf02d8c052@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <qt%rj.29998$1f.15889@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <86b59539-4d7b-440c-bb83-d653e4fc11cd@e10g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> My mistake. So you admit to being a bad christian?

You said that I "claim".  I made no claim at all in that regard. :-)

I honestly and sincerely desire to please God and be obedient to Him.
But I will clearly state that I have many, many times failed to live up to
what the Bible would call "Godly conduct." Fortunately, God made
provision for when Christians sin (Romans 8, 1 John 1:9). :-)

>> > Others, who behave in a manner
>> > that you find an anathema and un-christian, also claim to be good
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> who believes in christ is at least as good a christian as you are.

Why can't you get it through your head that it *IS NOT* about "how
good" I am, but about how forgiving God is? I do not deserve God's
forgiveness, but neither does anyone else. If Adolph Hitler or Joseph
Stalin or Suddam Hussein or Jack the Ripper truly came to the point
of having faith in Jesus as their savior, then God would have forgiven
them as freely as He forgave me, and they would be just as acceptable
to God as any other Christian. God *gives* this to us as a free gift.
It is through God's grace (unmerited favor). All we do is accept it, we
*do not* earn it, in any form or fashion. I desire to do God's will in
order to please Him, not to be acceptable to Him. My faith in Jesus is
all that God requires, or accepts, to make me acceptable to Him. :-)

>> > Also: given that you have a brain and are capable of independant
>> > thought don't you think it strange that god expects you to swallow the
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> entered my mind (and my eyes were opened....)

That is between you and God. However, it is one's faith in Jesus Christ
that makes one a Christian, not going to church, or being raised in a
Christian family, or in doing good deeds, or in intellectually accepting
what one has been taught, no matter how true and accurate that teaching
may be. Belief is not faith. Faith requires belief, but faith is more than
mere intellectual belief (James 2:19).

>> > It seems to me that you are setting yourself up as judge and jury over
>> > other fellow christians. Where does the bible say as you reported
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> your arguments.

Behind "God's skirts" if you will. :-) And yes, God is my defense and
my refuge (Psalm 59:9,17, Psalm 91).

I would not have my beliefs without that quoted authority. That quoted
authority, the Bible, *defines* my beliefs. I willingly and eagerly read
the Bible for the purpose of knowing and believing what it says.

>> I'm not the arbiter of such things; God is the only Arbiter.
>
> Therefore you can not define what a good christian is because you can
> not know your god's mind. Smoking pot whilst reading the bible may
> well be acceptable behaviour to your god.

I only know God's mind as it is revealed in His Word the Bible, which
is why I quote the Bible, and by the Holy Spirit, Who gives witness to
the authenticity of the Bible (1 Corinthians 2:16).

Apparently it was acceptable, at least for a person who doesn't know
better. God deals with people according to where they are at the time.
My friend wasn't acceptable to God because he did or did not smoke
pot; he was acceptable to God because he placed his faith in Jesus
Christ. :-)
```

On 13 Feb, 16:49, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> > "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> You said that I "claim".  I made no claim at all in that regard. :-)

You have certainly passed judgement on others as to whether they are
good or bad christians.

>
> I honestly and sincerely desire to please God and be obedient to Him.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> to God as any other Christian. God *gives* this to us as a free gift.

I doubt that god would be happy to hear you advising people to sin now
and repent at their leisure for that is the way to the Kingdom.

> >> As I've said here before, the Bible didn't make much sense to me either,
> >> before I accepted Jesus and received the Holy Spirit. Unlike yourself,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> may be.

I did once believe in god, jesus, attend church, pray, did good deeds
and accepted all of the twaddle and propaganda of the christian world.
But I opened my mind and became inquisitive of the world and began
questioning the reality of fairies, kelpies, angels and deities.

My point was that you had said that 'Unlike yourself, I've been on
both sides of this fence, and on that basis, my witness should be more
credible'.

The operative word is SHOULD BE. When your god looks into your heart
all he will see is the black sin of pride and smugness. Then he'll
remember that you have squandered your free will (which isn't really
free as Pete points out) by slavishly following the bible. Following
the bible is not what coming to god is about.

> Belief is not faith. Faith requires belief, but faith is more than
> mere intellectual belief (James 2:19).
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> the authenticity of the Bible (1 Corinthians 2:16).

That is the bible self-justification that is so tightly circuitous as
to be worthless.

>
> Apparently it was acceptable, at least for a person who doesn't know
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Christ. :-)

So he could quite happily have continued smoking the pot and god would
not have been in one bit bothered.
```

On Wed, 13 Feb 2008 10:49:51 -0600, Judson McClendon wrote:
> If Adolph Hitler or Joseph
> Stalin or Suddam Hussein or Jack the Ripper truly came to the point of
> having faith in Jesus as their savior ...

Seeing you have invoked Godwin's law, you may be interested in this.

http://www.nobeliefs.com/nazis.htm?

Another baseless assumption: that Hitler was not a Christian.

All the evidence points to Hitler being a Christian. Not the sort of
Christian you would hope for of course.

No-one knows who Jack the Ripper was so we don't know about his or her
religious beliefs. Given the era, it is probable he or she was religious,
as most people were at that time.

As far as I know Stalin was not a Christian. Actually what Hitler and
Stalin did have in common was abusive parents. Spare the rod and spoil the
child and all that... Technical question - is that one of the Old
Testament edicts that were swept aside by the New Covenant?

 Tim

> Judson McClendon wrote:
>> If Adolph Hitler or Joseph
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

I know Hitler did use Christian trappings, apparently to appease the
Christian community. But who am I to say what Hitler's true heart
was at the time of his death? Only God knows (and Hitler, of course).

Just because someone says "I'm a Christian" does not make them
a Christian. I could say "I'm a gazelle" but that wouldn't make me
one. Faith in Jesus makes one a Christian; nothing else.

There's no "swept aside" there's "fulfilled" (Matthew 5:17).

Possibly. Most people who think of themselves as Christians would disagree.

Jews, of course, don't have that problem. One is a Jew if born of a Jewish 
mother or converted to Judiasm according to Jewish law, like that "colored 
singer." Other famous Jews include Marilyn Monroe, Elizabeth Taylor, and, 
possibly, Madonna. (Madonna is supposedly studying the Kabbalah. I genuinely 
suspect she's studying mock Kabbalah. Celebreties are so gullible it is a 
mitzvah to take their money.)

Muslims, too, have an easy definition. Verbal acceptance of Allah and 
Mohammed as His prophet is all it takes.

You can't be an ex-Jew or an ex-Muslim. For those Jews who subscribe to 
another faith, the rest of the tribe simply shakes their head. For a Muslim 
who accepts another religion, for a short time he is known as an apostate, 
then he is known as deceased.

--- I love Jewish jokes
"Papa, I'm getting married!"
"Married! Mazel Tov! Who's the lucky girl?"
"Rosie O'Grady."
"Rosie O'Grady? A shicksa!? You're marrying a goyim?! What's wrong with a 
nice Jewish girl?"
"Papa, you know how Jewish girls are. Always a pain here, a complaint 
there."
"So? You think a shicksa won't get a headache? She maybe won't get a 
backache?"
"Of course, but with a shicksa, who cares."

---
Said the rabbi to the hippie: "Of course you can pray in jeans in a T-shirt. 
You just can't pray HERE in jeans and a T-shirt."

---
"Did you hear the one about the two Jews who ran into each other..."
"Hold it. I'm tired of your Jewish jokes! All the time Jew this, Jew that. 
Don't you have any jokes that don't involve Jews?"
...
"Uh, yeah. These two Chinamen run into each other..."
"That's better! Continue."
"... and one says to the other: 'Mazel Tov on your son's bar mitzvah...' "

- **References:** `<8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6a823d8c-33fa-4b72-a94d-c16e75691d9c@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <a3kjq319a2p9q7a28j6v54rqrrketnmr6c@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> believe that no parts of the Old Testament apply.

The Old Testament is just as true as it ever was, and certainly most
of it still applies. But the Mosaic Law, which required false prophets
be put to death (and was the issue I was responding to), has been
superceded by the New Covenant of Grace. The Mosaic Law was much
more than a standard of personal conduct, it also defined how the
government (a theocracy) would function. For example, the Biblical
principle "an eye for an eye" was never about personal conduct, but
about judicial punishment, which is very clear in every context in
which it appears. In more modern law, a similar principle is stated
"let the punishment fit the crime."
```

- **Message-ID:** `<13qksqppr523ge2@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <jhveq3h3tshepijcjhvi9jdsectjtf5tlv@4ax.com> <lNLpj.66139$Mu4.33329@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5d58b7ac-fb3d-4582-a165-b505f2b16088@i7g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <l1Opj.66220$Mu4.38190@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6a823d8c-33fa-4b72-a94d-c16e75691d9c@e25g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <BA7qj.61089$k27.25328@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <a3kjq319a2p9q7a28j6v54rqrrketnmr6c@4ax.com> <rYkqj.67284$Mu4.17205@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> "let the punishment fit the crime."

Oh, so much more than that. Mosaic law governed contracts, torts, planting 
of beans, evidentiary sufficiency, marriage, inheritance, adoption, the 
conduct of war, the administration of peace, the use of weapons, testimony, 
alliances and treaties, animal cruelty, theory of law, commerce, weights and 
measures, travel, admiralty, monopolies and fair trade, liabilities, 
harvesting and storing foodstuffs, taxes, road building, public order and 
safety, oaths, and so on. The entire corpus of human interaction.

The Talmud is roughly equivalent to the United States Code. Plus the Church 
Canons. Plus Supreme Court opinions. It's a lot. It continues.

For example, the Hebrew scriptures list seven birds that cannot be eaten. 
But what is permitted? The rabbis puzzled on this a great deal. They looked 
at the list trying to determine if any of the prohibited birds had something 
in common from which they could extrapolate. Some of the proscribed birds 
were scavengers, but not all. Some had two toes forward and two toes back. 
But not all. Finally, the rabbis said we can't figure out exactly what God 
wants so we'll fall back on the known "clean" birds offered in sacrifice: 
doves, quail, duck, etc.

For TWO THOUSAND years, there was not a single Jew on the planet who was 
least unsure of which birds were kosher.

Jews came early to the new world (The navigator on Columbus' voyage was 
Jewish). There they found - wait for it now - the TURKEY! Letters went back 
and forth... you can imagine. Finally it was decided that the turkey was 
more like a big chicken than a flightless vulture. Jews can eat turkey. Yum.

Another few hundred years pass...

The penguin(!).

Oh well. I don't think any Jews have started the ball rolling again, but you 
never know what'll turn up.
```

```
"HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> continues.

What you say is only partially correct, for the Talmud is not divinely
inspired Scripture, but a collection of rabinic writings. The Tahakh is
divinely inspired. Even the Jews do not place them on the same plane.
The Mosaic Law was dictated by God and recorded by the pen of Moses in
the Torah, Genesis, Exodus, Numbers, Leviticus and Deuteronomy, the
first five books of the Bible. Unlike most Scripture, the text of
Genesis suggests that at least some, if not all, of Genesis was
literally dictated to Moses by God in direct conversations on Mt. Sinai
and in Moses tent. The original ten commandments were literally
transcribed by God in Mt. Sinai. Jesus made it clear that not even the
tiniest part of a letter of the Mosaic Law would pass away, but that
He came to fulfill the requirements of the Mosaic Law, on our behalf
(Matthew 5:17,18). Because Jesus fulfilled the requirements of the
Mosaic Law, we are relieved of that requirement. *All* of it. However,
some of the Mosaic Law refers to rules of conduct defining Godly
people behave (e.g. ten commandments, honesty, acting in love, etc.).
Because Christians desire to please God, we behave in accordance with
those principles. Offering sacrifices, eating certain foods, observing
feasts and holy days are clearly defined in the New Testament as no
longer relevant. That doesn't mean we should act against the Mosaic
Law by being dishonest or cruel. We are encouraged to do good works
and will receive reward for them. But they don't buy our salvation.
```

On Wed, 6 Feb 2008 11:40:03 -0800 (PST), Alistair
<alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>Nice try but no. I believe that they were all opportunistic frauds.
>And that includes JC.

Philip Jose Farmer's _Riverworld_ series of novels has a planet with
everybody who ever lived.   In it he created a Jesus who was a good
man, interested in finding and talking with Moses, but got in trouble
when people found out who he was.   The more interesting part of this
was how it really didn't matter what Jesus was to the people who
*knew* their Truths.   (There were some old fairy tales with Jesus
coming down from heaven with similar results).

Sure there are frauds in religion, but other categories include True
Believers and people who were really good - who were elevated by
others into icons for their needs.     An example is Karl Marx who to
some people was a demon, and to others an angel.    But he was just a
man.
```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

I never suspected otherwise. ;-)

There are two viewpoints from which to assess the validity of the Bible,
or any other system of belief. One is to assess it based on external
evidence, and the other is to assess it based on internal consistency or
lack of same. The above argument was from the latter viewpoint, which
is why I stipulated the postulate "Assuming God did create the universe
in 6 days". This should, assuming intellectual honesty, of course, allow
even atheists to engage in a fair discussion of such Biblical issues. :-)

- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <l0Kpj.80164$rc2.67831@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <60qetmF1rpjmhU1@mid.individual.net> <aGZpj.60775$k27.7879@bignews2.bellsouth.net>`

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:aGZpj.60775$k27.7879@bignews2.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
> lack of same.

Really? Such limited black and white dogmatic thinking.

What about a combination of both?

What about probabilities and ranges within both?

What if one contradicts or supports the other?

What if both of these measurements contradict each other?

What about measuring the belief against similar beliefs and observations?

There are MANY ways to assess what we believe, and evaluate it. Usually this 
results in small adjustments until we arrive at a working set of postulates 
that are adequate for living on Earth in Human Society.

...Or, you could simply accept the most unlikely fairy story that cannot 
stand any kind of scrutiny (internal or external), because it is "Holy Writ" 
and you'll burn in a lake of fire after you die, if you don't.

Not really a hard choice for people capable of independent thought and 
observation. (It is much more difficult for people who are raised in 
ignorance; fortunately, they are becoming fewer in Western societies, at 
least, and we hope eventually the whole world...)

> The above argument was from the latter viewpoint, which
> is why I stipulated the postulate "Assuming God did create the universe
> in 6 days". This should, assuming intellectual honesty, of course, allow
> even atheists to engage in a fair discussion of such Biblical issues. :-)

Neither of the above options is sensible even if God DID create the world in 
6 days. That was my point and I accepted your postulate.

There would be no reason for God to "simulate" a "History", any more than it 
would make sense for Adam to see Proxima Centauri before 4 years had passed. 
Both of your "options" are stupid and myopic. If God created the universe in 
6 days he either did so with or without a "history" built into it. As both 
options are ridiculous, we can only conclude that the Universe was not 
created in 6 days, or the accounts of the 6 day creation are inaccurate.

Criticizing the Creation account on ANY basis is never asinine, although it 
is probably pointless.

We might as well argue about how many angels can stand on a pinhead. I 
believe the last time anyone did this, the argument went on for some 300 
years...

(Does anybody know or care what the final count was...?)

Pete.
```

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
> working set of postulates that are adequate for living on Earth in Human Society.

I'm talking about viewpoints, you are talking about conclusions - apples
and oranges. But, anything to take issue with God or Creation, eh? :-)

> ...Or, you could simply accept the most unlikely fairy story that cannot stand any kind of scrutiny (internal or external), 
> because it is "Holy Writ" and you'll burn in a lake of fire after you die, if you don't.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> in 6 days, or the accounts of the 6 day creation are inaccurate.

Fine, then please illustrate a scenario where God could have created an
operating "in balance" universe without the appearance of age. :-)

> Criticizing the Creation account on ANY basis is never asinine, although it is probably pointless.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> (Does anybody know or care what the final count was...?)

Only an idiot. And your evidence that such stupid human activity is in
some way derived from the Bible is ..., just what exactly? Is there a
reference to angelic size and density in the Bible somewhere that I
missed? Where does Scripture imply that we should be engaged in such a
discussion?

Don't blame God and Scripture for human stupidity. The Bible says we're
like sheep. Living in New Zealand, surely you know that sheep are stupid
and obnoxious animals, capable of getting lost within 100 feet of a herd
they can't see. Even Jesus compared His followers to sheep. Jesus didn't
say they were smart, he said they would hear His voice and follow Him
(John 10:27). One of the beauties of God's plan of salvation is that
even children and idiots can understand and accept it. Doesn't prevent
them from still being children and idiots. Are you suggesting there
aren't also idiots among atheists? :-)

Again, you present a typical straw man misdirection. Would you like me to
trot out the most ridiculous argument ever conducted by any atheist, as
if that was representative of atheist thought, as you just did? Are you
so bereft of valid points that you must resort to such tawdry tricks?
Such tactics illustrate exactly what I've been saying all along: You
aren't the least bit interested in reasoned discussion, and you don't
care what evidence may be contrary to your viewpoint, because you
trivialize and dismiss it, even when you present no evidence or logical
argument to the contrary, as you just did above. It's not just that
you're convinced of your beliefs, it's that you have such contempt for
Christian beliefs. Where do you suppose that emotion comes from? If you
think we Christians are so stupid and clueless, why does that incite
contempt, rather than compassion? In my case, though you strongly
disagree with my beliefs, I think you will have to admit that I am
trying to present and defend them honestly. I suggest the source of your
(and other Athiests like Alistair) emotional contempt is spiritual, just
as the Bible declares. (John 15:18,19) :-)
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:UM%pj.60858$k27.24968@bignews2.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[65 more quoted lines elided]…
> and oranges. But, anything to take issue with God or Creation, eh? :-)

No Judson, It isn't "anything to take issue with God or Creation". I am not 
a hostile atheist seeking to attack God (for the most part He and I get 
along fine). Rather, it is the nonsense that you persist in posting here, 
fired by your evangelistic zeal, that pisses me off.

It isn't even personal (although I have been very disappointed by some of 
your actions on this), it's just you grabbing a free speech forum and using 
it as a soap box. If you did it in a park, I'd pass right by and not even 
heckle you.

I'll gladly debate IT with you without animosity or rancour, I'll even do 
the same on any topic, controversial or not, including Religion. But you 
have shown that you are not capable of even looking at counter arguments and 
all we get is the same repetitive stream of rubbish, over and over. Frankly, 
it is tiresome.

I shouldn't even bother responding, but even when I don't, you still don't 
desist.

You can't help it; you are a driven man.

There you are, sword in hand, leading the charge for the Christian Soldiers, 
never even realizing that the enemy has gone to lunch.

It's sad.

>
>> ...Or, you could simply accept the most unlikely fairy story that cannot 
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> operating "in balance" universe without the appearance of age. :-)

Why? He didn't, and the "age" in the existing Universe is because  it is 
old.
>
>> Criticizing the Creation account on ANY basis is never asinine, although 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> some way derived from the Bible is ..., just what exactly?

I wasn't saying such activity derived from the Bible, (when I say "Holy 
Writ" I am referring to ANY Holy scripture), but now that you mention it...

1. The Roman Catholic church (a major Christian Sect) claims scripture as a 
basis for its catechism and belief.
2. The Roman Catholic church could not exist if the Bible had not been 
written.
3. The Roman Catholic church spent several hundred years discussing how many 
angels could stand on a pin head.

THEREFORE: The discussion was indirectly a result of the Bible having been 
written.

Personally, I don't think they're stupid; I just don't pretend to understand 
why they think it was important.

On the other hand, some of the statements you are making here I think are 
worse than stupid. It surprises me because I don't think you're a stupid 
person.

> Is there a
> reference to angelic size and density in the Bible somewhere that I
> missed? Where does Scripture imply that we should be engaged in such a
> discussion?

Don't ask me, ask the Pope...
>
> Don't blame God and Scripture for human stupidity. The Bible says we're
> like sheep. Living in New Zealand, surely you know that sheep are stupid
> and obnoxious animals, capable of getting lost within 100 feet of a herd
> they can't see.

Oh Judson. Living in NZ I know that sheep are far from stupid. Neither are 
they obnoxious (in fact many Kiwi kids keep pet lambs and become very 
attached ot them).. A couple of weeks ago, while entertaining a visitor from 
overseas, I took her to an entertainment at a place called the Agrodome in 
Rotorua. They had displays of sheep and dogs (amongst many other rural 
things) and there were very intelligent trained sheep there that were 
performing on cue without being coerced by dogs or humans. The sheep 
obviously enjoyed the performance and were available for people to pet and 
be photographed with afterwards. (A bit like humans getting autographs and 
being photographed with celebrities). Everyone thoroughly enjoyed the 
performance and my friend commented afterwards:" I had no idea that sheep 
were so smart...".

Again, without doing any homework you jump to a conclusion.

You believe people are wicked and sinful. The Bible says people are like 
sheep. You therefore decide that sheep must be stupid and obnoxious.

How wrong do you have to be before you are able to recognize it?

Some (uninformed) people think that pigs are stupid filthy animals too.

Doesn't mean it is the case.


>Even Jesus compared His followers to sheep. Jesus didn't
> say they were smart, he said they would hear His voice and follow Him
> (John 10:27).

Well, he was pretty wrong about that. An overwhelming majority of the peopl 
on this planet DIDN'T do that.

>One of the beauties of God's plan of salvation is that
> even children and idiots can understand and accept it.

You could remove "even" from that sentence and improve its accuracy many 
fold...


> Doesn't prevent
> them from still being children and idiots. Are you suggesting there
> aren't also idiots among atheists? :-)

I neither know nor care. I'm not an evangelist for atheism. What other 
people believe is of little concern to me, until they start killing people 
who disagree with them, or spend many hours (and thousands of words) 
spouting it onto  what has to be for the most part, stony ground.

Even Jesus knew when NOT to waste time.

>
> Again, you present a typical straw man misdirection. Would you like me to
> trot out the most ridiculous argument ever conducted by any atheist, as
> if that was representative of atheist thought, as you just did? Are you
> so bereft of valid points that you must resort to such tawdry tricks?

So we won't be discussing angels and pinheads then...

> Such tactics illustrate exactly what I've been saying all along: You
> aren't the least bit interested in reasoned discussion, and you don't
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Christian beliefs.

That is horseshit. I have great respect for Christian beliefs, as I do for 
most of the World's major religions. I have little respect for zealots, of 
whatever denomination.

> Where do you suppose that emotion comes from?

I'm not emotional about it, just irritated by the replacement of logical 
debate with repetition of Holy Writ. Over and over. The ultimate argument 
from Authority, but if I challenge it, I'm hostile to God and Christianity.


> If you
> think we Christians are so stupid and clueless,

I DON'T think Christians are stupid and clueless; I DO think YOU are 
behaving stupidly and cluelessly.


>why does that incite
> contempt, rather than compassion?

It doesn't.

I have contempt for your apology for an argument. For you personally, I feel 
sadness.

And don't think for one moment that only Christians have a monopoly on 
compassion.

>In my case, though you strongly
> disagree with my beliefs, I think you will have to admit that I am
> trying to present and defend them honestly.

No. You are trying to present them with a hidden agenda. That isn't honest. 
Your zeal REQUIRES you to do this to help ensure your place in Heaven. I 
thought for a while you were honest, but after the way you behaved on losing 
your challenge,  I realize now that is not the case.

As for defending them, no-one would be attacking them if you weren't pushing 
them so hard. I am more than happy to agree to disagree with people like 
yourself, recognizing that religious belief is a personal and private right 
for all humans. But you can't leave it at that. No, you are driven. It's 
sad.

> I suggest the source of your
> (and other Athiests like Alistair) emotional contempt is spiritual, just
> as the Bible declares. (John 15:18,19) :-)

I don't hate you. I hate your stupid repetition of  Holy Writ as an 
argument.

I refute your suggestion (Proverbs 17: 20, 22, 27 - 28).

Pete.
```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> As for defending them, no-one would be attacking them if you
> weren't pushing them so hard.

I'll only respond to that one statement, which is demonstrably false.
If you go back and look, you will see that I'm not the one who started
discussing religion in these threads. I and Christianity were being
bashed repeatedly for days, maybe weeks, in the threads on Global
Warming, when I had said nothing about religion, and kept insisting
that, as far as I'm concerned, the GW argument has nothing to do with
religion. I also insisted I did not want to get into a religious
discussion here, because I know from experience that people like
yourself are so hostile toward Chriatianity that you will overlook a
hundred posts bashing Christianity, and always wind up jumping on
those that defend it, as you're doing now. I often disagree with
other's statements, and maybe their motives, but I've never challenged
anyone's freedom to post whatever they want. In all this time, I have
only posted religous content in response to posts by others containing
religous content. Many of my posts have been in response to questions,
and I often do not respond to posts made to disagree with my comments.
Anyone can verify this by examining my posts here. At what point did I
become the one causing all this, and why am I the only one you are
taking to task? Or did I miss your posts taking to task the others
posting religious content? Please point them out if I did. If others
are free to make posts with religious content, am I not free to
respond in kind?

Believe me, I will be happy to drop comments on religion here. But
don't expect me to remin silent forever when I and my beliefs are
being openly attacked again and again. Fair enough?
```

>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'll only respond to that one statement, which is demonstrably false.

Ah, so all my other statements which you snipped are either true or unable 
to be demonstrated to be false... good.

> If you go back and look, you will see that I'm not the one who started
> discussing religion in these threads.

If you go back only as far as the opening paragraph to this post, you will 
see I made no statements about who started what; only that by pushing them 
so hard you are inviting even patient and reasonable people (like me :-)) to 
attack them.

> I and Christianity were being
> bashed repeatedly for days, maybe weeks, in the threads on Global
> Warming, when I had said nothing about religion, and kept insisting
> that, as far as I'm concerned, the GW argument has nothing to do with
> religion.

Not by me.

> I also insisted I did not want to get into a religious
> discussion here, because I know from experience that people like
> yourself are so hostile toward Chriatianity that you will overlook a
> hundred posts bashing Christianity, and always wind up jumping on
> those that defend it, as you're doing now.

I repeat (at the risk of becoming as tiresome as you...) that I am NOT 
hostile to Christianity or any other religious belief. I'm hostile to having 
your view of it played over and over like a broken record, in the mistaken 
assumption that this will somehow make it truer. You don't debate; you 
simply quote scripture and platitudes.

> I often disagree with
> other's statements, and maybe their motives, but I've never challenged
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> respond in kind?

It's an open forum. You can post whatever you like. But that's no reason to 
hijack it to your own agenda.
>
> Believe me, I will be happy to drop comments on religion here. But
> don't expect me to remin silent forever when I and my beliefs are
> being openly attacked again and again. Fair enough?

Everybody has the right to defend what they believe in. I don't agree with 
one jot or tittle of what you've been posting, but I'll defend to the death 
your right to post it. It isn't about your right to freedom of speech, which 
I feel just as passionate about as you do about your religion.

"why am I the only one you are  taking to task?"...

My problem here is that you behaved dishonourably by not doing what you said 
you would do, and you accused me of "wriggling out" of a trap you set, which 
you screwed up yourself by not paying attention to detail, in your glassy 
eyed fervour...

I have never "wriggled out" of anything...I don't do "wriggling"... I 
answered your stupid questions as honestly as I could, and you never even 
acknowledged the answers I gave you or that you had been wrong in your 
assumptions about my motives, because you didn't get the answers you wanted. 
The actual answers did not match the profile of me you had already decided. 
(Even in this post I have had to remind you again that I am NOT 
anti-Christian or anti-Religion (although I admit, I'd prefer the world got 
over it...). If I say something that doesn't fit your prejudice, you simply 
ignore it.

Even then, I would have let it go, but you persisted.

There are other Christians in this forum who don't get the same treatment 
from me, and for whom I have utmost respect (even if I don't agree with 
their beliefs, necessarily).

Why is that?

Because they post honestly, don't evangelize, and don't take every 
opportunity to quote reams of Holy Writ in support of their arguments, as if 
that is going to persuade anyone (other than already committed Christians). 
They therefore deserve respect. You don't do that (when it comes to 
religion) so you don't get my respect in that field. (I am careful not to 
let my disdain for you in this area, spill into other areas, where I DO 
respect your judgement, whether I agree with it or not.)

I never made any secret of how disappointed I was by your actions. But you 
don't care.

Fair enough. You certainly don't need my approval.

But then don't bleat about me picking on you.:-)

You knew the deal; I was clear at the outset. Keep posting tiresome rubbish 
and I'll keep jumping on it.

Welcome to Usenet.

Pete.
```

Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:aGZpj.60775$k27.7879@bignews2.bellsouth.net...
> >> True. Assuming God did create the universe in 6 days, which is more
> >> logical?
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>

<snip>

> There are two viewpoints from which to assess the validity of the Bible,
> or any other system of belief. One is to assess it based on external
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> --


You give as a postulate "Assuming God did create the universe in 6 days".
The word postulate means something like "assume without proof  as a basis
for reasoning; take for granted".  Taking something as a postulate does not
say anything about its truth.

My postulate is "Assuming God did not create the universe in 6 days".  That
is allowable: I'm not asserting it as a fact.  This should, assuming
intellectual honesty, of course, allow even Christain theists to engage in a
fair discussion of such Biblical issues.

Actually, these two postulates have a bag of assumptions in them which make
them a poor basis for discussion.  Is there a God?  What is a god?  Does the
universe exist independently of such a being?  Has the universe always
existed - i.e., did it have to be created at all?  What powers does this God
have?  Is this God consistent?

PL
```

> Nope - it just a simple logical deduction.  Adam was created as an

This does raise an interesting question though. Would Adam have had the

popped into existence on 24 Feb 4004 BC, traveling in just such directions
and numbers as to make it look like things were out there millions and
billions of years ago. Someone or something went to a *lot* of trouble to
make the universe look older than some books say it is. Why?

Tim

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 61)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-05T07:27:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mvZpj.60768$k27.20038@bignews2.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <13qft43dckur7a4@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"tim Josling" <tejgcc_nospam@westnet.com.au> wrote:
> LX-i wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> have left.

Not really. Out of over 200 odd methods to date the Earth, only a
very few indicate ages over perhaps a million years. Consider the
amount of nickel-iron from meteorites. Even at today's rate of
meteorite falls, if the Earth were 4 billion years old, there would
be some 70 or 80 feet of nickel-iron covering the surface. It is not
to be found. Look at the Moon's surface. They expected to find deep
seas of micro-dust from billions of years of exposure to radiation,
meteorites and heat/cold cycles, yet it simply isn't there. Consider
the salinity of the oceans. We know how much salt is being poured
into the world's oceans currently, and it is not likely that the
ground was less salty before being leached of salt from thousands
(or billions) of years of rainfall, yet if the earth were over about
10,000 years old, the oceans today would be like the Dead Sea.
Consider the ubiquitous polonium spheres in granite. There is no
reasonable explanation for them, except that the Earth was formed,
in place, essentially instantaneously. There are obvious human
artifacts, like rock hammers with wooden handles, found embedded in
seams of coal; I've seen them. There are literally hundreds of such
simple, obvious, common sense arguments for a young Earth and
Biblical Creation. But they are typically ignored and swept under
the rug by those who simply don't want to accept it. Instead, they
propose preposterous, convoluted, mind-bogglingly stupid
"explanations" (like punctuated equilibrium), and choose to believe
those instead. If you don't believe that, just observe the long and
complex "explanations" that will undoubtedly be posted to refute
the clear and simple things in this paragraph, all of which will be
assumptions, of course, that can't be experimentally verified as
true. Radioisotope dating methods are so error prone they are
practically useless. Lava flows that formed in front of cameras,
and known to be a few days old, have been radioisotope dated to
thousands of years old. If I had a ruler that couldn't reliably tell
the difference between an inch and five miles, I would throw it away.
The rest of the world would too, if it didn't give them an excuse to
ignore much simpler and more logical explanations that refute their
preferred world view.

> We look at the night sky and photons impinge on our eyes that
> popped into existence on 24 Feb 4004 BC, traveling in just such directions
> and numbers as to make it look like things were out there millions and
> billions of years ago. Someone or something went to a *lot* of trouble to
> make the universe look older than some books say it is. Why?

Creation of the universe was "a *lot* of trouble". Do you think the
God Who could do that would have trouble filling in such tiny details?
It's not a matter of deception, it's a matter of creating a universe
*in balance*. If God only created eggs, how would they be hatched
without a chicken to nest them? Adam was created as an adult so he
could take care of himself. God isn't an idiot. He simply created a
harmonious universe in balance. Can you suggest how He could have
done that *without* the "appearance of age?"

God also took care to tell us about Creation, so claims that God is
"deceptive" are pointedly absurd.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 62)_

- **From:** Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2008-02-05T15:33:51-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a45d128b-c091-4e78-ab4e-76d76d1da8f4@m34g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <YOqdnSH_foFvxjranZ2dnUVZ_r3inZ2d@comcast.com> <13qft43dckur7a4@corp.supernews.com> <mvZpj.60768$k27.20038@bignews2.bellsouth.net>`

```
On 5 Feb, 13:27, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

> > The universe certainly seems to have such indications of greater age. For
> > example, we can see things that were not there when the photons would
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> very few indicate ages over perhaps a million years.

I would be interested in knowing the list of 200 methods of dating the
earth. And how few of them give an age beyond 6000 years (all of them
excepting for an analysis of the bible).

Given that the stars consume hydrogen and produce helium and other
elements. Would you mind explaining where and how uranium is produced
and how long it takes to make that particular element.

> Consider the
> amount of nickel-iron from meteorites. Even at today's rate of
> meteorite falls, if the Earth were 4 billion years old, there would
> be some 70 or 80 feet of nickel-iron covering the surface.

The oldest rocks on the planet's surface date to approx 3.8 billion
years ago. Any iron falling upon the surface would be oxidised and the
rust washed away. Such rust has accumulated as iron oxide deposits in
rock strata and also been passed through the crust (via subduction) to
form the core.

> It is not
> to be found. Look at the Moon's surface. They expected to find deep
> seas of micro-dust from billions of years of exposure to radiation,
> meteorites and heat/cold cycles, yet it simply isn't there.

I remember being told that the moon was made of green cheese. Just
because someone idly moots that the surface is so doesn't make it so.
That is why we have scientists - to discover the truth. Without them
we would all being worshipping midionite storm gods.


> Consider
> the salinity of the oceans. We know how much salt is being poured
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 10,000 years old, the oceans today would be like the Dead Sea.

Only if the salts were not deposited and removed from the oceans by
geologic processes. Where do you think rock salt comes from?

> Consider the ubiquitous polonium spheres in granite. There is no
> reasonable explanation for them, except that the Earth was formed,
> in place, essentially instantaneously.

A search of wikipedia does not show polonium spheres as an article but
does mention polonium as a natural element with a half life up to 103
years. Polonium can be formed by the natural decay of radioactive
elements or by bombarding bismuth with radiation. Granite contains
radioactive elements so what is your problem?

> There are obvious human
> artifacts, like rock hammers with wooden handles, found embedded in
> seams of coal; I've seen them.

What would be really convincing would be a dinosaur skeleton holding a
placard with "End nuclear testing now!". A discarded rock hammer is
just that.

> There are literally hundreds of such
> simple, obvious, common sense arguments for a young Earth and
> Biblical Creation.

The only argument in favour of a young earth is the bible and that has
been soundly repudiated.

> But they are typically ignored and swept under
> the rug by those who simply don't want to accept it.

Quite right too.

> Instead, they
> propose preposterous, convoluted, mind-bogglingly stupid
> "explanations" (like punctuated equilibrium), and choose to believe
> those instead.

So, you would rather believe an improbable and far fetched convoluted
explanation lacking any verifiable scientifically derived evidence in
support, as written in the bible, and all that that implies than the
simple truth: the bible is wrong and your entire philosophy is based
on a handful of fallacious beliefs.

> If you don't believe that, just observe the long and
> complex "explanations" that will undoubtedly be posted to refute
> the clear and simple things in this paragraph,

Nothing long and complex. You start with one simple concept:
theorise, experiment, deduce the truth. Then you take your
observations and build upon it. The only simpler concept is to believe
verbatim every word of a work of fiction.

> all of which will be
> assumptions, of course, that can't be experimentally verified as
> true. Radioisotope dating methods are so error prone they are
> practically useless.

If they are practically useless then that implies that you believe
that in certain circumstances you find them useful. Please explain why
you choose to dismiss the majority of radioisotope measurements in
favour of the handful that you accept. Note - if the rock hammers to
which you referred were genuine then their ages could be verified by
radioisotope dating. Ironic isn't it?

> Lava flows that formed in front of cameras,
> and known to be a few days old, have been radioisotope dated to
> thousands of years old.

Geologic processes, outside of the bible, take millions of years.
Radioisotopes within those rocks would have been formed before the
rock cooled at the surface.

> If I had a ruler that couldn't reliably tell
> the difference between an inch and five miles, I would throw it away.

If you needed a ruler to differentiate between an inch and a mile then
you you would be an ignoramus.

>
> Creation of the universe was "a *lot* of trouble". Do you think the
> God Who could do that would have trouble filling in such tiny details?

Presumably the bible can explain the polonium spheres that you
mention? I just did a search of the bible and find no mention of
polonium.
```
