#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL - Is it hard to learn (by hp3kpro BJLPA@hotmail.com)

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <mmattias@talsystems.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-10T15:16:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WyErj.10870$Ej5.4650@newssvr29.news.prodigy.net>`
- **References:** `<b1838$47ae632d$6214dca7$31069@ALLTEL.NET>`

```
"SeaSideSam" <SeaSideSam@TheBeach> wrote in message 
news:b1838$47ae632d$6214dca7$31069@ALLTEL.NET...
> brian.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> regular cobol.  and what planet have you been on????

????

COBOL is COBOL.  The whole idea behind third-generation computer languages 
is that they use the same source-level statements and verbs and functions 
regardless of hardware, operating system or compiler publisher.

Sure, there are differences in how you edit source code and compile/link 
your programs, and different hardware/operating systems offer different 
exploitable capabilties; but IMNSHO if you can create applications using 
"brand X" COBOL but not using "brand Y" COBOL you are stretching the truth 
when you claim to be a COBOL programmer.
```

## Thread: Position to be filled — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### Position to be filled

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-11T05:13:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PPQrj.137145$X76.15851@fe08.news.easynews.com>`

```
This is "not a troll" but I do NOT know the rates (or any details whatsoever). 
However, one of the other "lists" that I follow included the following:

"My employer Microsoft is looking to fill some technical specialist positions in 
our EMEA region (Europe, Middle East and Africa) for people qualified in 
Mainframe Migration to the Microsoft Windows platform. This is often an area 
involving legacy COBOL applications. I thought I would post here to see if 
anyone over the pond is interested. There will be lots of travel most likely, 
but you can reside about anywhere in Europe. Qualifications are understanding of 
legacy mainframe applications and Microsoft technology stack (e.g. Windows and 
.NET). If anyone is interested, please let me know and I will hook you up."

  ***

If anyone is interested in pursuing this, send me an (off-list) note and I will 
forward it to the person who posted this.  I can "vouch" for his credibility 
even if I don't know anything about the actual position(s)>
```

<a id="t-a29ecac26e"></a>
## Thread: Re compile+link Fujitsu Linux (by charles.goodman@bell.ca) (by William M. Klein wmklein@nospam.netcom.com) — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### re: Re compile+link Fujitsu Linux (by charles.goodman@bell.ca) (by William M. Klein wmklein@nospam.netcom.com)

- **From:** "SeaSideSam" <SeaSideSam@TheBeach>
- **Date:** 2008-02-11T02:56:38-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d857c$47b00db2$62149d43$20271@ALLTEL.NET>`

```
bill.  thanks for the info on vmware.  downloaded it.  installed it.  checked it out.  uninstalled it.  i don't have the time or patience (sp) required to make it work.

charles.  thanks for the info on the pcs.  to answer your question, there is no connection between vmware and ibm's vm beyond 'vm' meaning 'virtual machine' in both cases.

once upon a time this 17-year-old (+41) was an ibm mainframe techsupport guru, and when i discovered vm i thought i had died and gone to heaven.  the short version of vm history is that ibm german software engineers got tired of doing the same thing all us users was doing so they developed vm to make their life easier.  an in-house product.  i heard about ibm/vm from an ibm/se working out of the tech center in denver, and had to buy him steaks and lots of scotch in order to get him to show vm to me.  three years later in san francisco ibm finally broke down and let their customers buy it.  didn't require much memory, was easy to configure, hard to screw up, and ran fast.  and solved a lot of headaches for the techies.... me most of all.  microsucks hasn't the talent or the interest in making our lives better.  before a doc screwed with my brain i was toying with the idea of writting a vm/os myself (machine language and assembly background).  history.

again.  thanks to the both of you for the info.

it's 0245 here, and i'm up chasing racoons off the roof of my house.  time to say good night.


--------------=  Posted using GrabIt  =----------------
------=  Binary Usenet downloading made easy =---------
-=  Get GrabIt for free from http://www.shemes.com/  =-
```

```
On Mon, 11 Feb 2008 02:56:38 -0600, "SeaSideSam" <SeaSideSam@TheBeach> wrote:

>  the short version of vm history is that ibm german software engineers got tired of doing the same thing all us users was doing so they developed vm to make their life easier.

CMS originally stood for Cambridge Monitor System, after the Massachusetts city where it
was developed.  For the history of VM, CP/CMS, CPC, Project MAC, CP-40, CP-67, Lincoln
Labs, CSS, IDC, CSC and IBM politics, see

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_CP/CMS
```

<a id="t-3309baca43"></a>
## Thread: The "One True Way" — 51 msgs

### The "One True Way"

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2008-02-12T19:14:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13r4djktdnvf970@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I am very, very dismayed at some of the current threads. We have the "One 
True Way" to faith  and salvation in God, we have the "One True Way" to link 
programs.  I thought that computer programmers were more flexible than this. 
Because the machines are so inflexible computer programmers must make up for 
it by being more flexible. IIRC I read that in Weinburg's " The Psychology 
of Computer Programming" but it was a long while ago so I may be wrong.

I believe in a multitude of ways.  I believe that Richard or anyone else 
should link his programs any damn way that works for them.  As the written 
word and the written Bible are relatively modern inventions, I believe that 
people that never had a chance to ever read the Bible or to ever hear about 
Jesus should have a way to salvation, and I believe they do because I 
believe that God is just, and if they have no chance at salvation then that 
seems extremely unjust to me. I can't tell you what those ways are but I 
trust in God  and I will leave it up to Him.

I am certain Judson can quote scripture telling me I am wrong. I guess he 
can just be very glad that he was not born an indian or of some other poor, 
unfortunate race of people who are all condemned to hell forever. Sort of 
like winning the ultimate lottery via birth.
```

#### ↳ Re: The "One True Way"

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-02-13T13:56:10+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<61eth9F1v4dnrU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<13r4djktdnvf970@corp.supernews.com>`

```


"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
news:13r4djktdnvf970@corp.supernews.com...
>I am very, very dismayed at some of the current threads. We have the "One 
>True Way" to faith  and salvation in God, we have the "One True Way" to 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Charlie, I am genuinely sorry that you are dismayed by these threads.

Watching Robert and Richard playing "tennis" is actually quite entertaining. 
These are two extremely competent and knowledgeable guys. By having this 
"battle" they are developing more understanding of each other and who knows, 
perhaps some mutual respect will come from it. It is also causing them to 
evaluate what they do so they can defend it; that is no bad thing.

(I have certainly learned something by watching this thread and have some 
sympathy with both of them :-))

You could say the same about Judson's posts (although I sometimes find them 
quite alarming :-)), and my responses.

I think the majority of people here would share the sentiments you express 
above, but having freedom to explore these ideas without censorship or 
constraint is one of the main reasons I still post here.

The bottom line is "Don't take it to heart" or get too serious about it. 
(Certainly, don't let it upset or dismay you... it's only opinion.)

These are the reasons I post here:

1. Help people where I think I can. (This is kind of like an obligation. 
Throughout a long and mainly enjoyable career, I received immense help from 
many people. There's no way I can give it back (some of these people are 
dead now), other than by trying to assist others here.

2. Entertainment. (This forum frequently has witty and amusing comments and 
posts from people. It is fun.)

3. Contact with people with a similar background (technology) to myself. 
This is something that my normal daily social interactions don't fulfill, 
especially when (as at present) I am not actually working. Most of my 
friends see what I do as a great mystery and have no desire to discuss 
it...:-)) (In a way, that's a good thing; it helps me not to be obsessive 
about work and to operate at other levels as well.)

4. Exploration and discussion of ideas, without constraint or moderation. 
(As noted above.)

It's not life and death; only Usenet... :-)

Pete.
```

It you want 'alarming', did you watch Louis Theroux a week or so ago ?
"America's Most Hated Family". It was about the 'Western Baptist
Church' (IIRC) which is led by Grandpa Phepse and most of the
congregation are the Phelpse's family.

They claim that they are the 'Only True Christians', all 75 of them,
and everyone else _including_ Judson, and especially Bush, is going to
hell. They go out on the streets with pickets saying 'God hates YOU'
and that 'God Hates America'.

Apart from that, they seem to be just like every other bunch of
religious maniacs.

At least the Bretheren don't go door knocking or try to get people to
join them, they say that they have the only tickets and there ain't no
more so 'shove off'.

Well if 'heaven' is full of people like that, or just the Phelpses, or

```

Agreed. :-) I came to that conclusion some years back :-) I can't think of 
any discussions here that have caused me to change my mind, although 
sometimes I have extended my knowledge about something and occasionally 
something here has prompted me ot investigate something further.

Nevertheless, it can be fun to explore ideas and sometimes the act of 
defending what you believe to be right can cause you to review it. No bad 
thing.
>

No, I missed that and I really wanted to see it. Forgot to record it.

> "America's Most Hated Family". It was about the 'Western Baptist
> Church' (IIRC) which is led by Grandpa Phepse and most of the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> and that 'God Hates America'.

LOL! How to win friends and influence people... Are these the same ones that 
disrupt veteran funerals? It's a miracle someone hasn't shot some of them... 
maybe they are right and God is protecting them... :-)
>
> Apart from that, they seem to be just like every other bunch of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> more so 'shove off'.

I thought that was the LDS... Now you mention it, I think it IS the 
Brethren... Certainly, there isn't much room in Heaven...:-)
>
> Well if 'heaven' is full of people like that, or just the Phelpses, or
> people like Judson then I don't want to go there.

I've often thought it must be pretty dull on Saturday night... :-)

I remember asking my father when he first recovered from a heart attack, (I 
was 8) whether he would go to Heaven or Hell if he died. He grinned and 
said: "Son, I have friends in both places..."

I think that the idea of "Eternal Life" is pretty dreadful, irrespective of 
where you end up. There must come a time when enough is enough and you get 
to stop. Nature certainly seems to take that view... :-)

Pete.

#### ↳ Re: The "One True Way"

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com>

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
>
> I believe in a multitude of ways.  I believe that Richard or anyone
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> hell forever. Sort of like winning the ultimate lottery via birth.

The Christian theologian has worked out your concern, based on the premise 
that God is just.

I come from a tradition that says what God wants is righteous conduct and 
that one can be a righteous Baptist or a righteous Buddhist or a righteous 
anything (or nothing). My co-religionists and I have, therefore, neatly 
avoided the problem you posed, but other religions have solved the issue - 
at least to their own satisfaction.

To the intelligent Christian, then, your objection is a non-starter.

Marilyn Monroe was once asked by a reporter whether what some said about her 
wearing falsies was true. She replied: "People who know me better, know me 
better."

In another thread you asserted that Jews believe in a multiplicity in the 
God-head. Here you assert an unjust contradiction within Christianity. 
Perhaps if you got to know us better...
```

"HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote in message 
news:13r5r7b4godqhc2@corp.supernews.com...
> Charles Hottel wrote:
>>
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't recall ever asserting anything about what Jews believe, I think that 
was Judson not me.

Judson keeps saying people like me reject part of the Bible. I don't reject 
any part of the Bible. I do reject some of Judson's interpretations because 
I see them as implying inconsistencies in either the Bible or in God. He 
says interpreting the Bible is straightforward and easy.  I say there are 
some verses that are hard to reconcile with the rest of the Bible or with 
scientific knowledge. In those cases there may be a good interpretation and 
I am sure God can give one but I simply am unaware of it. Some people think 
they must appear to know everything but for me if I do not know then I just 
say so. When I say either the Bible is inconsistent or our interpretation is 
wrong I guess I should just say I believe the interpretation is wrong.

Even the Bible supports this view in 1 Cor 13:12 (and yes I know that is 
irrelevant to you because you do not accept the New Testament) but I mention 
if for others:

For now we see through a glass, darkly, but then face to face: now I know in 
part; but then shall I know even as also I am known.

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
>>
>> In another thread you asserted that Jews believe in a multiplicity
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> think that was Judson not me.

You are absolutely right - you didn't utter a word about what you thought 
Jews believed. My apologies.

>
> Judson keeps saying people like me reject part of the Bible. I don't
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> know in part; but then shall I know even as also I am known.

Not to worry. Jews praise all of the moral teachings put forth in the New 
Testament: Justice, mercy, charity, fairness, lovingkindness, and the rest. 
We hold that Christianity is a good and holy road to God - we see no reason 
for any Christian to change his faith (and we wish more felt the same way).

Your other point is well-taken - that the more one studies, the more one 
knows. Often things cannot be taken at face value on the first reading. For 
example, here are two quick, interesting, Biblical trivia questions:

1. What was God's first commandment - not the big ten, the first thing He 
told humans to do or not do?
2. You recall the putative sacrifice of Isaac by Abraham? How old, about, 
was Isaac?

=
=
=
=
=
=
=
=
=
=
=
=
=
1. "Be fruitful and multiply" which teaches us that, to get someone's 
attention, start your talk with a sex topic.

2. Thirty-four - which doesn't take away from the story, but adds a new 
twist to Isaac's participation in the project.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The "One True Way"

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-02-16T13:00:36+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<61mnd6F2030pdU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<13r4djktdnvf970@corp.supernews.com> <13r5r7b4godqhc2@corp.supernews.com> <13r73gscu8ldoed@corp.supernews.com> <13r7ckfh9sh7s6d@corp.supernews.com> <61i652F1u675cU1@mid.individual.net> <13r8g5bm4lco58@corp.supernews.com> <61jtqdF1v8cdbU1@mid.individual.net> <13rb53cg1l1uo8b@corp.supernews.com>`

```


"HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote in message 
news:13rb53cg1l1uo8b@corp.supernews.com...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>>>> 37 (according to Rabbibinical sources)
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
:-)

This confirms what I was coming to suspect... "religious" , at least in this 
newsgroup, is a very narrow view.

1. The city was Varanasi in India. In English it is called Benares and is 
quite a remarkable place. The Buddha  (Gautama Siddhartha) is alleged to 
have sat there for forty days and nights (where have we heard that before?) 
vowing not to move until he either attained enlightenment or died. He used a 
tree for shade and the descendant of that tree is there to this day. (It 
gets very well looked after :-))

I have visited the city and it is very interesting and colourful. It is a 
Holy City to a number of different faiths, not just Buddhists.

The Holy river Ganges runs through the town and it is believed by Hindus to 
have restorative powers. Anyone who dies of leprosy or any other wasting 
disease is put into the river, and it is quite an experience to take a boat 
ride in the early dawn...The fish are very well fed and you can buy large 
specimens in the local marketplace. (You never know, you could be dining in 
the evening on a fish that dined on your Granny in the morning...It is food 
for thought.)

 People who don't need the restorative powers of the Ganges are simply 
cremated on pyres in the open and the smell of burning human flesh is one of 
the ineradicable memories I have from that visit... (I should stress that my 
visit there was 33 years ago and it may have changed much since...)

Many pilgrims take to the river to wash and bathe in its Holy waters. The 
town water supply for the city is taken from the same source.

In the 19th Century, the British decided to clean it up a bit and were 
amazed to find that the water a few hundred yards downstream was perfectly 
clean and potable. The locals had known that the river was restorative for 
thousands of years (that's one reason why it is considered Holy) but the 
British were baffled. It was many years (and a number of detailed surveys) 
before the mystery was solved: The river bed has a large number of fresh 
springs in it which are powerful enough to remove the effluent and replace 
the polluted water.

The usual filth of Asia is present in large doses and I saw rats as big as 
cats (no, I'm NOT exaggerating, I mean it literally). I can understand how 
the Buddha was too exhausted to continue his journey and needed to sit a 
while...

2. Arjuna was the charioteer of Krishna. The discourses between him and the 
incarnation of the Godhead are detailed in the Mahabharata and the subset of 
it, Bhagavad Gita. It is an interesting allegory with the chariot 
representing our senses and emotions and the charioteer needing a firm hand. 
The backdrop of mighty battles with good ultimately triumphing over evil, is 
a fitting setting for the discourses between man and God. Arjuna has all the 
doubts and fears that humans encounter, and his questions to Krishna are the 
kind of questions most of us would like to get answers to, if we had a 
chance to chat with God.

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: The "One True Way"

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-13T09:39:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bEsj.98945$L%6.61315@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<13r4djktdnvf970@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
>I am very, very dismayed at some of the current threads. We have the "One True Way" to faith  and salvation in God, we have the 
>"One True Way" to link programs.  I thought that computer programmers were more flexible than this. Because the machines are so 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> via birth.

Where your wrong is in your interpretation of my position. :-)

The only things about which I have said that there is only "one way" is
where the Bible says there is only "one way". If the Bible is wrong in
those cases, then I am wrong, if the Bible is correct, then I am correct,
for I am simply repeating what the Bible says. If I say something in
conflict with what the Bible actually says, then please point it out so
I may be corrected. But if you simply disagree with the Bible, don't
blame me for that. :-)

As far as people coming to God who have never heard of the Bible, sure
such things happen. The Bible clearly teaches that it is men's hearts
that God judges, and people are accountable according to their knowledge
(e.g. Romans 2:11-15). :-)

I remember the seeing testimony of a young man from (IIRC) Madagascar,
who had never heard of Jesus, never even heard the word "Jesus" spoken
in his presence, had never seen a missionary. According to him, God
spoke to him, told him about Jesus, and through a number of miraculous
events, brought him to a Christian seminary in the United States. For
example, God told him to go to the airport, where God would provide a
way for him to fly to the U.S. Having no money, he put his few clothes
in an old cardboard suitcase, and when he got to the airport, there was
pre-paid a ticket in his name waiting there. When he got to the seminary,
tuition in his name was already pre-paid. I have also heard others give
accounts of God directly and supernaturally telling them the truth about
Jesus. God does not desire that anyone be condemned (2 Peter 3:9).

But God does not let men pick and choose the way salvation should
work (e.g. Acts 4:10-12).
```

"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jesus or any concept of God or salvation?

Babies and young children have not yet reached an age where they can
know and understand right from wrong. The Bible declares in many
places that guilt comes with knowledge of sin (e.g. John 9:41, 15:22,24).
Though the Bible does not specifically say, it is my belief that God
considers them innocent. This is consistent with Scripture, and with my
understanding of God's character.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The "One True Way"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com>

- **References:** `<13r4djktdnvf970@corp.supernews.com> <7bEsj.98945$L%6.61315@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <13r73l2iqrs6236@corp.supernews.com> <kmNsj.71555$vt2.372@bignews8.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Wed, 13 Feb 2008 20:06:12 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[55 more quoted lines elided]…
>understanding of God's character.

The Roman Catholic Church now teaches, in regards to children who have
died without Baptism, that the Church can only entrust them to the
mercy of God, as she does in her funeral rites for them. Indeed, the
great mercy of God who desires that all men should be saved, and
Jesus' tenderness toward children which caused him to say: "Let the
children come to me, do not hinder them, allow us to hope that there
is a way of salvation for children who have died without Baptism. All
the more urgent is the Church's call not to prevent little children
coming to Christ through the gift of holy Baptism.

In addition, the Bible does give some credence to this.  In Matthew
19:14 "But Jesus said, Suffer little children, and forbid them not, to
come unto me: for of such is the kingdom of heaven."

For years there was a theological theory that unbaptized babies must
go to a place of eternal bliss but without the presence of God,
because they had not been purified through baptism. 

This place wasn't "Purgatory" but rather limbo as these unbaptized
children had never had a chance to commit actual sin.

Nonetheless, this was never the official dogma or doctrine of the
Catholic church and in fact has been in the news lately as being
officially "dropped."

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The "One True Way"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-15T12:22:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hihbr31qjj5581f403u6tc5ijg6upr0ncm@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<13r4djktdnvf970@corp.supernews.com> <7bEsj.98945$L%6.61315@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <13r73l2iqrs6236@corp.supernews.com> <kmNsj.71555$vt2.372@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <pcs8r3tjufug485b7oeftmrqqu4jib2ua4@4ax.com> <gO%sj.695$yk5.237@newsfe18.lga>`

```
On Thu, 14 Feb 2008 12:30:46 -0600, "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:

>Would any of you care to comment upon the exclusion and supression of the
>family of Jesus, and St. James the Just (his brother) in particular, in the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Who says they were excluded?  St. James the Just is an uncommon name
for St. James the Less.  He was one of the 12 original apostles.  He
is also referred to as James son of Alpaeus.  James the Less was an
important leader in the New Testament church. He is clearly
distinguished from James, son of Zebedee, another one of the Twelve
Apostles. 

The problem is that the name "James" was used four times in the New
Testament.     

1.  James, the son of Zebedee -- Apostle, brother of John, Apostle;
also called "James the Greater".

2. James, the son of Alpheus, Apostle -- Matthew 10:3; Mark 3:18; Luke
6:15; Acts 1:13.

3. James, the brother of the Lord -- Matthew 13:55; Mark 6:3;
Galatians 1:19. Without a shadow of doubt, he must be identified with
the James of Galatians 2:2 and 2:9; Acts 12:17, 15:13 sqq. and 21:18;
and I Corinthians 15:7.

4. James, the son of Mary, brother of Joseph (or Joses) -- Mark 15:40
(where he is called ï¿½ mikros "the little", not the "less", as in the
D.V., nor the "lesser"); Matthew 27:56. Probably the son of Cleophas
or Clopas (John 19:25) where "Maria Cleophï¿½" is generally translated
"Mary the wife of Cleophas", as married women are commonly
distinguished by the addition of their husband's name.

5. James, the brother of Jude -- Jude 1:1. Most Catholic commentators
identify Jude with the "Judas Jacobi", the "brother of James" (Luke
6:16; Acts 1:13), called thus because his brother James was beter
known than himself in the primitive Church. 

For further discussion of this, see:
http://www.newadvent.org/cathen/08280a.htm

In short, James was part of the founding of the Roman Catholic church
and was consulted on at least one occassion by Paul.

In addition, the term "Brother of the Lord, does not indicate that he
was Jesus' sibling.  Rather it is intended to express BRETHREN OF THE
LORD.

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

SAM: "What's shaking Norm?"
NORM: "All four cheeks and a couple of chins."
--From U.S. TV Show, "Cheers"
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Steve

```
tlmfru wrote:
> SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote in message
> news:hihbr31qjj5581f403u6tc5ijg6upr0ncm@4ax.com...
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Peter,

So having hung out your fishing line with bait, you waited for someone 
to take a bite. Steve offered a reasonable reply and your summation was 
"Rubbish !".

If my memory serves me correctly, you are at disagreement with YOUR OWN 
brother, because he is a 'Holy Roller' and gets up your nose. I don't 
care for 'Holy Rollers either' :-). You've got a real bee in your bonnet 
about the Jesus 'family', and more politely than above, rejected some 
two years ago, my off the cuff suggestion that 'brother' should not be 
taken to always literally mean blood-brother.

Consider - Mark 14 : 12-16 "And on the first day of the Unleavened 
Bread, when it was customary for them to sacrifice the passover, the 
disciples said to him, 'Where dost thou want us to go and prepare for 
thee to eat the passover ?' And he sent two of his disciples, and said 
to them, 'Go into the city, and there will meet you a man carrying a 
pitcher of water; follow him. And wherever he enters, say.......'"

So the two disciples look out for a man carrying a jug of water. In our 
very modern world, how many photos have you seen of 'Arabs' or African 
males carrying pitchers/jugs of water ? No way, that is women's work; 
and exactly the same during biblical times. Hence it was easy to spot 
the man carrying water, against accepted custom. Think of the musical 
'Fiddler on the Roof' and the song TRADITION.

I reiterate use of 'brother' or 'brethren' as used in the OT and NT
does not necessarily mean blood-brother/blood-sister.

I'm not going to argue the toss. Did you read Steve's reference. I'm 
going to give you a more specific one in answer to your original question :-

The Brethren of the Lord : http://www.newadvent.org/cathen/02767a.htm

It's a bit complicated figuring out who is who, so if you have problems 
- download the Mormon Genealogy software and draw up your own Jesus 
Family Tree :-).

I think the article offers a reasonable interpretation. I don't really 
care whether you reference it or not. In truth I don't believe you are 
interested, but just looking for a set of negatives. (That is, if Jesus 
wasn't the only child that Mary had, then it tends to bring into 
question His divinity etc., etc......).

Rather than research negatives look for the good points in the New 
Testament. The Beatitudes would be a good starting point - Matthew 
(5:3-10) and Luke (6:20-22).

Jimmy, Calgary
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The "One True Way"

_(thread depth: 9)_

On Fri, 15 Feb 2008 23:13:43 GMT, "James J. Gavan"
<jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:

>tlmfru wrote:
>> SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote in message
…[88 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary

Thanks for chiming in Jimmy.  We've missed you around here :)

I'd add on that the Roman Catholic Church is the only major religion,
that I am aware of, that insists Jesus had no brothers or sisters and
that his Mother Mary remained a virgin her entire life.  It is
possible that the Russian, Eastern and Coptic Orthodox Churches also
follow that belief but I can't be sure.

The New Testament contains several references to Jesus's brothers and
sisters, the most explicit of which is Matthew 13:55-56, in which the
neighbors wonder where Jesus gets off with all this preaching: "Is not
this the carpenter's son? Is not his mother called Mary? And are not
his brothers James and Joseph and Simon and Judas? And are not all his
sisters with us? Where then did this man get all this?"

The words in the Greek original are adelphoi, brothers, and adelphi,
sisters. They can be used in a metaphorical, brotherhood-of-man sense,
just like their English equivalents. But the context strongly suggests
the strict sense, i.e., children of the same mom.  However, the
Catholic take is that the words have yet another meaning: cousins, or
perhaps Joseph's children by a previous marriage.  

Can you imagine if Jesus actually had a brother or sister?  Lends new
poignance to the refrain "My parents treat my brother like he's God."
Or, "Mom how come Jesus can walk on water and I just sink?".

But seriously, one of the best explanations of the bible verses used
by Protestants and others to claim Jesus had brothers (James, Joeses
or Joseph, Simon and Judas) and a sister (Salome) can be found here:
http://www.ewtn.com/library/SCRIPTUR/JESBRS.TXT

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

SAM: "What's shaking Norm?"
NORM: "All four cheeks and a couple of chins."
--From U.S. TV Show, "Cheers"
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Steve

I think this is probably the clearest Scriptural answer to that:

(Romans 2:12-15)
  For as many as have sinned without law will also perish without law,
  and as many as have sinned in the law will be judged by the law (for
  not the hearers of the law are just in the sight of God, but the doers
  of the law will be justified; for when Gentiles, who do not have the
  law, by nature do the things in the law, these, although not having
  the law, are a law to themselves, who show the work of the law written
  in their hearts, their conscience also bearing witness, and between
  themselves their thoughts accusing or else excusing them)

- **References:** `<13r4djktdnvf970@corp.supernews.com> <7bEsj.98945$L%6.61315@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <13r73l2iqrs6236@corp.supernews.com> <kmNsj.71555$vt2.372@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <dit9r3daae3q3cb0ri246h4j2jjmf70vta@4ax.com> <wdntj.98370$K27.94136@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <vcotj.3091$y05.2646@newsfe22.lga> <Wrotj.98433$K27.22124@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <HHEtj.4858$097.869@newsfe21.lga>`

"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
> PL

Perhaps it's because I've spent a lot of time studying the Bible, but the
above seems very clear to me.  Here's a synopsis in my own words:

God judges the heart. When a person has knowledge of God's law and
disobeys it, they are guilty. When a person does not know God's law,
if their heart and conduct are in line the spirit (intent) of God's law, then
God accepts that, even though they don't know and may not follow the
precise letter of the law. When a person does not know God's law and
their heart and conduct are against the spirit of God's laws, they are
judged guilty. Without knowledge of God's law, it is their thoughts and
conscience that accuse or excuse them.

Though not specifically stated here, the implication is that those judged
innocent or guilty will receive similar after-life reward or punishment as
anyone else judged innocent or guilty. The Church, the collection of
true believers in Christ, are apparently in a different category from those
who are simply innocent. This is apparent from a number of Scriptures,
but the Bible doesn't supply much detail. One difference appears to be
the adoption by God as literally a member of God family and becoming
"one with God," and another is receiving reward for good works at the
judgment seat of Christ (2 Corinthians 5:10). But I say 'appears' because
I don't think the Scriptural texts are clear and specific enough to be
completely definitive. Someone who knows the Scriptures better than I
may see it as more definitive. If so, I would like to see it expounded.

As far as I understand your question, that answers what you asked. :-)

Those incapable of understanding right and wrong because of age or
mental impairment will be judged as innocent. I believe I could make a
very strong Scriptural defense of that position.
```

sui programmi la segnalazione di "errore 114"; non c'e' una situazione
sempre uguale quando si verifica, programmi tra loro identici (cambia
solo la parte di codice mentre la definizione dei dati e' uguale) si
comportano in maniera diversa: un programma da errore e un altro
programma invece no.

A friend of mine read some of the interactions between Judson and myself and 
said to me last night: "Pete, why are you engaging in a battle of wits with 
someone who is completely unarmed? There's no honour in that."

He was right.

Pete.
```

Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:33a2262d-5b9a-49d8-93fb-9df82b086ec9@e60g2000hsh.googlegroups.com...
>The S/360 card was green. In my years of browsing (in the interests of
>science and broadening my mind) I have uncovered the fact that human
>urine can be clear, straw coloured, yellow, orange, red and even green
>(the latter is a known effect of some chemicals; I think diuretics).

I can attest to orange.  During a nasty bout of kidney stones, the doctor
inserted  a "stent" into me - "urinary extender", a thin plastic tube from
the bladder to the kidney, to hold the stone there until lithotripter time
could be arranged.  It's uncomfortable and tiring but not actually painful.
But it makes you feel that you want to pee all the time - quite a sting.
There is a medication that supresses this - but - it turns your urine bright
orange!

PL
```

#### ↳ Re: Well at least this groups is more interesting than ...

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-15T08:00:04-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13rb6n4nmtu02a1@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ux_sj.217681$1x.187017@fe05.news.easynews.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> I guess discussions of religion, ethics, and Unix linking is better
> than the PL/I group that is currently discussing which characters
> could be punched and reproduced on early IBM keypunch machines.

Maybe what we need are posts that draw people in. For example, here's a post 
I made on another group:

--- begin quote

Wednesday Night at Walmart

I'm prowling around the four or five very crowded holiday aisles at Walmart
to purchase a Valentine's day chocolate for the staff at my veterinarian's
office, when a fight breaks out.

Two mid-20's black women commence to scream at each other. I couldn't quite
make out the nature of their complaint since I don't speak emotion-laden
Nigerian-accented, Ebonics. I did catch the word "mutherfucker!" several
times inasmuch as it seemed to be used by each to describe every shortcoming
of the other.

A big black guy who seemed to be with (at least) one of the women
continually tried to shush her and position himself between the two
antagonists. When the yelling reached maximum decibels to no apparent
effect, they each began pulling stuff from the shelves and throwing it: They
were limited in the ammunition by the geometry of the displays to bags of
candy and stuffed animals; grinning puppies and red bunnies came down like
hail stones.

Most of the other customers stood around slack-jawed and completely at a
loss. Not me. My training kicked in. I took action.

Hoping to change the dynamics I shouted: "Hit her in the mouth!" and "Run
for your life!" and "She was right about you!" Strangely, these calls for
restraint only seemed to incite the combatants to further frenzies of
chocolate-chunking.

After about five minutes of mayhem, one of the participants exited the
store, leaving in her wake a ripple of rejoinders and gestures that caused
mothers to cover the eyes and ears of innocent children.

I still don't know which of the two women got a little heart-shaped token
inscribed with "Will you be mine?"

Anyway, I bought an (undamaged) three-pound Hershey's chocolate heart for
the animal clinic staff.

--- end quote
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Well at least this groups is more interesting than ...

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-15T21:43:56-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13rcmvrtcpb0aa@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ux_sj.217681$1x.187017@fe05.news.easynews.com> <13rb6n4nmtu02a1@corp.supernews.com> <61mqgbF2097h9U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:

>> Maybe what we need are posts that draw people in. For example,
>> here's a post I made on another group:
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

When I posted this on "tx.guns" several responses were like yours. Some were 
along the lines of "That's why I don't go to Walmart" and "Why didn't you 
put a couple through the roof?" It's a pretty eclectic group, pretty much 
like this one - only one female posts regularily and there's the occassional 
drive-by troll. Then, of course, ordinary news reports get folks all 
exercised.

For example, in response to the University of Northern Illinois shooting, 
one of the ususal suspect anti-gun groups proposed legislation requiring gun 
stores to notify universities anytime a student buys a gun. This generated 
some non-nice things being said by our pro-gun folks.

There's cat stories, stupid pubic servant stories, stories about cats AND 
stupid public servants, a running tally of Tasercides, stupid criminal 
stories, stories that defy description [Like the one today where the 
Canadian RCMP report finding a third human foot washing up on the beach in 
18 months. Each was wearing a sneaker. An RCMP constable was quoted as "this 
is somewhat unusual."], stories about police work, happenings at the range, 
happenings at work involving guns, etc.

Guns stir debate. In a country of 300 million people, there are 200 million 
guns. 40% of American households have a firearm.There are rational arguments 
and emotional arguments on the subject - not about guns so much, but about 
the rules and laws. Some debates reduce down to bumper sticker slogans: "I 
carry a gun because I can't carry a cop" or "When seconds count, the police 
are just minutes away" or "9-1-1 Dial-A-Prayer" or "Keep honking - I'm 
reloading."

Here's one story that's a biggie. Washington D.C. has banned the private 
possession of handguns since 1976. Finally a case challenging this situation 
has percolated up to the Supreme Court which will hear arguments in early 
March. The case has generated an unprecedented number of friend-of-the-court 
briefs arguing one side or the other. Briefs have been filed by retired 
generals, various anti-gun groups, linguists, gays, feminists, racial 
equality advocates, Jews, sportsmen, private detectives, pediatricians, 
surgeons, 31 (of 50) state attorneys general, the Solicitor General of the 
United States, a majority of Congress, big city mayors, and even Dick 
Cheney, the Vice President of the United States. Fascinating reading.

Anyway, as I said, the court will hear arguments in early March and a 
decision is expected in early to mid-June (just in time for the presidential 
election campaign). Oh, I forgot: The issue before the court is whether the 
D.C. government CAN ban handguns under the U.S. Constitution (2nd Amendment: 
"... the right of the people to keep and bear arms shall not be 
infringed.").
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Well at least this groups is more interesting than ...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-02-16T23:09:56+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<61nr3nF1vlt12U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<ux_sj.217681$1x.187017@fe05.news.easynews.com> <13rb6n4nmtu02a1@corp.supernews.com> <61mqgbF2097h9U1@mid.individual.net> <13rcmvrtcpb0aa@corp.supernews.com>`

```


"HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote in message 
news:13rcmvrtcpb0aa@corp.supernews.com...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[99 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I can understand a tradition of gun ownership and a democratic right, dating 
from a time when the Republic could be threatened at any moment.

I can't judge America for gun ownership.

I'm sure most Americans see the awful tragedies at Universities and schools 
as terrible, but I can also understand that in a violent society, people may 
feel more secure with a gun in the house.

I had a girlfriend in California who was the sweetest person you could 
imagine. She was a schoolteacher, worked for charity and the community in 
her spare time, and was of Hispanic descent. The first night I stayed at her 
North Hollywood house, as we were preparing for bed, she placed a gun under 
the pillow. I was kind of shocked and told her I'd be good...:-)

I asked her if she would seriously kill somebody who broke into the house, 
and she assured me that she would do so in a heartbeat, had done a course in 
the use and maintenance of her firearm and would have no compunction about 
using it. "I'm a girl living alone in a sometimes rough neighbourhood. 
Anyone comes in my house, they're dead..." were the actual words she used.

There is no doubt in my mind that she was serious about it.

Although there has been an increase in violent crimes here over the past 50 
years, the cops still don't carry guns as a matter of course (they can call 
an armed offenders squad which responds in a VERY short time, and I have 
been told some squad cars in certain inner city areas now carry fiearms, 
although this fact is not well publicised), and we have comparatively few 
shootings by either cops or offenders. Rifles and shotguns must be licensed 
and handguns are illegal. Military munitions like automatic weapons, 
grenades, etc are simply not readily available. (Anyone found with anything 
like that could face 10 years in jail; it is considered serious...)

This, of course, doesn't stop crazy or drugged people who are Hell-bent on 
murder; they just use a knife or an axe, or a shotgun.

Without making comment on guns in the USA, I can only say that, from the 
point of view of Law and Order, I'd rather be here...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Well at least this groups is more interesting than ...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-02-18T08:49:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ro9jr3hm4qvsnd7fajqgum6vmusfjvsoto@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ux_sj.217681$1x.187017@fe05.news.easynews.com> <13rb6n4nmtu02a1@corp.supernews.com> <61mqgbF2097h9U1@mid.individual.net> <13rcmvrtcpb0aa@corp.supernews.com> <0472d665-da87-43af-8d56-259f77b023a4@p73g2000hsd.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Sat, 16 Feb 2008 03:27:38 -0800 (PST), Alistair
<alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>As for bearing arms, doesn't that only apply to members of legitimate
>militias?

It doesn't actually say that.   I figure that clause was to define
"arms", so that we don't think it is limited to swords or hunting
weapons.   But some people think it is used to define "people", saying
that only authorized people should have weapons.

I've even seen some people say that it is used to define "arms" - but
to include only weapons suitable for an 18th century militia.

But if the objective of  using arms to ensure "the security of a free
state", muskets aren't effective.

(While I wouldn't mind seeing this amendment repealed - times have
changed - I mind very much people pretending that the Constitution
doesn't mean what it says, and can be ignored or mangled).
```

On Mon, 18 Feb 2008 14:07:37 -0600, "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:

>>> I've even seen some people say that it is used to define "arms" - but
>>> to include only weapons suitable for an 18th century militia.

suited for a militia - without being limited to the 18th century
militia, where the fit might actually be better.

that doesn't make sense.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Well at least this groups is more interesting than ...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-02-19T01:34:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fpdbm9$ni5$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<ux_sj.217681$1x.187017@fe05.news.easynews.com> <ro9jr3hm4qvsnd7fajqgum6vmusfjvsoto@4ax.com> <fpce1a$kht$1@reader2.panix.com> <13rjpc3iepjpp61@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <13rjpc3iepjpp61@corp.supernews.com>,
HeyBub <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> In article <ro9jr3hm4qvsnd7fajqgum6vmusfjvsoto@4ax.com>,
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>a sawed-off shotgun was not a weapon normally used by the military. 

According to 
<http://caselaw.lp.findlaw.com/scripts/getcase.pl?navby=CASE&court=US&vol=307&page=174> 
the Supreme Court of the United States of America opined (among other 
things):

--begin quoted text:

In the absence of any evidence tending to show that possession or use of a 
'shotgun having a barrel of less than eighteen inches in length' at this 
time has some reasonable relationship to the preservation or efficiency of 
a well regulated militia, we cannot say that the Second Amendment 
guarantees the right to keep and bear such an instrument.

--end quoted text

Whether this is 'something close' - or even vaguely capable of being 
related - to 'saying the freedom of the press specified in the immediately 
preceding Amendment applies only to mechanisms familiar to Gutenberg or 
Zenger' will be left to the interpretation of The Reader.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Well at least this groups is more interesting than ...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-16T12:10:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sv5er3dkj5dhqgeqm8g3jf5a7vhh51iipo@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ux_sj.217681$1x.187017@fe05.news.easynews.com> <13rb6n4nmtu02a1@corp.supernews.com> <61mqgbF2097h9U1@mid.individual.net> <13rcmvrtcpb0aa@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Fri, 15 Feb 2008 21:43:56 -0600, "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:

>Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[98 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I hope D.C. wins.  Lets be clear what the Second Amendment says. The
Second Amendment of the United States Constitutionï¿½s Bill of Rights
declares a well-regulated militia as "being necessary to the security
of a free State" and prohibits infringement of "the right of the
people to keep and bear arms."

One key controversy revolves around who is prohibited from
infringement and why the Supreme Court has never ruled whether the
Second Amendment prohibits individual States from infringing upon this
right. The most recent Supreme Court precedent, from 1875, is that the
Second Amendment is only a limit on the power of the federal
government, (see United States v. Cruikshank) but some people contend
that it extends to state jurisdictions.

Another major point of contention is whether it protects against
infringement of an individual right to personal firearms or a
collective State militia right. The United States Courts of Appeals
are in disagreement over the "collective" interpretation and
"individual" interpretation. There is also a "modified collective"
view that says the right is protected for individuals to bear arms
based on their needs while serving in a militia.  And I believe this
would extend to police (city, state, county) and other law enforcement
agencies.

Other points of disagreement include the meaning of the militia clause
and the meaning of infringement (does any regulation at all constitute
infringement, and why have federal regulations been allowed.) All
federal courts have found that reasonable firearm regulation is
allowable, while an outright firearm ban is currently the subject of
Supreme Court review in District of Columbia v. Heller which is the
case HeyBub mentioned.

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

SAM: "What's shaking Norm?"
NORM: "All four cheeks and a couple of chins."
--From U.S. TV Show, "Cheers"
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

>Thanks for clarifying the matter. Now, does the National Guard
>constitute a legitimate militia? If so, can any other organisation
>claim to be a militia as a means to carrying arms? I'm thinking of the
>nutters who train in a quasi-military style in the woods.

Nope, the definition of militia members excludes mental cases. 

---------------------------------------
I'm looking for a phrase for mass murder. It used to be called Going Postal. After
Columbine, I coined the alliterative Going Goth. Now that socially inept teens have been
replaced by socially inept postgrad college students, what's the phrase?

We had one today in Chicago, when a former grad student burst into a lecture hall at
Northern Illinois University (NIU) in DeKalb, murdered five students and then himself with
a shotgun. He had a FOID, a firearms owners identification card, and all his guns were
purchased legally. No amount of profiling would have prevented the murders. The system
simply doesn't work.

Robert wrote:
>
> We had one today in Chicago, when a former grad student burst into a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> murders. The system simply doesn't work.

I agree. Any "system" that puts obstacles in the path of a gun owner (FOID, 
5-day waiting period, or outright prohibition of handguns as in Chicago) is 
busted. Subsystems, as in the univeristy's prohibition of weapons on campus, 
is double-busted.

Contrast what happened at Virginia Tech and NIU with an attempt in December 
at the Colorado church. There, a goblin killed two people in the church 
parking lot, then, carrying over 1,000 rounds of ammunition, prepared to 
enter the building. He was met, not by the ladies auxillary passign out 
cookies, but by a female member of the church with a concealed handgun 
license (CHL) and her own 9mm pistol.

Lest you think "How much damage could one person do in a church?" consider 
that the New Life Church has over 10,000 members and a goodly portion were 
on hand that day (in excess of 3,000).
```

SkippyPB wrote:
>>
>> I agree. Any "system" that puts obstacles in the path of a gun owner
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

She was NOT a professional security guard - she was a civilian member of the 
congregation seconded to hall-monitor duty. Security Guards in Colorado have 
to be licensed by the state. The church guardians are members of the church 
who are screened and not "mercenaries that we hire to walk around our campus 
to provide security," [senior pastor] Boyd said.

I wouldn't hold her former police training against her inasmuch as the 
result turned out okay, possibly because her police training wore off. 
Police are 11 times more likely to shoot an innocent bystander than a 
civilian.

As for the NIU mess, recall that in the class was a 32-year-old Army 
reservist who had recently completed a tour in Bosnia. Certainly she (yes, 
it was a she) could handle a weapon.

You're right about whackos being able to obtain guns willy-nilly. Even the 
NIU whacko was able to obtain a gun under, arguably, some of the more 
stringent laws in the nation (prior registration of individuals wanting to 
purchase a gun, 10-day waiting period, etc.).
```

SkippyPB wrote:

>
> I hope D.C. wins.  Lets be clear what the Second Amendment says. The
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> that it extends to state jurisdictions.

Correct. The 2nd Amendment has never been "incorporated" to affect the 
states (see Wikipedia on "incorporation doctrine").

>
> Another major point of contention is whether it protects against
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> agencies.

Correct on the disparity of decision amongst the circuits. It is the job of 
the Supreme Court to sort this disparity out and impose a single rule on the 
entire country.

The notion of law enforcement being part of any "militia" has never, to my 
knowledge, been asserted by anyone.

>
> Other points of disagreement include the meaning of the militia clause
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Most modern scholarship dismisses the "militia" notion. In every other use 
of the the word "people" in the Bill of Rights, the unequivocal, unmodified, 
meaning is the collection of individual citizens.

All "rights" have limitations. One of the issues is the degree of 
limitation. There are tests applied to limitation of rights, the highest 
being "strict scrutiny." "Strict scrutiny," to which all Constitutional 
rights are subject has, at its core, three subordinate tests: 1) Does the 
proposed restriction serve a compelling social necessity, 2) Can the 
necessity be acomplished by less intrusive means, and 3) Do constitutional 
alternatives exist once the action is prohibited. For example, a state may 
require all hunting rifles to be colored dayglow orange as a safety measure. 
This law would meet the strict scrutiny test because the banning of guns is 
not contemplated.

If you want to study the issue in depth, a good time can be had by reading 
the briefs filed so far. They can be found here:

http://dcguncase.com/blog/case-filings/

[This link goes to the entire paper trail for this contest.]

For example, in the brief filed by criminiologists, we find the fact:

"These figures discredit the theory that predicts increased murder from an
increase in guns.  For example, the gunstock per 1000 persons rose from 627 
to 858 over the 30-year
period between 1974-2003, but the murder rate fell 41%  [from 0.069 to 0.055 
per 1000]." [But the murder rate went UP in Washington.]

In the brief filed by Jeanette Moll, an individual, she says "the owning of 
a gun in one's own home should be as free from government interference as 
the activities of two or more homosexuals."

She didn't exactly put it like that, but close.

General John D. Altenburg and other general officers opine that lack of gun 
familiarity impedes national defense.

The Pink Pistols argue that the Lesbian, Gay, and Transgender community is 
especially at risk and that swatting someone with a handbag just won't do.

Women Legislators & Academics argue that the time has passed for women 
having to rely on barrel-chested men to protect them. Guns equalize 
gender-based differences.

The Association of Physicians & Surgeons argue that the availability of 
firearms "serve an essential role
against misuse of medicine by tyrannical governments for unethical goals."

Contrast this well-reasoned position with the American Academy of Pediatrics 
(which is not on the available list, but we know their mantra to be "it's 
for the children!").

The Paragon Foundation has the best argument: "Keeping and bearing arms is 
good."

As to what the Supreme Court will do, there are several possibilites, not 
all mutually exclusive:

1.Strike down the handgun ban for D.C. but allow the city to "effectively" 
ban handguns by staying silent on oppressive "regulations" that would so 
restrict ownership as to be a ban (registration, prohibitive fees, etc.).

2. Strike down the ban absolutely, with a warning that excessive regulations 
will not stand.

3. Strike down the ban and incorporate the 2nd Amendment as binding upon all 
the states.

4. Reverse the Court of Appeals and say Washington is not a state, hence the 
2nd Amendment does not apply.
```

On Sat, 16 Feb 2008 12:10:26 -0500, SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:


>One key controversy revolves around who is prohibited from
>infringement and why the Supreme Court has never ruled whether the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>that it extends to state jurisdictions.

It doesn't matter what "some people contend," the Second Amendment does not apply to
states until the Supreme Court 'incorporates' it into the Fourteenth Amendment. Most of
Amendments 1 through 8 were individually incorporated during the '50s and '60s. The Second
Amendment is the major exception. 

The most recent Supreme Court case was United States v. Miller, 307 U.S. 174 (1939). Its
ruling said the Second Amendment doesn't protect sawed-off shotguns because they are not
militia weapons. Actually they are, they were used in Vietnam.

>Another major point of contention is whether it protects against
>infringement of an individual right to personal firearms or a
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>agencies.

Who are militia members? Gun nuts claim all males aged 18 through 45 are, based on an
obscure law 10 U.S.C. 331-333. In reality, the concept of 'unorganized militia' is
obsolete. After the Revolution, the unorganized militia was never called to service nor
used in any conflict. And it never will be in the future. 

State militias were federalized as the National Guard by the National Defense Acts of 1916
and 1933. What we have left are State Defense Forces aka State Guards, which are active in
about 25 states. Members are unpaid volunteers who provide their own uniforms and guns.
They have been used a few times for civil emergencies, notably Hurricane Katrina. 

Oddly, although states are expected to have militias, they are legally forbidden to have
troops. In other words, they're not allowed to pay soldiers, because that would constitute
a Standing Army. 

Congress should repeal 10 U.S.C. 331-333, rewriting it to apply to organized State Defense
Forces. 

>Other points of disagreement include the meaning of the militia clause
>and the meaning of infringement (does any regulation at all constitute
>infringement, and why have federal regulations been allowed.)

Because only militia weapons are covered by the Second Amendment. OK, a 155 howitzer is
clearly a militia weapon, why can't I own one? I can, provided I get permission from the
county sheriff, register it with ATF and pay a $200 tax. If the projectile flies over
three other counties, there is no requirement to get premission from those sheriffs.
```

On Sun, 17 Feb 2008 13:17:35 -0500, SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:

>On Sat, 16 Feb 2008 17:06:48 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:

>>The most recent Supreme Court case was United States v. Miller, 307 U.S. 174 (1939). Its
>>ruling said the Second Amendment doesn't protect sawed-off shotguns because they are not
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>weapons.

The Thompson was issued by the US Military from 1938 to 1944, when it was replaced by the
M3 to save money. Thompsons were used a lot in WWII and Korea.  The Thompson fires .45 ACP
(pistol ammunition)  at 500-1,000 rounds per minute from a 30 round magazine. The M3 fires
.45 ACP at 400 rounds per minute from a 30 round magazine. The current Special Ops
submachine gun is the H&K MP5, which fires 9mm pistol ammunition at 900 rounds per minute
from a 30 round magazine. See picture linked below.

Big round magazines seen in gangster movies were never popular. It's better to duct tape
two 30 round magazines together, the second upside-down. 

--- quote ---
M-1014 Joint Service Combat Shotgun

The Joint Service Combat Shotgun is a a compact, lightweight, semi-automatic, 12 gauge
weapon configured with a standard magazine with a minimum capacity of six 2 3/4 inch
cartridges. The Combat Shotgun is capable of firing 12 gauge 3.0 inch magnum ammunition
and is interoperable with standard 2 3/4 inch ammunition without adjustment to the
operating system.

The Marine Corps was the lead agency in the test and evaluation of this shotgun. It is
designed to replace the many different shotguns used in all of the military service. The
shotgun was officially accepted for production in 2001. The weapon is primarily used by
security forces and by special operations forces. 
http://usmilitary.about.com/od/armyweapons/l/aainfantry2.htm
```

- **References:** `<ux_sj.217681$1x.187017@fe05.news.easynews.com> <13rb6n4nmtu02a1@corp.supernews.com> <61mqgbF2097h9U1@mid.individual.net> <13rcmvrtcpb0aa@corp.supernews.com> <sv5er3dkj5dhqgeqm8g3jf5a7vhh51iipo@4ax.com> <12mer39r4oe9c1kfmj0lgoghrrlu5jc9j5@4ax.com> <2fugr3h78cf0hf670r7ihgd1cuhq3ca9v4@4ax.com> <jqchr3lc9u5dg4r94c3saabb9642m63h06@4ax.com>`

On Sun, 17 Feb 2008 18:04:21 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:

>On Sun, 17 Feb 2008 13:17:35 -0500, SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You were talking about Vietnam, not WW2 or Korea.  The US Army (at
least..don't know about the Marines) quit issuing the Thompson because
the M14 and M16's that came along had a faster fire rate and could
easily handle 30 round clips.  They were also much more accurate at
long distances on single fire.  The Thompson was only accurate in very
close quarters and tended to suffer from barrel melt with extensive
use (as did the early models of the M-16).  However, some grunts in
Vietnam preferred them over the M-16 because they were lighter,
smaller and less likely to jam up in the rain and/or mud.  Guys that
had them, and I knew several, got them through the black market which
was rampant in Vietnam.  Same way many guys got the sawed off
shotguns.  Most of the folks that got those weapons were in recon
platoons and were more subject to close in fighting than most other
grunt platoons.

Also, many Army guys preferred the 38 caliber pistol issued by the Air
Force over the 45 issued by the Army and would trade to get one.  Not
sure why.  May have just been the "cowboy" factor.  I loved my 45!

>--- quote ---
>M-1014 Joint Service Combat Shotgun
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Your original post was about a "sawed off" shotgun which is illegal
just about anywhere including the armed forces but they were available
on the black market in Vietnam.  The M1014 Combat Shotgun, which is
issued to Army and Marine soldiers for use in Iraq is not the same as
a sawed off shotgun.  In Vietnam, Army Rangers, Marine Recon and Navy
SEAL units found lots of good uses for 12 gauge pumps as well as a
devastating 27 pellet buckshot load for the acclaimed M79 Thumper
grenade launcher.  Neither the M79 nor the pump gauge shotguns used by
those units could be classified as a "sawed off" shotgun.

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"Have I learned from my mistakes?  Yes, I believe
I could repeat them all exactly the same."
--  Kurt Vonnegut
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Steve

On Mon, 18 Feb 2008 11:22:06 -0500, SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:

>On Sun, 17 Feb 2008 18:04:21 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:
>
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>smaller and less likely to jam up in the rain and/or mud. 

Yes, the US military lost interest in high-firepower low-accuracy weapons for regular
infantry use. Rifles and carbines have much longer range of accurate fire. But submachine
guns were still preferred for Special Ops (recon), defensive use (tanks, engineers),
hostage rescue and shotgun for door breeching. 

Recon scouts need a weapon for defense, not offense, Their mission is getting information,
not killing people. They only use a weapon when detected and confronted by the enemy. In
that situation they throw out a burst of firepower to frighten the enemy into taking cover
long enough to make their escape. 

In urban fighting, such as hostage rescue, you don't want the penetrating power of a rifle
round lest you accidentally shoot someone on the other side of a wall.

>Guys that
>had them, and I knew several, got them through the black market which
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>grunt platoons.

Marine Corps Force Recon were issued M3 submachine guns during the Vietnam era. Now
they're issued the similar MP5. 

>Also, many Army guys preferred the 38 caliber pistol issued by the Air
>Force over the 45 issued by the Army and would trade to get one.  Not
>sure why.  May have just been the "cowboy" factor.  I loved my 45!

You must have been an officer. Grunts didn't carry handguns for the same reason:  no good
beyond 50 yards. 

Seems to me the cowboy factor would favor a Dirty Harry .44 Magnum or a Desert Eagle. My
personal favorite was a Wilkinson Linda with a shrouded (air cooled look) barrel. A
silenced MAC-10 is pretty scary too.
```

On Mon, 18 Feb 2008 20:31:06 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:

>On Mon, 18 Feb 2008 11:22:06 -0500, SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:

You're grasping at straws here.  The M3 was NOTHING like a Thompson.
Didn't look like it, didn't shoot like, didn't act like it.  The only
thing they had in common was that they both shot .45 caliber ammo.  In
fact, the M3 in 1942 began replacing the Thompson's that used to be
issued by the Army and Marines.  The M3 could easily convert to a 9 x
19 Parabellum pistol cartridge.  The M3 was nicknamed, "The Grease
Gun, because that's what it looked like.

The M5 is a 9mm weapon, not a .45 caliber weapon.  It is issued
primarily to Navy personnel but is favored by the groups you noted.

>>Also, many Army guys preferred the 38 caliber pistol issued by the Air
>>Force over the 45 issued by the Army and would trade to get one.  Not
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

And you must have never been a grunt in Vietnam.  I was and not an
officer.  Handguns were issued to artillary and some mortar units.  I
was in a 4.2 mortar platoon and we could get handguns if we wanted
them.  

>Seems to me the cowboy factor would favor a Dirty Harry .44 Magnum or a Desert Eagle. My
>personal favorite was a Wilkinson Linda with a shrouded (air cooled look) barrel. A
>silenced MAC-10 is pretty scary too. 
>

On Thu, 21 Feb 2008 14:20:32 -0500, SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:

>On Mon, 18 Feb 2008 20:31:06 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:
>
…[64 more quoted lines elided]…
>thing they had in common was that they both shot .45 caliber ammo. 

Same ammo and magazine capacity, similar weight, length and rate of fire (after the
Thompson was slowed down for the Navy). Both blowback operated. Both had non-adjustable
sights. Same tendency to 'walk upward'.  Both were used by recon and other special
operations units. 

"By the time of the Korean War, the Thompson had been withdrawn from service as a
standard-issue submachine gun with U.S. forces. It was replaced by the M3/M3A1 submachine
gun, and the M1/M2 carbine."
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thompson_submachine_gun

Most people think of the Thompson as an offensive weapon, the M3 as defensive. Wrong, both
are defensive. 

>In fact, the M3 in 1942 began replacing the Thompson's that used to be
>issued by the Army and Marines.  The M3 could easily convert to a 9 x
>19 Parabellum pistol cartridge. 

Only if you had access to the barrel, bolt, magazines and ammo. We didn't. 

"Close to 600,000 weapons were produced by the end of World War II, including approx.
25,000 models chambered for the 9x19mm Parabellum cartridge (which was achieved through
swapping out the barrel, bolt and applying an adapter for use with the British 9 mm Sten
submachine gun magazines), which were delivered to the OSS in 1944."

>The MP5 is a 9mm weapon, not a .45 caliber weapon.  It is issued
>primarily to Navy personnel but is favored by the groups you noted.

Same firepower. 

.45    230gr @ 850 ft/sec = 500 joules
9mm  123gr @ 1100 ft/sec = 494 joules

>>>Also, many Army guys preferred the 38 caliber pistol issued by the Air
>>>Force over the 45 issued by the Army and would trade to get one.  Not
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>And you must have never been a grunt in Vietnam. 

You're right.  I was in Marine recon elsewhere in SE Asia and in Cuba.

> I was and not an
>officer.  Handguns were issued to artillary and some mortar units.  I
>was in a 4.2 mortar platoon and we could get handguns if we wanted
>them.  

Marine artillery had infantry rifles, no handguns.
```

In article <sv5er3dkj5dhqgeqm8g3jf5a7vhh51iipo@4ax.com>,
SkippyPB  <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:

[snip]

>I hope D.C. wins.  Lets be clear what the Second Amendment says. The
>Second Amendment of the United States Constitution�s Bill of Rights
>declares a well-regulated militia as "being necessary to the security
>of a free State" and prohibits infringement of "the right of the
>people to keep and bear arms."

I was taught that when it comes to matters of scholarship - and 
Constitutional Law may be thought of as a kind of scholarship - that it is 
best to have a copy of the original text close by.  From 
<http://www.law.cornell.edu/constitution/constitution.billofrights.html#amendmentii>

--begin quoted text:

A well regulated militia, being necessary to the security of a free state, 
the right of the people to keep and bear arms, shall not be infringed.

--end quoted text

[snip]

>Another major point of contention is whether it protects against
>infringement of an individual right to personal firearms or a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>based on their needs while serving in a militia.

I find it... odd that this question of collective versus individual 
interpretation is raised regarding Amendment II but has never, to the best 
of my knowledge, been raised regarding rather similar language in 
Amendment I.  From 
<http://www.law.cornell.edu/constitution/constitution.billofrights.html#amendmenti>

--begin quoted text:

Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion, or 
prohibiting the free exercise thereof; or abridging the freedom of speech, 
or of the press; or the right of the people peaceably to assemble, and to 
petition the government for a redress of grievances.

--end quoted text

The 'rather similar language' I refer to is '... the right of the people 
peaceably to assemble, and to petition the government for the redress of 
grievances.'

In short... the last clause of Amendment I to the Constitution of the 
United States of America refers to 'the people'.  The next-to-last clause 
of Amendment II refers to 'the people'.  How the intervening twenty-nine 
words, three commas and one period would render the earlier reference as 
one towards individuals and the latter reference as one towards a 
collective would seem to require some legal interpretation worth 
examining.

DD
```

was legal at the time to own a naval vessel capable of taking out
other warships and that Congress had the right prior to the second
amendment to authorize the use of this vessel through letters of
marque and reprisal.  Since the Supreme Court has ruled in other cases
(primarily business and civil rights) that the 14th amendment extends
the prohibitions of the bill of rights to state actions, it is weak to
say that it wouldn't apply here.

…[37 more quoted lines elided]…

## Thread: Question regarding Line Sequential files on MF Cobol running under HP-UX — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Question regarding Line Sequential files on MF Cobol running under HP-UX

- **From:** The Walrus <I_Am_The_Walrus@invalid.email.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-15T02:45:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<47b4fcde$0$4047$9a566e8b@news.aliant.net>`

```
I have a very odd scenario that I have been asked to investigate in my 
job.  I am a technical support person and we are responsible for the 
installation and configuration of MF Cobol under HP-UX.  There is a 
program which is running in our production environment which opens a flat 
text file as LINE SEQUENTIAL, but it is really a SEQUENTIAL file.  That's 
not the issue here because the program doesn't do anything with the 
status of the open (I didn't write the code). The program redefines the 
main record and attempts to validate a part of it against a literal of 
"HDR".  The compiled code runs fine in production and under a "test" 
environment.  However, when I run the same program under any other 
environment it is unable to validate the literal as what it is retrieving 
fromt the file is a 0.  Apart from the COBSW variable, is there anything 
else I can check?  I'd like to tell our programmers that they need to 
change their code, and someday, I will.  But it's not having problems in 
our production environment.
comp.lang.cobol
I've spent 4 days on this problem and have come to the conclusion that it 
has something to do with the way that the "LINE SEQUENTIAL" file is being 
read, but all of my research has not revealed anything.

I am not able to post from work, but I can review responses and I will 
reply when I have usenet nntp access again.

Thanks for anyone who can help me with this.  Again, I didn't write the 
program and I know it's not good coding technique to open a file without 
checking the status of it, but it's not my code.
```

#### ↳ Re: Question regarding Line Sequential files on MF Cobol running under HP-UX

- **From:** tim <TimJ@internet.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-15T06:06:46
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13raavm97bnsu3d@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<47b4fcde$0$4047$9a566e8b@news.aliant.net>`

```
On Fri, 15 Feb 2008 02:45:50 +0000, The Walrus wrote:

> ... The program redefines the 
> main record and attempts to validate a part of it against a literal of 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> from the file is a 0.

I don't think you are allowed to access the record field if you have not
read a record into it. I have seen this produce memory faults, random data
etc.

This is like the two legged dog - it's not surprising that it walks badly,
it's surprising it walks at all. With your program, it could crash any
time.

I kill-filed the off-topic discussions. This is a very quiet group now ;-)

Tim
```

#### ↳ Re: Question regarding Line Sequential files on MF Cobol running under HP-UX

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-02-15T01:09:10-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hcar3h9aq6moqutrgs2rfjlv8im70rj20@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<47b4fcde$0$4047$9a566e8b@news.aliant.net>`

```
On 15 Feb 2008 02:45:50 GMT, The Walrus <I_Am_The_Walrus@invalid.email.com> wrote:

>I have a very odd scenario that I have been asked to investigate in my 
>job.  I am a technical support person and we are responsible for the 
>installation and configuration of MF Cobol under HP-UX.  There is a 
>program which is running in our production environment which opens a flat 
>text file as LINE SEQUENTIAL, but it is really a SEQUENTIAL file.  

The only difference is that line sequential has a newline at the end of each record, a
record sequential file (the default) does not, records are fixed length. When you edit the
file with vi , if you see more than one line, it's line sequential.

If it is only reading the first record, it can do so with EITHER file organization. That's
not the problem.

>That's 
>not the issue here because the program doesn't do anything with the 
>status of the open (I didn't write the code). 

Does the select say FILE STATUS? If not, the program will abort on open error or other
error. 

>The program redefines the 
>main record and attempts to validate a part of it against a literal of 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>from the file is a 0.  

Sounds like it's not finding the file at open time. Exactly what does the select say after
ASSIGN TO?  If it is using an environment variable, what value is in that variable?

>Apart from the COBSW variable, is there anything 
>else I can check?

Check the filke pointed to by $COBCONFIG. That file contains runtime options. 
It is possible that prod and test cobconfig are setting an environment variable the
program is using in ASSIGN TO.  It is also possible they are sourcing in an environment
script that sets an environment value the program is using in ASSIGN TO.

> I'd like to tell our programmers that they need to 
>change their code, and someday, I will.  But it's not having problems in 
>our production environment.

If they specify FILE STATUS, they should check it. The alternative is remove it and let
the program abort.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Question regarding Line Sequential files on MF Cobol running under HP-UX

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-02-15T07:28:42-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ng4br3tq4751f2v8k6qrtioqvlo3rnq10t@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<47b4fcde$0$4047$9a566e8b@news.aliant.net> <8hcar3h9aq6moqutrgs2rfjlv8im70rj20@4ax.com>`

```
>On 15 Feb 2008 02:45:50 GMT, The Walrus <I_Am_The_Walrus@invalid.email.com> wrote:

>>Apart from the COBSW variable, is there anything  else I can check?

Maybe the program doesn't have read permission on the file or any directory between the
file and root. Log on as the same user and try to edit the file.
```

#### ↳ Re: Question regarding Line Sequential files on MF Cobol running under HP-UX

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-02-15T07:27:54-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13rb4qrk7epaf2e@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<47b4fcde$0$4047$9a566e8b@news.aliant.net>`

```
The Walrus wrote:
> I have a very odd scenario that I have been asked to investigate in my
> job.  I am a technical support person and we are responsible for the
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> file is being read, but all of my research has not revealed anything.

I'm confused. The program works in a production environment and in a test 
environment, but doesn't work in your environment. What is "your" 
enviornment if it isn't "test" or "production?" Under water?

Further, how are you opening this file? Input?

If you open the file as input and the file doesn't exist then you inspect 
the first record, you'll find garbage or initialization values - not 
anything from a file.
```
